Configuring binary inputs (LPDI)
Binary inputs are available for control purposes. For example, the binary inputs
can provide the position of the circuit breaker block/enable/disable functions,
trigger a programmable logic, and indicate a function or object status.
The number of available inputs depends on the number of ordered module
options. There are eight available inputs on each MIO module.
The binary inputs require an external control voltage (AC or DC) and are activated
after the voltage exceeds the pick-up threshold. Deactivation follows when the
voltage drops below the drop-off threshold limit.
To view the status of the binary inputs:
•

From the HMI, expand

> General > Device config > Slot # > BI status.

The status of binary inputs can also be read by the SCADA system via different
protocols.

P7/EN M/02-004A

175

Protection and control device

Engineering

Configuring binary inputs with PowerLogic Engineering Suite
The binary inputs are set with PowerLogic Engineering Suite under Device
configuration > Binary inputs.
When the BI common setting is ON, the auxiliary supply range of all standard
binary inputs on the module will be set as the same as BI1/BI2. Special binary
inputs such as BI7/BI8 in speed mode and TCS binary inputs always have
independent settings.
The settable items include:
•

Auxiliary supply range (Not used/24V DC/48V DC/110V DC/220V DC/220V
AC/TCS)
NOTE: The nominal voltage pickup for TCS is 19 V with no inrush and a
constant current of 1 mA.

•

Pick-up ratio
The available range is from 40% to 80% based on the input voltage.

•

Drop-off ratio
The available range is from 50% to 90% based on the pick-up voltage.

•

Filter time

Applying a time over 10 ms makes the input operation immune to AC induction on
long DC circuits. If a faster response is required, external burden should be added
to the circuit.
Timing diagram of a binary input during pick-up, page 176 and Timing diagram of
a binary input during a drop-off, page 176 below illustrate the timing diagrams of a
binary input during pick-up and drop-off:
Figure 74 - Timing diagram of a binary input during pick-up

P7/EN M/02-004A

Engineering

Protection and control device

Configuring control inputs
The control inputs function as software switches that can be set or reset either
locally or remotely. These inputs can be used to trigger any function that they are
connected to via the fast scheme logic (FSL) or matrix.
In PowerLogic P7, there are 64 control inputs in total.
The mode of the control inputs can be configured with PowerLogic Engineering
Suite under Device control > Control inputs. The control inputs can be
configured as either Latched or Pulse mode:
•

A latched control input will remain in the set state until a reset command is
given.

•

A pulsed control input will remain energized for the configurable pulse
duration after the set command is given and will then reset automatically (i.e.
no reset command required).

Configuring function keys
PowerLogic P7 provides 12 function keys for programming any operator control
functionality via fast scheme logic or matrix. These function keys can be used to
trigger any function that they are connected to as part of the fast scheme logic or
matrix.
To select the function keys with front panel:
1. Expand

> Control > Function keys.

2. From the Function keys page, select the desired Function key.
NOTE: In case of two keys pressed at the same time, only one key works
and the other is time out.
Two control models are available for each function key:
•

Direct control model - The function key with direct control model has the direct
control icon showing the finger with clock.
NOTE: For the function key with direct control model, the key should be
pressed and held on the key for at least 500 milliseconds so as to prevent
the inadvertent key presses.

•

SBO control model - The function key with SBO control model has the twostep icon showing the finger with touch.

The function key has three kinds of mode:
•

Pulse mode - In this mode, the function key output remains high as long as
the key is pressed, and then be reset automatically after the key press is
released.
NOTE: The pulse can be detected by the receiving application function by
the pulse duration. A minimum pulse duration is 1 s.

•

Toggle mode - In this mode, a single keypress will set or latch the function key
output as high or low in fast scheme logic and the signal will be mapped via
the matrix on PowerLogic P7 as well. This feature can be used to enable or
disable the relay functions.

•

Disable mode - In this mode, the function key is disabled.
NOTE: Mode setting is used to allow the key to be disabled.

P7/EN M/02-004A

181

Protection and control device

Engineering

Matrix
Description
PowerLogic P7 protection and control device has a flexible structure that supports
a virtually infinite number of configurations. To fulfill the protection scheme’s
needs, different parts of this structure can be linked (i.e. physical and network I/
Os, protection functions, custom logic equations, front panel I/Os).
Each part can emit and receive signals. Those signals from one function can be
mapped to another using routing matrices.
PowerLogic P7 has several matrices that are used for linking the hardware and
software elements together to create a protection chain:
•

BO matrix
It is used to link various signals to the contact relays. See Configuring binary
outputs (LPDO), page 173.

•

LED matrix
It is used to control virtual LEDs. See Configuring virtual LEDs (LEDIHMI),
page 165.

•

Alarm matrix
It is used to link various signals to activate alarms. See Configuring alarms,
page 173.

•

CT/VT matrix
It is used to link hardware channels in CT/VT board with virtual channels in
CT/VT group. CT/VT matrix is for current and voltage mapping. See CT/VT
matrix, page 463.

•

Protection matrix
See Protection matrix, page 193.

•

Protection trip matrix
It is used to link the combination of all protection functions’ start outputs and
operate outputs to a general start and a general operate. See Trip conditioner
matrix, page 561.

•

Bay dead matrix
It is used to monitor all CB positions in the bay. See Bay dead matrix, page
586.

•

Interlocking matrix
It is used to inhibit the CB and ground switch control. See Interlocking matrix,
page 505.

•

Disturbance record matrix
It is used to trigger the disturbance record. See Disturbance record triggering
matrix, page 554.

Matrix processing for test bit
Table 39 - Matrix process for test bit
Matrix process for test bit

Input

182

Receiving function
ON

Test/Test- Blocked

Valid, Test=1

Invalid

Valid

Valid, Test=0

Valid

Valid

Invalid, Test=0/1

Invalid

Invalid

P7/EN M/02-004A

Engineering

Protection and control device

NOTE: As the matrix is not within an LN or LD, the Beh should be inherited
from the LN that owns the output from the matrix. If the function is On, then the
gate should consider the input signals with the test flag set as invalid. If the
function is Test or Test-blocked, then the test bit of the input data can be
ignored.

Configuring signal routing
To configure signal routing in PowerLogic Engineering Suite.
1. Expand a function and select the related mapping you want to configure
within the function configuration.
The matrix is displayed in the Setting view.
NOTE: The available sources are arranged in rows and the available
destinations are arranged in columns.
2. Click IEC 61850 or Standard to change the mapping to IEC 61850 view or
Standard view.
NOTE: The IEC 61850 view indicates the IEC 61850 name and the data
type for the sources. At the Logical Node (LN) level, data type indicates
the type of LN.
3. Select the Routed signals only if you want to list the routed signals only in
the mapping.
4. Select the Expand/collapse top level if you want to expand or collapse
signal top level. This allows the detailed view of the data attribute mapping to
be displayed for complex data objects.
5. Select a signal and click on the cell in white to link the signal to the
appropriate output from the matrix. This displays a tick symbol (√) in the cell.
NOTE: Inputs and outputs can be expanded by selecting the arrows next
to the source and destination. This allows linking at the main signal (data
object) or sub signal (data attribute) level or selection of specific states for
enumerated values.
6. Optionally, click the cell again to deselect the signal. This removes the tick
symbol.
7. Optionally, right-click the cell with the tick and select Invert to invert the signal
from the matrix.
8. Optionally, right-click the output destination and select Invert to invert the
matrix outputs which feed into function inputs. This displays a line on the top
of the outputs.

Mimic
Mimic is an advanced visualization and simulation function for IED applications. It
provides real-time graphical representation and monitoring capabilities for
electrical distribution networks, and improves system reliability and operational
efficiency.
The mimic can only be created and configured with PowerLogic Engineering
Suite. Creating multiple mimic sheets is supported.
NOTE: To navigate between multiple mimic sheets (if configured) on the HMI
homepage, perform a horizontal swipe gesture (left or right) on the active
mimic display.
Two types of symbols are available for mimic sheets:

◦

◦

Shapes: Ellipse, rectangle and static line

SLD symbols: Elements used for SLD diagrams. They are organized into
libraries such as busbars, switchgear, plant and sensors. Symbols include
ANSI, IEC and electrical drawing symbols.
NOTE: Some widgets and SLD symbols can be mapped to signals for a
dynamic display, while others are static.

Blocking remote configuration
PowerLogic P7 can be configured locally or remotely. Remote configuration can
be blocked to help prevent unintended changes. Local configuration cannot be
blocked.
Local configuration
Setting changes made through the following interfaces are considered local
configuration:
•

The HMI

•

PowerLogic Engineering Suite connected through the mini-USB or DHMI
ports

Remote configuration
Setting changes made through the following interfaces are considered remote
configuration:
•

PowerLogic Engineering Suite connected through Port 1/2/3

•

Communication clients (IEC 61850/Modbus/DNP3/IEC 60870-5-103)
connected through Port 1/2/3

Remote configuration can be blocked remotely or locally, but can only be
unblocked locally.

Block remote configuration
To block remote configuration:
•

From the HMI or PowerLogic Engineering Suite, set Remote PLES setting
block/Comms setting block to On under General > System info.

•

From a remote communication client, change the BlkRemHttps/
BlkRemComm parameter values and write to the device.

To unblock remote configuration, set Remote PLES setting block/Comms
setting block to Off on the HMI or locally connected PowerLogic Engineering
Suite.

Fast scheme logic (FSL)
PowerLogic P7 supports user-defined programmable logic for Boolean and
enumerated signals via the fast scheme logic (FSL). FSL provides the flexibility to
define custom logic functions that extend beyond the device's predefined
capabilities.

FSL fundamental
The FSL configuration is performed in PowerLogic Engineering Suite under
Device control > Fast scheme logic.
Up to 64 FSL pages can be created per device. Each page includes named inputs
and outputs that can be reused across the configuration. Logic input and output
status can be viewed on the HMI under Control > Fast scheme logic > Page#.

Logic gate functions
The following table provides the total number of available gates for each logic gate
type:
Table 41 - Available logic functions
Logic gate type

Number of gates reserved

OR

1000 (total number for OR, NOT, AND and XOR)

NOT
AND
XOR

186

TIMER

256

RS

128

Fast TIMER

32

P7/EN M/02-004A

Engineering

Protection and control device

Logic timer types and modes
Logic timers allow configurable time characteristics for Boolean outputs. Two timer
types are available:
•

TIMER
Provides standard timing behavior for logic operations. This timer is
applicable for precise timing in protection or control logic.

•

FAST TIMER
Provides high-speed timing behavior for logic operations. This timer is
applicable for very short delays in fast protection schemes or interlocking
logic.

Each timer supports the following modes:
•

Pickup
When the input changes from 0 to 1, the timer starts. After the configured
delay has elapsed, the output changes to 1.

•

Dropoff
When the input changes from 1 to 0, the timer starts. After the configured
delay has elapsed, the output changes to 0.

•

Pickup + dropoff
When the input changes from 0 to 1, the timer starts. After the configured
delay has elapsed, the output changes to 1.
When the input changes from 1 to 0, the timer starts. After the configured
delay has elapsed, the output changes to 0.

•

PULSE
When the input changes from 0 to 1, the output immediately changes to 1 and
the timer starts. The output remains at 1 for the duration of the configured
delay. When the delay has elapsed, the output changes to 0, regardless of
the input state.

•

DWELL
When the input changes from 0 to 1, the output immediately changes to 1 and
the timer starts. The output remains at 1 for the duration of the configured
delay. When the delay has elapsed:
◦

If the input remains at 1, the output stays at 1.

◦

If the input is 0, the output changes to 0.

Logic gate behavior
Predefined logic behavior is applied to each gate type. For example:
•

OR outputs true if any valid input is true.

•

AND outputs true only if all valid inputs are true.

•

XOR outputs true if inputs differ.

•

NOT inverts the input.

•

RS/SR gates follow reset/set dominant logic.

The following tables show predefined logic gate outputs based on binary inputs A
and B, and their quality status.
NOTE: Quality Questionable is treated as Valid regardless of its detailed
quality.

NOTE: The inputs of the logic gates should be connected. Care must be taken
when using logic outputs as logic inputs. Toggling feedback loops must be
avoided. If a bay is placed into test mode without placing the device in test
mode, the input will be seen as invalid which may cause the output to become
invalid. If this signal is fed back, it may create a situation where the logic is
permanently invalid even after disabling test mode.

P7/EN M/02-004A

Protection functions

Protection and control device

Protection functions
General features
Setting groups
Activation of a setting group can be made from the HMI, communication clients, or
the matrix.
For each protection stage, eight setting groups are available to allow different
settings to be applied for different operating conditions. Functions such as trip
conditioning, breaker fail, and pole dead are constant for all applications and
setting groups are not provided.

Activating a setting group with the matrix
When an activation signal from the matrix is active, the currently selected setting
group cannot be changed by the HMI or communication clients.
To enable activating a setting group from the matrix, use PowerLogic Engineering
Suite to map function keys, binary inputs, or other signals to ActSG1-8 under Bay
x configuration > Basic info. When any one of the eight ActSG signals is active,
that specific setting group is successfully activated.
NOTE: If multiple ActSG signals are active, the current selection is retained
until only one signal is active and that group is then activated.

Activating a setting group with the HMI or communication clients
The HMI or communication clients can activate a setting group only when no
ActSG signals from the matrix are active.
To activate a setting group from the HMI:
1. Expand

> Control > Bay 1 > Setting groups.

2. Tap Active group from the Setting groups tab.
3. Select the desired group number from the pop-up window to activate the
setting group.
To activate a setting group from communication clients, change the corresponding
parameter value and write to the device.

Blocking setting group switching
Setting group switching can be blocked by using the matrix.
Use PowerLogic Engineering Suite to map function keys, binary inputs or other
signals to BlkActSG under Bay x configuration > Basic info. When the
BlkActSG signal is active, the active setting group can not be switched by any
means.

Input and output signals

Enabling the protection functions
The different protection function can be enabled or disabled individually in
different setting groups.
•

With the HMI
1. Expand
function.

> Protection > Bay 1 to locate to the desired protection

2. Select the desired stage.
3. From the Settings tab, tap Group field at the top right corner.
4. Select the desired group from the Group drop-down.
5. Select On from the Function status drop-down to enable the protection
with the desired stage.
•

With PowerLogic Engineering Suite
1. Expand Bay 1 protection to locate to the desired protection function.
2. Select On from the Function status drop-down to enable the protection in
the specific setting group.

P7/EN M/02-004A

Protection functions

Protection and control device

Protection matrix
Functions like Selective Overcurrent Logic must interact with the protection
functions to adapt the timing and threshold constraints. It must be determined
which function should be linked with the dynamic input (Dyn).
Functions like Inrush Detection (ID) must interact with the protection functions to
dynamically block the related protection stage(s). For example, when the inhibit
signal (Inh) is linked with ID function, the protection will be blocked. From the
matrix of a specific protection, link the output signal of the ID function to the inhibit
signal of the protection.
The binary inputs or GOOSE signals also can be linked to the dynamic input or
block signals of protection functions.
Under protection matrix, configure the input signals of Inhibit, Op block, Block and
Hold for each protection respectively according to actual requirements.

Operation mode of the bay
Each PowerLogic P7 offers one bay, which has a separate mode under the BayLD
(Logical Device of a bay). The bay mode can be changed with matrix inputs or on
the HMI under Control > Bay x > Bay mode > Mode. The bay mode represents
the mode of the entire bay and has four options:
•

On

•

Test

•

Test-blocked

•

Off

The bay mode affects all the functions under the bay. These functions are used to
ensure the normal operation of this bay, i.e., the protection functions and the
measurement functions.
NOTE:
•

If the bay is in Test or Test-blocked mode, sampled values published by
the device will include the test bit.

•

The bay mode is affected by the device mode. For more information, see
Normal mode , page 602.

Table 51 - Related input signals under the BayLDLLN0
Name

Short label

Infotip

ModTestIn

Test mode

Sets bay in test mode, if Block
mode is also active device will
go to Test-blocked mode

ModBlockIn

Block mode

Sets bay in Test-blocked mode
if Test mode is also active

Table 52 - Related output signals under the BayLDLLN0
Name

Short label

Infotip

Beh

Behavior

Behavior

Mod

Mode

Bay mode

Quality
The current application function considers the validity of each input signal and
manages the behavior accordingly if the input is invalid. The behavior is to block
the function or let the function operate if the signals are less critical.

P7/EN M/02-004A

193

Protection and control device

Protection functions

NOTE: When the quality is questionable, the application will execute the
normal logic, except for blocking the output.
It also considers the Test bit as follows:
•

When the receiving application function is in On state, any data received with
the test bit set to True is treated as invalid.

•

When the receiving application function is in Test state or Test-blocked state,
the test flag of the received data is not taken into consideration for the input
validity.
NOTE: Functions with physical outputs, for example, LPDO will block all
inputs when they have Test-blocked state.

When the application function is in Off state, the binary output will be reset to
default value, and the analog output will be reset to zero.
•

Quality will be set to invalid for each output signal in Off state.

Timer
There are up to four matrix inputs which have common behavior for all protection
functions in which they are used.
•

Inhibit input (Inh)

•

Timer block input (TmBlk)

•

Timer hold input (TmHld)

•

Operate block input (OpBlk)

Inh, when asserted, will result in the protection function being held in a reset
condition with internal timers and latches reset and outputs set to default values. It
will also result in a block output being asserted.
TmBlk, when asserted, will result in the timer function being reset to zero.
TmHld, when asserted, will result in the timer function being frozen at the value
that it had reached at the instant that the Hold input became asserted.
NOTE: TmBlk has priority over TmHld, if both these inputs are
simultaneously asserted the timer function will be reset to zero, see figure
below.
OpBlk, when asserted together with start output, will prevent the operate output
from being asserted, no matter whether the timer has elapsed or not.
Figure 76 - Behavior of timer block and timer hold
Operate
signal
Start (pick-up)
signal
TmBlk
TmHld

Operate level
Value of internal
time delay counter
P71169A

P7/EN M/02-004A

Protection functions

Protection and control device

Overshoot time
Overshoot time is the time the IED need to notice that a fault has been cleared
during the operate time. This characteristic is important when grading the operate
time settings between the devices.
Figure 77 - Definition for overshoot time

A
B
C
D

P7119JA

A

Fault time

B

Overshoot time (≤ 50 ms)

C

Operate time configuration, have to
be greater than the sum of fault time
and overshoot time.

D

Trip contacts

For example, when there is a heavy fault in an outgoing feeder, it will start both the
incoming and outgoing IEDs. However, the fault must be cleared by the outgoing
IED and the incoming IED must not trip. Although the operate delay setting of the
incoming IED is more than that of the outgoing IED, the incoming IED might still
trip if the operate time difference is not big enough. The difference must be more
than the overshoot time of the incoming IED plus the operate time of the outgoing
feeder circuit breaker.
Definition for overshoot time, page 195 shows an overcurrent fault seen by the
incoming feeder when the outgoing feeder clears the fault. If the operate delay
setting is slightly shorter or if the fault duration is slightly longer than in the figure,
an unselective trip might happen (the dashed 40 ms pulse in the figure). In
PowerLogic P7, the overshoot time is less than 50 ms.

Disengaging time and reset time
The disengaging time is the time between the moment when the fault conditions
disappear and the moment the tripping contact relay opens.
According to IEC 60255-151 standard, Disengaging time and adjustable reset
time, page 196 shows an example of disengaging time, that is, release delay
when PowerLogic P7 is clearing an overcurrent fault. When the trip contacts of
PowerLogic P7 are closed, the Circuit Breaker (CB) starts to open. After the CB
contacts are open, the fault current still flows through an arc between the opened
contacts. The current is finally cut off when the arc extinguishes at the next zero
crossing of the current. This is the start moment of the disengaging delay and the
trip start contacts are opened unless dwell or latching is configured. The precise
disengaging time depends on the fault size; after a significant fault, the
disengaging time is longer. The disengaging time also depends on the specific
P7/EN M/02-004A

195

Protection and control device

Protection functions

protection stage. The maximum disengaging time for each stage is specified
under the characteristics of every protection function.
Figure 78 - Disengaging time and adjustable reset time

Tr: Reset delay setting

P7119HB

The adjustable reset time is a definite or inverse time hold used mainly to detect
restriking faults (DT) or allow coordination with electromechanical devices
(Inverse). The adjustable reset time is inhibited if the element operates.

Physical contact operate time and reset time
Performance from the characteristic table of each protection in this chapter is
based on the function without the delay caused by the physical contact or GOOSE
transmission.
From hardware aspect, the relay contact operate time is 4 ms and the reset time is
5 ms.

Hysteresis and reset ratio
When comparing a measured value against a start value, some amount of
hysteresis is needed to avoid oscillation near equilibrium situation. With zero
hysteresis, any noise in the measured signal or any noise in the measurement
itself would cause unwanted oscillation between fault-on and fault-off situations.
Figure 79 - Example of behavior of an over-protection with hysteresis
A

C

Hysteresis

B

Start level

C

Reset level

D

Start

The reset ratio is the ratio between the reset level and the start level. To avoid any
chattering of the protection for low settings of thresholds, a reset ratio can be
claimed with a minimum value of hysteresis. For example: < 93% with a minimum
of hysteresis of 0.005 In.

CT requirements
NOTICE
IMPROPER EQUIPMENT OPERATION
Select the CT size according to the requirement from the electrical network.
Failure to follow these instructions can result in improper operation.
PowerLogic P7 standard current inputs can be connected to standard 1 A or
5 A CTs. The CBCT can only be connected to a 1 A secondary.
Sizing rules of line CTs can be specific for each function and depend on which
function is being considered:
•

Phase overcurrent (PHPTOC, ANSI 50/51)

•

Ground overcurrent (EFPTOC, ANSI 50N/51N)

•

Sensitive ground overcurrent (VSEFPTOC, ANSI 50SG/51SG)

•

Bias differential (PHPDIF, ANSI 87)

•

High impedance diff (HIZPDIF, ANSI 87/64G)

•

Transformer differential (TR2PDIF/TR3PDIF, ANSI 87T)

•

Busbar differential (BBPDIF, ANSI 87B)

•

Line differential (L2PDIF, ANSI 87L)

Calculating the saturation current in class P
A class P CT is characterized by:

P7/EN M/02-004A

•

I.prim.nom: Rated primary current (in A)

•

I.sec.nom: Rated secondary current (in A)

•

Accuracy class, expressed by a percentage, 5P or 10P, followed by the
Accuracy-Limit Factor (ALF), whose usual values are 5, 10, 15, 20, 30

197

Protection and control device

Protection functions

•

VACT: Rated burden, whose usual values are 2.5/5/7.5/10/15/30 VA

•

RCT: Resistance of the secondary winding (in Ω)

The installation is characterized by the burden resistance Rb at the CT secondary
(wiring + protection relay). If the CT load complies with the rated burden, i.e.
Rb.nom × Isec.nom2 = VACT, the saturation current is equal to Isat = ALF × Iprim.nom.
If the CT winding resistance RCT is known, it is possible to calculate the actual CT
ALF, which takes account of the actual CT load. The saturation current then
equals actual ALF × Iprim.nom, where:
Actual ALF = ALF ×

RCT× I sec.nom2+ VA CT
(RCT+ R b) × Isec.nom2

E71172A

Hence if the actual CT load is less than its nominal burden (Rb.act < Rb.nom), a
higher short-circuit current can be transmitted without saturation.
Example:
A CT with the following characteristics is given:
•

Transformation ratio: 100 A / 5 A

•

Accuracy class and accuracy limit factor: 5P20

•

Rated burden: 2.5 VA

•

Resistance of the secondary winding: 0.1 Ω

To get an ALF of at least 20, i.e. a saturation current of 20 x Iprim.nom = 2 kA, the
load resistance Rb of the CT must be less than:
Rb,max=

VACT
Isec.nom2

= 0.1 Ω
E71173A

This represents 12 m (39 ft) of wire with cross-section 2.5 mm² (AWG 14) for a
resistance per unit length of 8 mΩ/m (2.4 mΩ/ft) approximately.
For an installation with 50 m (164 ft) of wiring with section 2.5 mm² (AWG 14), the
actual resistance is Rb = 0.4 Ω, and hence actual ALF is:
Actual ALF = ALF×

RCT× Isec.nom2+ VACT
(RCT+ R b) × Isec.nom2

=

0.1 × 25 + 2.5
=8
(0.1 + 0.4) × 25
E71174A

Therefore, the saturation current for this CT installation is:
Isat = 8 x Iprim.nom = 800 A.
NOTE: The actual CT burden Rb connected on its secondary side is given as
follows:
•

For phase-to-ground faults: Rb = 2 RW + Rrel

•

For phase-to-phase faults: Rb = RW + Rrel

Where the wire lead burden is calculated as:
l
RW= ρ ×
A

E71159A

with ρ = specific conductor resistance, for example, for copper 0.021 Ω mm²/m
at 75°C
l = wire length in [m]
A = wire cross section in [mm²]
The impedance of PowerLogic P7 current inputs (Rrel = 4 mΩ) is often
negligible compared to the wiring resistance.

Determining the knee point voltage of P class CT
The suitability of an IEC standard protection class CT can be checked against the
kneepoint voltage requirements specified previously.
For example, if the available CTs have a 15 VA 5P10 designation, then an
estimated kneepoint voltage can be obtained as follows:

Vk = 0.85 ´

(VA ´ ALF )
+ ALF ´ I n ´ RCT
In

E71277A

Where:
Vk = Required kneepoint voltage
ALF = Accuracy limit factor
In = Current transformer secondary rated current (A)
RCT = Resistance of current transformer secondary winding (Ω)
If RCT is not available, then the second term in the above equation can be ignored.
0.85: Typical maximum difference between the knee point voltage and the
secondary voltage at rated burden for a 5P class CT at ALF.

Worked example
•

CT ratio: 400/5A

•

IEC protection class: 5P10

•

Rated burden: 15 VA

•

CT secondary winding resistance: RCT = 0.2 Ω

•

Rated secondary current: In = 5 A

•

Accuracy limit factor: ALF = 10

Vk =0.85 × (15 × 10)/5 + 10 × 5 × 0.2 = 35.5 V

Converting IEC CT data to ANSI/IEEE C class voltage rating
The ANSI/IEEE C class rating defines the voltage that a current transformer (CT)
must deliver 100 A (20 × 5 A rated secondary current) while maintaining less than

P7/EN M/02-004A

199

Protection and control device

Protection functions

10% error with 60° burden angle. Because IEC P class and PX class CTs differ in
how their performance is specified, the conversion to ANSI C class must be
performed using different methods.

P class CTs (5P/10P)
The maximum usable secondary voltage is determined directly from the CT’s
rated burden and accuracy-limit factor (ALF). This voltage corresponds to the CT
output at the rated accuracy-limit current. However, since ALF is defined with a
resistive burden a 5P CT must be used which gives 10% error with a 60° burden.
Refer to the following expression:

VC =

VA
VA
´ ALF ´ I n =
´ ALF
2
In
In

E71278A

Where:
Vc: ANSI C class equivalent voltage
VA: Rated burden (VA)
In: Rated secondary current (A)
ALF: Accuracy limit factor of the CT
This voltage is compared directly with ANSI C class ratings such as C100, C200,
and C400.

Worked example (P class CT)
The following example illustrates the conversion of a P class CT to an ANSI/IEEE
C class voltage rating.
•

IEC protection class: 5P10

•

Rated burden: 30 VA

•

Rated secondary current: In = 5 A

•

Accuracy limit factor: ALF = 20

Converting to an ANSI C class rating:
VC = 30/5 × 20 = 120 V
Available ANSI C class ratings are C50, C100, C200, C400 and C800. The above
CT can be used for an application requiring a C100 class CT.

PX class CTs
PX class CTs specify the knee-point voltage (Vk) and excitation characteristics
directly. For ANSI conversion, the CT must supply the required terminal voltage at
100 A.
Refer to the following expression:

VC = 1.1´ Vk - 100 ´ RCT

E71279A

Where:
Vc: ANSI C class equivalent voltage
Vk: Manufacturer-specified IEC knee-point voltage
RCT: CT secondary winding resistance (Ω)
1.1: Typical minimum difference between the knee point voltage and Vc.

200

P7/EN M/02-004A

Protection functions

Protection and control device

Worked example (PX class CT)
The following example illustrates the conversion of a PX class CT to an ANSI/
IEEE C class voltage rating.
•

CT nameplate: 1000:5 1PX400R2

Converting to an ANSI C class rating:
VC = 1.1 × 400 - 100 × 2 = 240 V
The exciting current at the knee point should be checked.
In the above example, at the knee voltage, 1 Amp will be exciting the core giving a
1% error relative to the 100 A load. This is well below 5%, indicating VC will be
above Vk as expected. The above CT can be used for an application requiring a
C200 class CT.

where accuracy is required, according to the rules described below for DT or IDMT
operation.

Biased differential protection
ALF > 0.875 × 1/(1 - KR) × Kssc, minimum 25
KR = remanent flux/saturation flux
Kssc = Ipsc / Ipr

P7/EN M/02-004A

•

•

Ipr: Rated primary current

201

Protection and control device

Protection functions

This formula is based on using the default slope settings (K1, K2 and Slope 2
pickup). The saturation time constant should be set above the system time
constant for stability with any CT type.
The worst-case remanence factor is usually assumed to be 0.75 for high
remanence CTs (5P, PX). A lower remanence determined in accordance with IEC
61869-2 can also be used.

High impedance differential protection
The current transformer requirements for phase faults with peak-to-peak
measurement mode are:
Vs = If × (RCT + 2RL)
Rs = Vs / Is1
VK ≥ 2 Vs
Where:
Rs = Value of stabilizing resistor (ohms)
Ιf = Maximum secondary through fault current level (amps)
VK = CT knee point voltage (volts)
ΙS1 = Current setting of differential element (secondary amps)
RCT = Resistance of current transformer secondary winding (ohms)
RL = Resistance of a single lead from relay to current transformer (ohms)
Vs = Stability voltage

Transformer differential protection
ALF > 0.875 × 1/(1 - KR) × Kssc, minimum 17.5
KR = remanence flux/saturation flux
Kssc = Ipsc / Ipr
•

Ipsc: Minimum of High set op value equivalent primary current and rated
primary short-circuit current

•

Ipr: Rated primary current

This formula is based on using the default slope settings (K1, K2 and Slope 2
pickup).
The worst-case remanence factor is usually assumed to be 0.75 for high
remanence CTs (5P, PX). A lower remanence determined in accordance with IEC
61869-2 can also be used.

Busbar differential protection
CT requirement for external faults
CTs must satisfy the following formula to help achieve proper operation of the
busbar differential protection function:

ALF > K c ´ PC

E71275A

Where,

Kc = Dimensioning factors
PC = Phase comparison value
Determine Kc as follows:
•

When X/R ratio < 31, Kc = 25

•

When 31 ≤ X/R ratio ≤ 63, Kc = 32

•

When X/R ratio > 63, Kc = 40

CBCTs
Unlike a line CT, the rated primary current for a CBCT may not be equal to the
load. This has been considered in the formula:
VK = 6 × N × In × (RCT + 2RL + Rr)
Where:
VK = Minimum CT kneepoint voltage for through fault stability
In = Relay rated current
N = Ground fault current/CBCT rated primary current
RCT = Resistance of CT secondary winding (Ω)
RL = Resistance of a single lead from relay to CT (Ω)
Rr = Resistance of any other protective relays sharing the CT (Ω)
NOTE: N should not be greater than 2. The CBCT ratio should be selected
accordingly. Requirements based on FFT measurement mode.

P7/EN M/02-004A

203

Protection and control device

Protection functions

Independent and dependent characteristics
Description
The relationship between operate delay and characteristic quantity can be
expressed by two types of characteristics:
•

Independent time characteristic (i.e. definite time delay)

•

Dependent time characteristic (i.e. inverse time delay)

Independent time characteristic
The operate time in Definite Time (DT) mode is defined regardless of the fault
value. It is activated immediately after the pickup value is reached.
For overcurrent functions, when the current exceeds the set threshold, the
independent time characteristic is fixed to the defined operate delay.
Figure 81 - Overcurrent definite time characteristic
t

T

Is

I
P71160A

Dependent time characteristic
The dependent time characteristic is the Inverse Definite Minimum Time (IDMT)
type of operation in accordance with standards IEC 60255-151, IEEE C-37.112
and BS 142.
The dependent time characteristic is available for several protection functions:
•

Overcurrent protection

•

Ground fault protection

•

Negative Sequence (unbalance) protection

•

Overvoltage protection

•

Undervoltage protection

Dependent operate delay
A large number of dependent time characteristics can be selected for each
overcurrent function. A minimum operate time can be defined for each overcurrent
function. If the calculated operate delay is less than the set threshold, the operate
signal is issued after the minimum operate time has elapsed.
The dependent time characteristics of overvoltage and undervoltage functions are
described in the protection functions.
NOTE: Once a ratio measured value/pickup value (G/Gs) greater than 40 is
reached, the tripping time is bounded on the lower end.

204

P7/EN M/02-004A

Protection functions

Protection and control device

207

Protection and control device

Protection functions

Phase overcurrent /Phase direction
(PHPTOC/PHRDIR, ANSI 50/51/ANSI 67)
Description
Phase overcurrent
The phase overcurrent function is applied to detect phase-to-phase short-circuit
currents (phase-to-ground in specific cases). Discrimination is achieved because
of the current threshold and operate delay of the different stages of the protection.
The measurement type can be subject to the phase overcurrent function: either
the fundamental value or the RMS value of the phase currents, or the fundamental
minus the zero sequence current.
Operation can be set to 1 of 3 or 2 of 3 mode. In 2 of 3 mode the element will only
operate when 2 elements operate and will typically not operate for ground faults.
The start signals are phase based, however, the timers will only start when the
number of operating elements exceed the operating mode. A Drop Out output
signal is provided to indicate the start has reset but the timers are being held by
the reset timer.
Additional functions such as Selective Overcurrent Logic (SOL) and variable
loading improve the behavior of the protection function in terms of stability or
sensitivity.

Dynamic setting element
The element provides dynamic settings which allow it to adapt to different system
conditions. Typical uses of dynamic settings can be one of the following:
•

Selective Overcurrent Logic (SOL)
This allows for close coordination with upstream and/or downstream
protection. When SOL is configured and linked to this function, the current
timer settings are replaced by the timer settings defined by the dynamic
settings. Other dynamic settings are normally not changed.

•

Variable loading
The curve pickup can be changed during certain operating conditions to lift
the pickup without affecting grading for the rest of the curve. This is typically
applied for transformer applications where a higher pickup may be needed
with one transformer out of service. In this case the status of the other
transformer is brought to the device. When it is in service, the start factor
multiplier is left at 1, but when it is out of service, it is set to 1.5.

Phase direction
The phase direction element (PHRDIR) is optionally linked to the phase
overcurrent function (PHPTOC). The Directional mode of a PHPTOC stage can be
selected as Forward, Reverse, or Non-directional. When in Non-directional mode,
the directional element is ignored and only phase overcurrent protection is
applied. When in Forward or Reverse mode, with a PHRDIR stage linked, the
current direction is determined by comparing voltage and current angles, see
Direction Determination, page 209.
NOTE: If set to Forward or Reverse mode, PHPTOC must use the same CT
as the PHRDIR that provides the Dir input signal. Otherwise, the PHRDIR will
not operate as intended.
NOTE: If no PHRDIR stage is linked to the PHPTOC stage, set Directional
mode to Non-directional. Setting it to Forward or Reverse triggers the Blocked
output, which indicates an invalid configuration.

208

P7/EN M/02-004A

Protection functions

Protection and control device

This function is applied to systems where phase-to-phase fault currents can flow
in both directions, such as parallel feeders, ring feeders, and interlocked
overcurrent busbar schemes. Depending on the application, the direction signal is
used to generate either a tripping or a blocking.

Direction Determination
The determination of the fault current direction considers the quadrature voltage
angle, the current angle, and the selected Relay Characteristic Angle (RCA)
angle.
Forward

-90° < (angle(I) - angle(V) - RCA) < 90°

Reverse

90° < (angle(I) - angle(V) - RCA) < 270°

NOTE: Voltages are memorized when all phases are above 70% nominal and
the memory voltage is available for 5s following any voltage loss to allow
correct operation for bolted 3 phase faults. Memory voltage is used whenever
the measured voltage is below the Minimum line voltage setting. Indication is
provided when the memory voltage is used and for operation after expiry of
the memory voltage. If all voltages are below the minimum line voltage, an
additional indication is provided.
The following diagram shows the direction determination mechanism for a phase
A fault.
Figure 82 - Direction determination for a phase A fault

Ground overcurrent /Ground direction
(EFPTOC/EFRDIR, ANSI 50N/51N/ANSI 67N)
Description
The ground overcurrent protection function is used on three wire systems to detect
when one of the phases has come into contact with ground. This can be due to
insulation failure in insulated cables or fallen wires/external contact in uninsulated
applications.
The protection function can be provided by the summation of three-phase currents
either numerically or physically.
•

When the CT connection type is 3ph+N or 2ph+N, the protection function will
use the measured value.

•

When the CT connection type is 3ph, the protection function will use the
derived value.

•

When the CT connection type is 2ph, the input of the protection function will
be invalid.

The ground overcurrent protection function can also be provided using a toroidal
CT around all phases or the transformer neutral. The protection function does not
need to consider the load so it can be set quite sensitively.

Dynamic setting element
The element provides dynamic settings which allow it to adapt to different system
conditions. Typical uses of dynamic settings is:

216

P7/EN M/02-004A

Protection functions

Protection and control device

•

Selective Overcurrent Logic (SOL)
This allows for close coordination with upstream and/or downstream
protection. When SOL is configured and linked to this function, the current
timer settings are replaced by the timer settings defined by the dynamic
settings. Other dynamic settings are normally not changed.

Ground direction
The ground direction protection function (EFRDIR) is optionally linked to the
ground overcurrent protection function (EFPTOC) which can then be selected as
Forward, Reverse, or Non-directional.
The EFRDIR determines the fault direction based on comparison of voltage and
current angles. When available neutral voltage is the preferred polarizing quantity
using -VN and IN, but requires a 5 limb VT or 3 single phase VTs. Two winding VTs
can be used but require negative sequence polarization using -V2 and I2 as the
polarizing quantities.
NOTE: If no EFRDIR stage is linked to the EFPTOC stage, set Directional
mode to Non-directional. Setting it to Forward or Reverse triggers the Blocked
output, which indicates an invalid configuration.
The EFRDIR is used when ground faults can flow in multiple directions:
•

Parallel feeders

•

Interlocked overcurrent busbar schemes

•

Ring feeder protection

These systems will have directional overcurrent, but the directional ground fault is
usually also applied for greater sensitivity. It applies to solid and low resistance
grounded systems where ground current flows from a system ground to the fault.
Several devices may see the fault simultaneously and graded operation is
required. Primary ground fault current exceeds 100 A and will normally be
measured using residual current either calculated from phase currents or using a
Holmgreen connection.

Direction determination
The determination of the fault current direction considers the polarizing voltage
angle, the polarizing current angle, and the selected Relay Characteristic Angle
(RCA) angle is shown in Direction calculation, page 217.
Table 66 - Direction calculation

P7/EN M/02-004A

Forward

-90° < (angle(I) - angle(V) - 180° - RCA) < 90°

Reverse

90° < (angle(I) - angle(V) - 180° - RCA) < 270°

Sensitive ground overcurrent
(VSEFPTOC, ANSI 50SG/51SG)
Description
The sensitive ground overcurrent protection function is applied to the system in
which a single phase ground fault is not detected by means of standard current
operated ground fault protection. A fully discriminative ground fault protection is
achieved by the VSEFPTOC that is used to detect the resultant imbalance in the
system charging currents that occurs under ground fault conditions, usually
achieved by direct measurement of ground current through a toroid on the ground
connection or around all the phase connections.
The current measurement can come from a summated input (physical or
numerical), standard 1 A/5 A CT input, or the 1 A CBCT (when fitted). When
summated inputs are used, CT errors may cause operation for settings below 1%.

222

•

When the CT connection type is 3ph+N or 2ph+N, the protection function will
use the measured value.

•

When the CT connection type is 3ph, the protection will use the derived value.

•

When the CT connection type is 2ph, the input of the protection will be invalid.

P7/EN M/02-004A

High speed instantaneous overcurrent (PHPIOC,
ANSI 50HS)
Description
The high speed instantaneous overcurrent function provides fast clearance of very
high overcurrent conditions.
The protection operates instantaneously when the calculated peak-to-peak
current in any phase exceeds the configured threshold, generating fast phasebased Str and Op signals when the fault current is high.
The peak-to-peak current is calculated for each phase using the following formula:
Peak to peak =

Max ( 48 samples in latest cycle ) - Min ( 48 samples in latest cycle )
2 2

E71267A

The calculated peak-to-peak current can be viewed on the HMI under >
Protection > Bay 1 > High speed instantaneous overcurrent > Status, if this
function is enabled.

Negative sequence overcurrent (NPSPTOC/NPSRDIR,
ANSI 67Q)
Description
During phase-to-phase faults and phase-to-ground faults, 57% and 33% of the
fault current will appear as NPS current respectively. These values will normally
exceed any NPS current present during load and can be used to help detect
faults.
NPS overcurrent can detect phase-to-phase faults at the end of long lines where
the fault current is below the overcurrent threshold. It can also detect earth faults
on the LV side of star-delta transformers which are below the HV overcurrent
threshold. Directional NPS overcurrent can be used in a similar manner to
directional overcurrent to allow faster operation for faults in one direction or within
a zone by linking signals between devices.
For rotating machines, NPS overcurrent provides protection against a temperature
rise caused by an unbalanced power supply, phase inversion, loss of phase, and
unbalanced phase currents.

P7/EN M/02-004A

227

Protection and control device

Protection functions

Dynamic setting element
The element provides dynamic settings which allow it to adapt to different system
conditions.
One typical use of dynamic settings is Selective Overcurrent Logic (SOL). This
allows for close coordination with upstream and/or downstream protection. When
SOL is configured and linked to this function, the current timer settings are
replaced by the timer settings defined by the dynamic settings.

Negative sequence direction
The negative sequence direction element (NPSRDIR) is optionally linked to the
negative sequence overcurrent function (NPSPTOC).
The Directional mode of a NPSPTOC stage can be selected as Forward, Reverse,
or Non-directional. When in Non-directional mode, the directional element is
ignored and only the NPSPTOC function is applied. When in Forward or Reverse
mode, and an NPSRDIR stage is linked, the determined fault direction will be used
by the NPSPTOC stage as a starting condition.
NOTE: The linked NPSPTOC and NPSRDIR stages must use the same CT
group as input.
NOTE: If no NPSRDIR stage is linked to the NPSPTOC stage, set Directional
mode to Non-directional. Setting it to Forward or Reverse triggers the Blocked
output, which indicates an invalid configuration.
The directional element can be useful for the following scenarios:
•

When negative phase sequence current may flow in either direction through a
relay location, such as parallel lines or ring main systems.

•

When the primary protection for transformers fails to operate and a backup
protection is needed. In this application, by comparing I2 directions on both
sides of the transformer, the device can distinguish between faults inside the
transformer and external faults on the connected network.

Direction determination
For the NPSRDIR element to determine the fault direction, V2, I2 and I2/I1 must
exceed the Minimum voltage, Minimum current and Minimum I2/I1 settings,
respectively. If any of the three conditions are not met, the fault direction is
indicated as unknown.
NOTE: The I2/I1 threshold is added to avoid operation due to CT imbalance at
high currents.
The fault current direction is determined based on the angles of V2, I2, and RCA
(Characteristic angle).
Table 83 - Direction calculation

228

Forward

-90° < (angle(I2) - angle(V2) - 180° - RCA) < 90°

Reverse

90° < (angle(I2) - angle(V2) - 180° - RCA) < 270°

Broken conductor (BCPTOC, ANSI 46BC)
Description
The broken conductor function helps detect broken (open circuit) phases in a
three-phase power system.
The function is based on the ratio of negative to positive phase sequence current
(I2/I1). The function starts and operates when the measured I2/I1 ratio exceeds
the set threshold. To avoid unintended operation with low load currents, the I1
current must be above the set minimum level. The broken conductor function
operates with definite time characteristic.
The measured I2/I1 ratio can be viewed from HMI under > General > Bay 1 >
3ph CT x > Measurements > Fundamentals where the CT corresponds to the
signal used by the broken conductor element.

P7/EN M/02-004A

233

Inrush detection (IDPHAR, ANSI 68ID)
Description
The Inrush Detection (ID) function detects high inrush current flows that occur
when transformers or machines are connected (transformer differential uses its
own inrush detection). The inrush current detection is determined by 2nd
harmonic/fundamental current ratio. Inrush which is non directional could be used
to block the following functions:

P7/EN M/02-004A

•

50/51: Phase overcurrent

•

50N/51N: Standard ground fault

•

50SG/51SG: Sensitive ground fault

•

46: Negative sequence overcurrent

•

32P/32Q: Overpower/Reactive overpower

235

Protection and control device

Protection functions

Since the element is a blocking element, it can normally operate for any signal
quality. When the CT signals are invalid, it also operates. For operation the
fundamental current is higher than a fixed threshold (equal to 0.05 pu) on a per
phase basis. On the other hand, when the fundamental input current value of each
phase is higher than setting value of maximum phase current for inrush operation,
all output signals of the inrush detection would reset. When the start output of one
phase is asserted, if the cross blocking status is Enabled, the start output of the
three phases are asserted at the same time. Whilst the element is normally used
to block other elements, by connecting the inrush start to their inhibit input, a timer
can also be set to operate an alarm or trip if the inrush is detected for an excessive
time. If tripping is required, the start and trip signals from Inrush detection should
be connected to the trip conditioning inputs. Elements to be blocked need to have
a minimum delay of 30 ms to ensure the blocking is always received before
operation.

Block diagram
Figure 95 - Block diagram of IDPHAR
IA

> CurblkVal

IB

> CurblkVal

IC

> CurblkVal

≥1
Blk

Inh.gen

≥1
>0.05

H2Rat.phsA

Protection and control device

Protection functions

Selective overcurrent logic (SOL) (SOLGAPC)
Description
The SOL function provides the ability to block or temporarily increase the time
delay setting of the upstream devices. For example, the following upstream
elements could be adapted by the SOL signal.
•

50/51: PHPTOC

•

50N/51N: EFPTOC

•

46: NPSPTOC

This function is the SOL order sender, and the SOL order receiver is the
connected upstream device overcurrent functions. The SOL signal (GOOSE or
wired) is connected to the dynamic or block input of selected functions in the input
matrix for that function. This connection makes the connected function a SOL
receiver.
The creation of SOL order is dependent on the following signal inputs and
settings:
•

Start signals from PHPTOC, EFPTOC or NPSPTOC.

•

Input signal from RBRF.

When a fault is cleared by the CB, the start signal of the protection is disengaging,
and the SOL order is also disengaging. If the disengaging time between both
devices (downstream and upstream) is not same (because the execution cycle is
not synchronized between devices, or the ratio fault current/threshold is not same,
which has an impact on the disengaging time, etc.), the SOL order is disengaged
before disengaging of the start signal of the upstream device. In this situation,
there could be a false trip of the upstream device, because the SOL order
disappears before disengaging of the start signal and a short timer could elapse.
An OFF-delay timing (e.g. 1 cycle-20 ms) is added after the OR of all start signals.
When the downstream CB fails to clear the fault, the SOL signal is reset by the
downstream device unblocking the upstream device. Therefore, a CBF signal from
RBRF function is used to reset the SOL signal.
For schemes with radial current flow only the forward inputs and outputs of the
SOL are used. For schemes with bi-directional current flow, the SOL elements
used need to be directional and the forward and reverse inputs and outputs of the
scheme are used.

238

P7/EN M/02-004A

Characteristic time
SOL time

< 10 ms (based on start input)

The performance in the table above is just for SOL function alone (SOL order
creation).
To allow time for the SOL operate order to initiate a change of setting, the
protection time setting should be bigger than Ttotal in the figure below. If the setting
is less than Ttotal, there is a risk that the upstream device operates before the SOL
signal is received and processed..
Figure 98 - Chronogram for SOL order between two devices

Fault

Start

Downstream device
SOL (Sender)

SOL (Receiver)

Upstream device
Change Setting (Receiver)

T1

T2

T3

Ttotal

T4

P7119GB

Legends:

P7/EN M/02-004A

•

T1 is the time interval between a fault occurs and PTOC start is issued in the
downstream device. This is the start time of the PTOC function which can
vary depending on whether the element is set directional.

•

T2 is the time interval between PTOC start issued and SOL order issued. In
general, T2 is less than 5 ms.

•

T3 is the time delay of SOL order transmission between the downstream
device and upstream device. For GOOSE transmission, this is the total
transfer time. For wired transmission, this is the output relay and input filter
delays.

•

T4 is the time interval between the SOL signal being processed by the device
and the function adapting to the SOL condition. This time is based on the
signal acquisition and the execution cycle of PTOC function and is typically 5
ms.

241

Protection and control device

Protection functions

•

Ttotal is the total time device between a fault occurs and the setting is
changed. Typical values depend upon the type of logic (block or delay) and
the communications used. Ttotal is used to determine the delay setting on the
upstream device. In a blocking application a safety margin is normally added,
a delay of 80 ms/200 ms typically being applied in non-directional/directional
applications. For delay applications, the upstream device usually has a time
delay characteristic similar to the downstream device. In this case, the delay
needs to consider what percentage of operation has occurred before the SOL
is received and to ensure that the downstream clearance does not cause
operation on the new characteristic considering that the element is already
partially operated.

Phase undercurrent (PHPTUC, ANSI 37)
Description
The phase undercurrent protection is used to detect under current conditions. This
protection detects the loss of load in motor applications but can also be applied to
other applications.
For low current self blocking, to make the difference between a normal operation
of the Circuit Breaker (CB) and an undercurrent condition, a zero value setting is
used to disable the function. The phase undercurrent protection is blocked when
the maximum of the phase currents drops below 0.015 pu to avoid the unwanted
tripping.

Starting logic
In the starting logic, to prevent the protection from maloperation when the CB is
reclosed with some load, the current must first go above the drop-off value before
asserting the start signal.
Figure 99 - Assert the start output signal

1.05 Is
Is

0.015 pu
I

Str
P7119PA

In the starting logic, to distinguish between the fault and the CB opening, when the
current drops below 0.015 pu in less than 50 ms from the time when current is
below the start threshold, the start signal will not be asserted.

Voltage dependent overcurrent (PHPVOC, ANSI 51V)
Description
Voltage Dependent Overcurrent (VDO) calculates different settings during every
protection execution in accordance with the measured voltage and the
characteristic of VDO. The characteristic diagrams are shown as below.

244

P7/EN M/02-004A

Protection functions

Protection and control device

Figure 102 - PHPVOC characteristic 1

I

Is

K*Is

VS2

VS1

V
P71180A

Figure 103 - PHPVOC characteristic 2

I

Is

K*Is

VS1

V
P71181A

VS1 and VS2 are two set points of VDO, and K is the multiplying factor. When VS2 <
VS1, the function follows the PHPVOC characteristic 1, page 245. When VS2 > VS1
, the function follows the PHPVOC characteristic 2, page 245. The FB compares
current magnitude with different threshold phase segregated. Take phase A for
example, three kinds of setting cases are required, which depends on the
comparation result between VS1 and VS2 and input voltage VAB.
•

Case 1: Current setting = IS,
(VAB > VS1 > VS2 or VAB > VS2 > VS1)

•

Case 2: Current setting = K × IS,
(VAB < VS2 and VAB < VS1)

•

Case 3: Current setting = K × IS + (1 – K) × IS × (VAB – VS2)/(VS1 – VS2),
(VS2 < VAB < VS1)

The red line shows the corresponding drop-off level which is 0.95 multiple current
setting for each of the cases above. Furthermore, the current setting IS of case 1 is
set as default, bad quality on voltage will force to case 1.

Sensitive directional earthfault (PSDE, ANSI 67SG/32N)
Description
The sensitive directional earthfault protection function (ANSI 67SG/32N) is applied
for ground fault detection on petersen coil, isolated and high impedance grounded
system. On these systems, the primary ground fault current is low. The grounded
fault is detected by standard current measurement, but non-faulted circuits may
have more current than faulted circuits. The PSDE is used to distinguish between
faulted and non-faulted circuits when there is a grounded fault occurs in system.
This function provides three operating modes,

248

•

IN mode

•

INcosφ mode

•

PN mode
P7/EN M/02-004A

Protection functions

Protection and control device

NOTE: IN sinφ and QN can be provided by setting the characteristic angle to
90°.
For IN mode, the element starts when all the following conditions are met:
•

VN > VN start value, and the VN Supervision time is expired

•

IN > IN start value

•

Fault occurs in directional characteristic

For INcosφ mode, the element starts when all the following conditions are met:
•

VN > VN start value, and the VN Supervision time is expired

•

|IN × cos(φN-Ang)| > IN start value

•

Fault occurs in directional characteristic

For PN mode, the element starts when all the following conditions are met:
•

VN > VN start value, and the VN Supervision time is expired

•

IN > IN start value

•

|VN × IN × cos (φN-Ang)| > PN start value

•

Fault occurs in directional characteristic

The protection calculates the active and reactive neutral powers as follows:
PN = IN × VN × cos (φN-Ang)
The φN is the angle of IN relative to -VN; IN and VN are inputs to the function.
This element can be set to either:
•

forward fault zone

•

reverse fault zone

The forward zone issues the signal or the trip command during a fault on the
downstream side of the protection. The reverse zone issues a signal during a fault
on the upstream side of the protection.
The operation area is defined by Sector angle and Characteristic angle from
settings.

P7/EN M/02-004A

•

Forward: -sector+Ang < φN < sector+Ang

•

Reverse: 180°-sector+Ang < φN < 180°+sector+Ang

249

Switch on to fault (PSOF)
Description
The switch on to fault (PSOF) is beneficial in situations where an operator closes a
circuit breaker onto a fault, such as inadvertently left line earths in the circuit.
For basic protection like the PHPTOC, the PSOF is often not necessary because
these basic protective measures can eventually clear the fault.
However, for applications using directionality, such as directional and distance
protection functions, the PSOF is normally applied when the Voltage Transformer
(VT) is situated on the line side. For a dead short situation, the voltage will be zero
and the fault direction cannot be determined. This will normally prevent the high
speed protection function operation. If the protection response is delayed, it will
result in a slower trip which may lead to a wider power outage.
The PSOF protection function addresses the following two distinct scenarios:
•

The PSOF activates specific protective elements when a fault is detected
upon the manual closing of a circuit breaker. The rapid response can help
prevent damage and minimize disruptions.

•

Trip on reclose (TOR) is triggered by a persistent fault during the autoreclosing sequence of the circuit breaker, it helps ensure a rapid response to
clear the fault.

Both the PSOF and the TOR are integral components of the Trip on close (TOC)
functionality, which is part of a comprehensive protection strategy for circuit
breakers.

Feature overview
•

254

Activation conditions: The system provides the capability to activate the
PSOF or TOR logic using an initiate input signal. This matrix mapped input
signal will activate the function with an activation delay time and a release
time.
◦

When the PSOF or TOR is activated by CB closing command signal, the
activation delay time should be set to 0 ms.

◦

When the PSOF is activated by bay dead conditions, the activation delay
time serves to distinguish between a standard open-close sequence
where grounding switch closing may not be possible, and the situations
where grounding switch closing may be present. It is normally around
200 s.

◦

When the TOR is activated by bay dead conditions, the activation delay
time must not exceed the minimum dead time required for auto-reclose as
both times start simultaneously and the TOR protection must be ready by
the time of CB closing on potentially persistent faults.

•

Response to operating conditions: If an operating condition, such as an input
signal or Current No Volts (CNV) is detected, when the element is activated,
the PSOF element will initiate operation, including the Definite Time (DT)
delay operation if required.

•

Matrix mapped operating conditions: The operating condition for the element
is based on a single input mapped to multiple application function blocks,
providing flexibility in the system integration and response.

P7/EN M/02-004A

Protection functions

Protection and control device

•

TOR: The TOR operates during the auto reclosing sequence with a reduced
activation time when activated by Bay Dead conditions. The basic logic of
TOR is consistent with PSOF. That means PSOF is also applicable in
scenarios where TOR is utilized.

Protection functions

Ground admittance (EFPADM, ANSI 21YN)
Description
The ground admittance protection function is applied for ground-fault detection on
high-resistance grounded, ungrounded or compensated power systems.
This function uses neutral current and voltage to calculate the admittance (YN).
Angle correction can be achieved via settings of 1ph CT group if needed.
YN = GN + jBN = IN / -VN = 3I0 / -3V0
Conductance (Gn) is the real part of YN while susceptance (Bn) is the imaginary
part of YN.
This function supports over-admittance, over-conductance, over-susceptance or
any combination of the three elements. When any of the selected elements meets
its operation criteria for the configured operate delay, the function trips.
NOTE:
This function starts working only when both the following two conditions are
met:
•

The neutral voltage is above its configured threshold for the time defined
in the VN supervision time setting.

•

The neutral current is above its configured threshold.

Operation of over-admittance
The over-admittance element is non-directional and trips when the magnitude of
admittance exceeds the YN start value for the operate delay.
Figure 110 - Operate zone of over-admittance
jBN
YN

Ys

GN

A
YN > Ys
P7122BA

A

Operate zone

Operation of over-conductance
The over-conductance element can be set to reverse, forward, or non-directional.
When the directional criteria is met and the magnitude of conductance exceeds
the GN start value for the operate delay, the element trips.

Phase undervoltage (PHPTUV, ANSI 27)
Description
The undervoltage protection function is used to detect undervoltage conditions to
help protect plant under conditions such as:
•

Increased system loading which normally is handled by voltage regulating
equipment.

•

Faults which cause a reduction in the phase voltages associated with the
fault.

•

Loss of busbar voltage that requires isolation of output circuits.

•

Excessive voltage dips that cause motor loads to stall.

The function can measure phase-to-phase or phase-to-neutral voltages and
operation can be based on one, two or three elements dropping below the start
setting, and a start signal is issued. If the fault situation remains on longer than the
operate delay setting, a trip signal is issued. The input voltage may be configured
as phase-to-phase or phase-to-ground for 1, 2 or 3 VT phases.

Start signal decision
The function of the start signal decision block is to determine which start signal of
the phase will be triggered according to the measurement mode. For example,
•

For phase to phase measurements, when VAB is below the threshold, both
the start signal of phase A and phase B will be triggered together.

•

For phase to ground measurements, when VA is below the threshold, the
start signal of phase A will be triggered.

Output condition
The function of the output condition block is to determine which operate signal of
the phase will be the output according to the setting Operation mode. For
example,
•

262

If Operation mode = Three phase, the operate signal of the three phases will
be triggered.

P7/EN M/02-004A

Protection functions

Protection and control device

•

If Operation mode = Two phase, when any output of the Operate delay of the
two phases is true, and the operate signal of the two phases will be triggered;
otherwise, the operate signal of the two phases is false.

•

If Operation mode = One phase, the status of the operate signal is the same
as the output of Operate delay.

Operate characteristic
The undervoltage function operates with either the DT or the IDMT characteristic
that is selected. The IDMT characteristic follows the equation below:

1

t = TMS X

1where: =

G
Gs

E71112A

×

•

t is the Operate delay;

•

G is the measured value;

•

Gs is the setting value;

•

TMS is the setting IDMT delay multiplier.

Remanent undervoltage application
This is used in auto transfer schemes to ensure large motors have been
disconnected or run down before transferring to the alternate supply. This helps
avoid system shock and possible damage to the motor shaft and windings.
In this application the undervoltage element is measuring the busbar voltage.
When the incomer or an upstream breaker opens, the motor will start to run down
and back feed other loads if present. Normally the motor breaker will trip on
undervoltage, but until this occurs the motor will output a voltage at a frequency
proportional to the speed.
The undervoltage element can be used for this application and will function down
to 10 Hz. The element will operate when the voltage is below the setting or 10 Hz
and indicates to the auto transfer scheme there is no remanent voltage on the bus.
It is normally set to 20% nominal volts with a short delay of 100 ms.

P7/EN M/02-004A

Operation mode has three options:
•

•

•

When the measurement mode is phase-to-ground, VA is one element triggered
(same to VB and VC).
When the measurement mode is phase-to-phase, VAB lower than start value is
one element triggered (same to VBC and VCA).
When the triggered elements are more than the setting of operation mode, the
start signals can be triggered. Otherwise, even if VA is lower than the start value,
but the operation mode is two elements, the start signal of phase A cannot be
triggered.

Phase overvoltage (PHPTOV, ANSI 59)
Description
The overvoltage protection function is used to detect system voltages that are too
high.
The function measures either phase-to-phase or phase-to-neutral voltage and
operation can be on one, two or three elements exceeding the start value.

Start signal decision
The start signal decision block is to determine which start signal of the phase will
be triggered according to the measurement mode. For example,
•

For phase to phase measurements, when VAB is over the threshold, both the
start signal of phase A and phase B will be triggered together.

•

For phase to ground measurements, when VA is over the threshold, the start
signal of phase A will be triggered.

Output condition
The output condition block is to determine which operate signal of the phase will
be the output according to the setting of the operation mode. For example,
•

If the setting is three-phase, the operate signal of the three-phase will be
triggered.

•

If the setting is two-phase, when any output of the operate delay of the twophase is true, the operate signal of these two-phase will be triggered,
otherwise, the operate signal of all phases is false.

•

If the setting is any phase, the status of the operate signal is same as the
output of Operate delay.

Operate characteristic
The overvoltage function operates with either the DT or the IDMT characteristic
that is selected. The IDMT characteristic follows the equation below:

t = TMS X

where: =

•

•

•

Positive sequence undervoltage (PPSPTUV, ANSI 27D)
Description
The positive phase sequence undervoltage protection function helps protect
motors against faulty operation due to insufficient or unbalanced network voltage.
This undervoltage protection function calculates the positive sequence of the
fundamental frequency component V1.
By using the positive sequence, all three phases are supervised, with one value,
and if the motor loses the connection to the network (loss of mains), the
undervoltage situation is detected even if the frequency decreases significantly
from nominal frequency.
Whenever the positive sequence voltage V1 drops below the start setting of a
particular stage, this stage activates and a start signal is issued. If the fault
situation remains on longer than the time defined in the operate delay setting, a
trip signal is issued.

Blocking during breaker open
As this element is normally used for starting supervision, it is not blocked by
default when the bay is dead. If the element is used for tripping, the BayDead
signal can be linked to the inhibit operation when the breakers are open.

Block diagram

Positive sequence overvoltage (PPSPTOV, ANSI 59D)
Description
The positive sequence overvoltage protection function is applied to help protect
plant from faulty operation caused by network overvoltage.
This function uses the positive sequence voltage as its main input to supervise all
three phases. Once the positive sequence voltage exceeds the start value, a start
signal is issued. If the fault condition remains longer than the operate delay
setting, a trip signal is issued.

Reactive power controlled undervoltage (DQPTUV,
ANSI 32/27)
Description
The reactive power controlled undervoltage (QV protection) function provides
time-delayed disconnection from the power system, allowing temporary support
through extended feed-in during a power system fault.
The QV protection function is based on evaluating directional reactive power and
three phase-to-phase voltages at the grid connection point. The protection

272

P7/EN M/02-004A

Protection functions

Protection and control device

operates after a configured time delay when the Start signal triggers. Two definite
time (DT) operate delays are available, one for the generator circuit breaker (CB)
and one for the grid circuit breaker (CB).
The Start signal triggers when all of the following conditions are met, as illustrated
in Operate zone of the DQPTUV, page 273:
•

The direction of reactive power matches the configured Direction mode.

•

All three phase-to-phase voltages drop below the configured voltage
threshold.

•

The positive sequence currents exceed the minimum current Imin.

•

The absolute value of the reactive power exceeds the minimum power Qmin.

•

The angle between reactive and active power exceeds the configuredSector.

The operate zone of the protection is shown as the shaded gray area in the figure
below. The minimum current Imin is represented by a circle centered at the origin of
the coordinate system. The minimum power Qmin is indicated by a dashed straight
line. The symbol α in the figure represents the setting value of the parameter
Sector. The direction is determined by the value of Q: a positive value indicates
the forward direction, and a negative value indicates the reverse direction.
Figure 118 - Operate zone of the DQPTUV

Application example
The output Str, Op, and OpGri are linked to the start and operate of trip
conditioner (PRTC) by default. The output Op can be mapped to the Generator
CB and OpGri can be mapped to the Grid CB. See the following figure for an
application example. In this example, the Generator CB would normally be tripped
via the main trip conditioner. The OpGri signal would then be routed to an output
contact which would inter trip the Grid CB. The RstTmms setting holds the contact
closed for a minimum time to help ensure correct trip operation for the Grid CB.

Neutral overvoltage (EFPTOV, ANSI 59N)
Description
The neutral overvoltage protection function is used as non-selective backup for
ground faults and also for selective ground fault protection for motors having a unit
transformer between the motor and the busbar.
This function is sensitive to the fundamental frequency component of the neutral
displacement voltage. The attenuation of the third harmonic is more than 60 dB
because the third harmonics is present between the neutral point and ground even
when there is no ground fault.
Whenever the measured value exceeds the start setting of a particular stage, this
stage starts and issues a start signal. If the fault situation is present longer than
the operate delay setting, a trip signal is issued.

276

P7/EN M/02-004A

Protection functions

Protection and control device

Generator inter-turn overvoltage application
Inter-turn faults in a generator with a single winding can be detected by observing
the zero sequence voltage across the machine. Normally, no zero sequence
voltage should exist but a short circuit of one or more turns on one phase will
cause the generated Electro Motive Force (EMF) to contain some zero sequence
component. This method of inter-turn protection requires a dedicated insulated VT
with its neutral connected to the generator star point. The VT will normally provide
a broken delta secondary which can be monitored by an EFPTOV element.
Figure 121 - Inter-turn protection (V2) and ground fault protection (V1) by
zero sequence voltage

Negative sequence overvoltage
(NPSPTOV, ANSI 47)
Description
The negative sequence overvoltage protection monitors the voltage phase
sequence detecting a reverse rotation or voltage unbalance due to a missing
(asymmetrical) phase. The detection of these conditions is used to trip the
machine and prevent damage to both the motor and the mechanically coupled
process. If the negative sequence voltage input exceeds the voltage setting, this
function starts instantaneously, and it operates with the definite time delay.
This protection stage has a settable Reset delay that enables the detection of
intermittent faults. This means that the Operate delay timer does not reset
immediately after the fault is cleared, but resets after the Reset delay has elapsed.
If the fault appears again before the Reset delay has elapsed, the Operate delay
timer continues from the previous value, the function eventually trips if faults are
occurring often enough.

Interturn overvoltage (ITPTOV, ANSI 59IT)
Description
The interturn overvoltage protection function is applied to help detect inter-turn
faults. For higher sensitivity, this function is normally applied to generators whose
stator winding has limited branches and turns, such as large steam generators.
This function does not require a dedicated inter-turn VT, but instead uses the interturn voltage calculated from the star point voltage (Vsp) and the summated threephase voltage (VN). When the calculated inter-turn voltage exceeds the start
value for the configured operate delay, a trip signal is sent.
The third harmonic voltage generated by the stator winding imbalance is filtered
out for better performance.
Figure 124 - Measurement points for inter-turn voltage calculation

Vsp
VN

P7121TA

Inter-turn voltage calculation
The inter-turn voltage is calculated as follows:

VIT = VN/3 − Vsp

P7121UB

VN is the summated three-phase voltage, which is either calculated by adding all
three phase voltages together for the 3ph connection, or directly measured for the
3ph+N and 2pp+N connections.
Vsp is the measured star point voltage.
