Overfluxing (PVPH, ANSI 24)
Description
The overfluxing protection function is applied to help protect transformers and
generators from excessive magnetic flux, which induces excessive magnetizing
current and, in turn, results in overheating and possible insulation failure.
Excessive magnetic flux can be caused by voltage increase, frequency decrease,
or both. The higher the V/Hz ratio, the greater the magnetic flux and the risk of
damage.
This function uses the V/Hz ratio to monitor the magnetic flux in the iron core of
the plant. When calculating the V/Hz ratio, the voltage value is taken from the
maximum of the phase-to-phase voltages for delta windings, the maximum of the
phase-to-ground voltages for solidly earthed star windings, or the maximum phase
to phase voltage minus zero sequence voltage for resistance earthed windings.
When the V/Hz ratio exceeds the alarm threshold setting long enough, an alarm is
raised. When the V/Hz ratio exceeds the start value setting, the functions uses
either a DT timer or IDMT curves to determine the operate delay, after which the
function trips.
NOTE:
•

If the V/Hz ratio returns below the alarm threshold before an alarm is
raised, the alarm timer instantly resets.

•

If the V/Hz ratio returns below the start value before a trip is triggered, the
start signal resets after the configured Reset time.

•

If the operate delay is lower than the Time to operate alarm value setting,
an alarm is raised.

Operate delay
When Operating curve is set to Definite Time, the operate delay is fixed to the
value from setting DT/IDMT adder op delay.

P7/EN M/02-004A

283

Protection and control device

Protection functions

Otherwise, the operate delay is the sum of the calculated top and DT/IDMT adder
op delay, as detailed below:
•

When Operating curve is set to Type A, top is calculated with the following
formula:

top =
•

TMS
(M − 1)0.5

E71199E

When Operating curve is set to Type B, top is calculated with the following
formula:

top =
•

TMS
(M − 1) E71199D

When Operating curve is set to Type C, top is calculated with the following
formula:

top =

TMS
(M − 1)2 E71199C

TMS is the IDMT delay multiplier setting;
M is the V/Hz ratio divided by the start value.
NOTE: When the calculated top exceeds Max op delay, Max op delay is used
as top; when the calculated top is lower than Min op delay, Min op delay is used
as top.

Block diagram
Figure 126 - Block diagram of the PVPH

Rate of voltage change (PVRC, ANSI ROCOV)
Description
The rate of voltage change (ROCOV) protection function is mainly applied to:
•

help prevent power system instability caused by reactive power imbalance

•

help detect islanded power systems that are mainly based on inverterinterfaced power sources. In this case, other functions such as ROCOF are
often used together.

This function only works with a 3ph VT group and the operate signal applies to all
three phases. The ROCOV is calculated with phase-to-phase voltages and the

286

P7/EN M/02-004A

Protection functions

Protection and control device

configurable measuring window. A longer measuring window improves the
accuracy of the ROCOV calculation but also increases the operating time.
This function has three operation modes:
•

Falling: The start signal is issued when the absolute ROCOV values of all
phases exceed the start value and the maximum of the phase-to-phase
voltages is below the Voltage release value.

•

Rising: The start signal is issued when the absolute ROCOV values of all
phases exceed the start value and the minimum of the phase-to-phase
voltages is above the Voltage release value.

•

Both: The start signal is issued when the absolute ROCOV values of all
phases exceed the start value.
NOTE:
•

To avoid oscillations, a fixed 50 ms delay is added before the start signal
is issued.

•

When the minimum of the phase-to-phase voltages is below the UV inhibit
value, the function is blocked.

Voltage vector shift (VVSPPAM, ANSI 78VS)
Description
The voltage vector shift protection function (VVSPPAM) helps detect Loss of
Mains (LOM) conditions for embedded generators. When a generator is islanded
from the grid, a sudden shift in the voltage vector occurs at the point of
disconnection. This function detects such shifts and issues a trip signal to
disconnect the generator, helping protect the generator from operating under
islanded conditions.
The function monitors the positive sequence voltage angle (V1φ) and calculates
the angular difference over a 50 ms window, equivalent to 2.5 cycles at 50 Hz or 3
cycles at 60 Hz. When the shift exceeds the configured threshold and persists for
a defined number of processing cycles, the function starts and operates after a
definite time delay.
The following figure illustrates the voltage vector shift that occurs when an
embedded generator becomes islanded due to an LOM.

P7/EN M/02-004A

289

Protection and control device

Protection functions

Figure 128 - Voltage vector shift during LOM
A: Grid-connected voltage vector

B: Voltage vector after disconnection

ZG

ZG

ΔU

ΔU'
Z

U

EG

Zs

EG

Z

U'

ΔU

ΔU'

Zs

ΔU
U'
U

U
φ

EG

EG

φ

Δφ

P7124SA

P7124TA

A: The generator is connected to the grid.
•

The voltage vector U represents the voltage at the point of common coupling.

•

The vector ΔU indicates the voltage drop across the generator impedance ZG,
calculated as the difference between the generator voltage EG and U.

•

The angle φ represents the initial phase angle between EG and U.

B: After disconnection from the grid, the generator operates in islanded mode.
•

The voltage vector U′ represents the new voltage at the point of common coupling.

•

The vector ΔU′ shows the shift in voltage due to the loss of mains.

•

The angle Δφ represents the change in phase angle between EG and U′, used by
the VVSPPAM function to detect islanding conditions.

The location of the open point and the magnitude of the load change influence
both the direction and size of the vector shift. When the shift is below the pickup
threshold, it may not be detected.
To improve detection reliability, the VVSPPAM is typically used in combination with
following functions:

290

•

Voltage protection

•

Frequency protection

•

Directional overcurrent protection

•

Power protection

P7/EN M/02-004A

Protection functions

Protection and control device

Overfrequency (PTOF, ANSI 81O)
Description
The overfrequency protection function detects the abnormally high frequency
compared to the rated frequency to monitor power supply quality or help protect a
generator against overspeed conditions.
The overfrequency protection function is generally applied where the generation
capacity is greater than the connected load to prevent the system frequency from
increasing above a specified threshold and subjecting the generator to an
overspeed condition.
This protection is used in load restoration schemes to detect that the power
system frequency is recovered sufficiently to allow load which is previously shed
to be reconnected. The frequency measurement is fixed to the bay VT input.

Protection and control device

Underfrequency (PTUF, ANSI 81U)
Description
The underfrequency protection function detects the abnormally low frequency
compared to the rated frequency to monitor power supply quality. The protection is
used for overall tripping or load shedding.
The underfrequency protection function is generally applied where the generation
capacity is less than the connected load to prevent the system frequency from
decreasing below a specified threshold.
This protection is used in load shedding schemes, often in conjunction with the
frequency rate of change protection, to cover slow or fast reductions in frequency
caused by the prevailing system conditions, including consideration of rotating
loads. The frequency measurement is fixed to the bay VT input.

Underfrequency load shedding (LSPTUF, ANSI 81LS)
Description
The underfrequency load shedding (UFLS) protection function is applied to trip the
load when the system frequency is dropping due to generation and load
imbalance. The underfrequency setting can be adapted for up to 10 thresholds to
allow feeders to be prioritized for tripping. To avoid further frequency collapse,
feeders are only shed when they are not generating back into the system. A block
CB closing signal is also available to ensure closing only occurs when the system
frequency has recovered sufficiently.

Active stage selection
The UFLS protection function has up to 10 settable underfrequency thresholds.
Only one threshold is active at a time.
Figure 132 - Active stage selection
Stage1

A
ActStg

Stage2
......
Stage9
Stage10

ActStgStrVal
NoStage
Stg
P7122IA

A

•

P7/EN M/02-004A

Stage activation logic

Stage 1 to Stage 10 inputs are used to select the active stage. The activation
is based on the rising edge. When two inputs are received simultaneously, the
higher stage is selected. If the selected stage is not enabled, out of the range
defined by NumStg, its input will be ignored, and the active stage does not
change.

297

Protection and control device

Protection functions

•

ActStg displays the active stage and can be controlled to select the active
stage. The selected stage must be in the range defined by NumStg, or its
input will be ignored.

•

NoStage is to deactivate all stages, but it does not disable the external input.

•

ActStgStrVal is the start value of the activated stage.

Active power blocking
To help ensure the load is tripped and the generation is retained, the UFLS
function includes an active power blocking element which blocks tripping when the
power is flowing to the busbar.
The active power blocking is triggered when all the following three conditions are
met, as shown in the figure below.
•

All three-phase currents exceed the minimum current Imin.

•

The active power exceeds the minimum power Pmin.

•

The angle between the active power and the apparent power is within the
range of 90°-Sector.

The blocking of the protection stages is indicated as the gray zone in the following
figure. The minimum current Imin is indicated as the circle centered at the origin of
the coordinates. The minimum power Pmin is indicated as the dashed straight line.
The symbol α in the figure represents the setting value of the parameter Sector.
Figure 133 - Blocking of the UFLS

Q/S

α
P

P

Q

Q

G

Imin
P/S

P

P

Q

Q

G

α

Pmin
P7122JB

α

Sector angle

External operation
An external operation signal (ExOp) is available to connect the UFLS to other
functions, such as PTUF and PFRC, to allow them to trip through a common UFLS
element.
298

Frequency rate of change (PFRC, ANSI 81R)
Description
The frequency rate of change (ROCOF) protection function is applied to detect the
immediate condition of power imbalance, and can help shed the load in a more
timely manner to reduce the possibility of a widespread network collapse.

P7/EN M/02-004A

301

Protection and control device

Protection functions

The ROCOF is calculated with the frequency from FTGAPC and the configurable
measuring window. A longer measuring window improves accuracy but also
increases the operating time. The Operation mode can be set as Falling, Rising,
or Both:
•

If the Operation mode is set as Falling, the function is frequency-supervised
ROCOF for underfrequency. When the absolute value of ROCOF exceeds
the start value and the measured frequency is below the Frequency release
value setting, a start signal is sent. If the start signal continues for the
configured operate delay, the function trips.

•

If the Operation mode is set as Rising, the function is frequency-supervised
ROCOF for overfrequency. When the absolute value of ROCOF exceeds the
start value and the measured frequency is above the Frequency release
value setting, a start signal is sent. If the start signal continues for the
configured operate delay, the function trips.

•

If the Operation mode is set as Both, the function is plain ROCOF for
underfrequency or overfrequency. The function trips when the absolute value
of ROCOF exceeds the start value for the configured operate delay.
NOTE:
•

To disable frequency supervision in the falling or rising mode, set
Frequency release value (BlkHzVal) as 70 Hz or 40 Hz, respectively.

•

The function can be inhibited by any of the following cases:
◦

The frequency or ROCOF is invalid.

◦

Input Inhibit is true.

◦

The absolute value of ROCOF is above the Frequency release value
setting when High ROCOF blocking is set to On.

◦

The positive sequence voltage is below the UV inhibit value setting.

Protection and control device

High impedance diff (HIZPDIF, ANSI 87/64G)
Description
For high impedance diff protection, CTs are placed at the ends of the differential
zone and paralleled. A resistor is placed in series with PowerLogic P7 CT input to
stabilize the protection for CT saturation during through fault conditions.
•

For high impedance applications, the Measurement mode should be set to
Peak to peak.

•

For CBCT-based residual current applications, the Measurement mode
should be set to FFT.

Peak to peak can be calculated as follows:
Peak to peak = ABS(max(48 Samples in latest cycle) - min(48 Samples in latest
cycle)) / (2 × SQRT(2))
High impedance differential protection requires all CTs to be the same ratio. The
high impedance principle is best explained by considering a differential scheme
where one CT is saturated for an external fault, as shown in the diagram below.
Figure 136 - Principle of high impedance differential protection

CT2

P71183A

Voltage across device circuit:

VS = If x (RCT + 2RL )

E71121A

Stabilizing resistor, RST, limits spill current to setting IS:

RST =

VS
- RR
IS

E71122A

Where:
If = Maximum secondary through fault current
RR = Device burden
RL = Resistance of a single lead from the device to the current transformer
RCT = Current transformer secondary winding resistance
•

For an internal fault, the current feeding into the device is ICT1 + ICT2

•

For an external fault with no CT saturation, the current feeding into the device
is ICT1 - ICT2 = 0

•

For an external fault with one CT saturated, e.g. CT2, the secondary current
produced by the CT1 will flow through CT2.
If the magnetizing impedance of CT2 is negligible, the maximum voltage
across the device circuit will be equal to the secondary fault current multiplied
by the connected impedance, (RL3 + RL4 + RCT2)

For the device to achieve stability at the maximum applied voltage, it is necessary
to elevate the overall impedance of the device circuit. This adjustment helps
P7/EN M/02-004A

305

Protection and control device

Protection functions

ensure that the current passing through the device remains below the current
setting (IS), preventing maloperation.
As the impedance of the device input alone is relatively low, a series of connected
external resistors is required. An additional non-linear resistor, Metrosil, may be
required to limit the peak secondary circuit voltage during internal fault conditions.
Examples of the device connections for high impedance protection are shown
below. Phase mode can be applied to any system with three phase CTs at all ends
of the differential zone with no transformers within the zone.
Examples include bus, machine and reactor differential. Ground mode is used for
restricted ground fault applications and only detects ground faults within the
protected zone.
Examples include transformer winding and machine ground fault detection.
Figure 137 - Example of high impedance protection connections with phase
mode

Bias differential (PHPDIF, ANSI 87)
Description
The biased differential protection calculates the difference between the currents
entering and leaving a protected zone. The protection operates when this
difference exceeds the threshold.
Differential currents may also be generated due to CT saturation. To provide
stability during saturation, PowerLogic P7 adopts a biasing technique. This
method effectively raises the setting of the device in proportion to the value of
saturation to help prevent relay maloperation. The figure below shows the
operating characteristics of the biased differential protection.
A three-slope biased differential protection operating characteristic is applied. The
lower slope K1 provides stability for small CT mismatches, whilst ensuring good
sensitivity to resistive faults under heavy load conditions. The higher slope K2 is
used to improve device stability under heavy through fault conditions where CT
saturation may occur.
Figure 142 - Biased differential protection operating characteristic
CT1

Id = ICT1 + ICT2

CT2

C

A
HiSet
K2
B
K1
Is

Ir

Ir2

Max fault

Differential and bias currents calculation
The calculation of differential, general bias and saturation bias currents is
performed on a per phase basis.

Differential current calculation (Id)
The differential current is the vector sum of the phase currents measured at CT1
and CT2.

Id = | ICT1 + ICT2 |

E71101A

General bias current calculation (Ibias)
The bias current is the average of the phase currents measured at CT1 and CT2.
| | ICT2 |
|
Ibias = ICT1 +
2

E71102A

Bias current calculation (Ibias) with AddBias
If the bias differential function is used with summated CTs, then I1 and I2 are the
currents used as input. I1 comes from a 3 phase CT summation group, which
summates I11 and I12 and generates the additional bias current.
I11
I1

I2

S11

S2
CT11

CT2

I12

S12
CT12

P71237B

In such a case, the bias current is calculated as follows:
| | ICT2 | + IAddBias
|
Ibias = ICT1 +
2

E71220A

Where,
IAddBias = |I11| + |I12| - |I11 + I12|

Restraint current calculation (Ir)
The biased differential protection takes the maximum bias current from all three
phases for the operating characteristic (Ir = max(IbiasA, IbiasB, IbiasC)).

Saturation bias current calculation (Isat)
CT Saturation is very likely to occur during faults or for load currents with a DC
offset such as transformer inrush. To avoid differential operation during severe

310

P7/EN M/02-004A

Protection functions

Protection and control device

saturation the device applies a saturation bias to temporarily increase the
differential trip level when saturation is detected.
The device monitors the change in bias compared to the previous two cycles on
per phase basis. A drop in bias can be due to load change, fault clearance or CT
saturation. For CT saturation during external faults there will be a corresponding
increase in differential current. For load changes, fault clearance or saturation
during internal faults, there will be a reduction of differential current or no
differential current.
The saturation bias is calculated on a per phase basis as three times the bias drop
over the last 40 ms. This value is limited to the differential measurement.
The saturation bias is added to the trip level to stabilize the element when CT
saturation occurs.
The CT saturation is due to DC components on the power system and it will come
out of saturation depending upon the primary time constant. The saturation bias
will decrement according to the Saturation time constant setting which can
normally be left at the maximum setting.
The saturation bias is set to zero if the protection has started, Saturation time
constant is zero or when the bias current is less than 0.5 LoSet.
Detailed saturation bias is calculated as follows:
Isat(n) = max[min(3 × (max(Ibias(n-1), Ibias(n-2),...Ibias(n-8)) - Ibias(n)), Id(n)), Isat(n-1) ×
(1 - 5/SatTC)]
•

If biased differential protection starts, Isat(n) = 0;

•

If setting Saturation time constant = 0, Isat(n) = 0;

•

If Ibias(n) < 0.5 LoSet, Isat(n) = 0.

Where:
Ibias(n-x): bias current calculated at x execution cycles (5...40 ms) before.
Ibias(n): bias current calculated at current execution cycle.
Id(n): differential current calculated at current execution cycle.
Isat(n-1): saturation bias current calculated at 1 execution cycle (5 ms) before.

Phase comparison
The phase comparison is to indicate if the fault is internal or external. It is
implemented in K2 slope (Ir > Ir2).
•

When Ir > Ir2, and one of the following five conditions happens, it will be
treated as an internal fault and ISatbias will be set to 0.
1. (I1 < 0.05) OR (I2 < 0.05)
2. [max(I1(n),...,I1(n-5)) < 0.05] OR [max(I2(n),...,I2(n-5)) < 0.05]
3. [max(I1(n),...,I1(n-5)) < I2 × 0.05] OR [max(I2(n),...,I2(n-5)) < I1 × 0.05]
4. [AND (min(I1(n), ..., I1(n-8)) < 0.5, | I1(n) - I1(n-8) | < 0.05)]
OR [AND (min(I2(n),...,I2(n-8)) < 0.5, | I2(n) - I2(n-8) | < 0.05)]
5. [(I1 > Ir2 × 0.25) OR (I2 > Ir1 × 0.25)] AND [| Angle(I1) - Angle(I2) | < 50°]
NOTE: The first four conditions are to predict internal fault in single end
fed system. The last condition is to predict internal fault in double end fed
system.

•

When the angle between I1 and I2 is within [90°, 180°], also the five conditions
are not achieved, it will be treated as external fault and Isat will be set as Id.

•

Otherwise, Isat will be set as the result derived from the equation:
Isat(n) = max[min(3 × (max(Ibias(n-1), Ibias(n-2),...Ibias(n-8)) - Ibias(n)), Id(n)), Isat
(n-1) × (1 - 5/SatTC)],
which is described in Differential and bias currents calculation, page 310.

P7/EN M/02-004A

311

Protection and control device

Protection functions

CT ratio scaling
It is recommended that CT1 and CT2 have the same ratio, but different ratios of
up to 10 times are allowed. If the ratio of Primary nominal currents of CT2 and CT1
is outside 0.1 to 10, the biased protection is blocked.
The differential and bias current values for each measurement system are
calculated from the current values after CT ratio matching. CT1 is used as the
reference and the currents of CT2 are scaled to CT1.
CT2Prim
|
CT1Prim

Id = | ICT1 + ICT2 X

E71103A

CT2Prim

Ibias =

| ICT1| + | ICT2 X CT1Prim |
2

E71105A

Split phase mode
If the generator stator windings are wound with two identical three-phase windings
connected in parallel, and the windings are brought out separately, biased
differential element can be set to Split Phase Mode to provide additional inter-turn
protection.

Tripping criteria
The differential current (Id) threshold changes according to the value of restraint
current (Ir) as shown in the operating characteristic.
The saturation bias current (Isat) is on a per phase basis and is not affected by the
K1 or K2 setting.
The operating threshold of per phase is formulated as follows:
•

0 < Ir ≤ 0.5 Is
Id threshold = Is

312

P7/EN M/02-004A

Protection functions

Protection and control device

•

Slope K1: 0.5 Is < Ir ≤ Ir2
Id threshold = Is + ( Ir - 0.5 × Is) × K1 + Isat

•

Slope K2: Ir > Ir2
Id threshold = Is + ( Ir2 - 0.5 × Is) × K1 + ( Ir - Ir2) × K2 + Isat

The start signal of the biased differential protection is active when the following
conditions are simultaneously present:
•

No inhibit input.

•

Ratio of CT2 and CT1 primary nominal currents within 0.1...10.

•

The differential current is more than Id threshold for four consecutive
measurements near the threshold or
two consecutive measurements above 1.1 Id threshold.

An operate signal is issued once the operate delay has elapsed. The operate
signal will be reset when differential current drops down under 95% of the
threshold.

Restricted ground fault (RGFPDIF, ANSI 64RGF)
Description
The restricted ground fault protection function is used for resistance grounded
transformers where the backup ground fault time is usually too long to prevent
excessive transformer damage and inadequate coverage provided by differential.
The RGFPDIF can also be applied to
•

autotransformers to provide a differential unaffected by transformer ratio.

•

grounded star windings to provide a more sensitive ground fault detection
than that available from differential.
NOTE: The element applies low impedance restricted ground fault, high
impedance restricted ground fault is also available in the high impedance
(HIZPDIF) element.

Low impedance RGFPDIF
Each individual CT is usually considered for biasing and saturation detection. As
each winding is considered independently, there is no ratio/phase correction, but
all CTs must be matched to a common reference which is the primary CT.
The low impedance RGFPDIF uses a two-slope biased characteristic.
•

The low impedance RGFPDIF compares the vector sum of the restricted
ground fault protection function of the transformer winding to the neutral point
current. The differential and bias currents are calculated from the current
variables after scaling.

The limitation is for high resistance grounded systems where the pickup relative to
the CT nominal is very low and the phase CT ankle point can cause
maloperations.
•

The minimum pickup range relative to the primary CT should be 5% to 200%.

•

Primary ratio matching factors should be:

•

P7/EN M/02-004A

◦

Neutral CT/Reference CT: 0.05 to 2.

◦

Second CT/Reference CT: 0.05 to 1.

◦

Load neutral CT/Reference CT: 0.5 to 1.

The low impedance RGFPDIF compares the vector sum of the restricted
ground fault protection function of the transformer winding to the neutral point
current. The differential and bias currents are calculated from the current
variables after scaling.

315

Tripping Characteristic
The tripping characteristic of the low impedance RGFPDIF has one knee, which is
when Ibias = 1 pu
The lower slope provides sensitivity for internal faults. The higher slope provides
stability under through fault conditions, since transient differential currents may be
present due to current transformer saturation.
Once the differential and bias currents are calculated, the following comparisons
are made and an operate/restrained signal is obtained:
Flat slope:
•

Ibias ≤ 1

•

Idiff ≥ Loset

A slope:
•

Ibias > 1

•

Idiff ≥ Loset + (Ibias - 1) × 0.5

characteristic.

Phase comparator
Phase A phase comparator is used to distinguish internal fault and external fault.
If the phase difference between INeut and Isum is within [-90°, 90°], it is an internal
fault, otherwise it is an external fault.
When NeutChkValA is set as 0, PhComp is False. Otherwise, PhComp is True
when any of the situations in each mode satisfies:
•

•

•

When operation mode is star mode,
◦

(INeut > NeutChkValA) and (sum(RefIA, RefIB, RefIC) < NeutChkValA
and lasts for greater than 10 ms)

◦

(INeut > NeutChkValA) and (sum(RefIA, RefIB, RefIC) > NeutChkValA)
and (| INeutang - sum(RefIA, RefIB, RefIC)ang | < 90°)

When operation mode is Auto-transformer mode,
◦

(INeut > NeutChkValA) and (sum(RefIA, RefIB, RefIC, SndIA, SndIB,
SndIC) < NeutChkValA and lasts for greater than 10 ms)

◦

(INeut > NeutChkValA) and (sum(RefIA, RefIB, RefIC, SndIA, SndIB,
SndIC) > NeutChkValA) and (| INeutang - sum(RefIA, RefIB, RefIC,
SndIA, SndIB, SndIC)ang | < 90°)

When operation mode is 4-wire mode,
◦

(INeut > NeutChkValA) and (sum(RefIA, RefIB, RefIC, ILoad) <
NeutChkValA and lasts for greater than 10 ms)

◦

(INeut > NeutChkValA) and (sum(RefIA, RefIB, RefIC, ILoad) >
NeutChkValA) and (| INeutang - sum(RefIA, RefIB, RefIC, ILoad)ang | <
90°)

NOTE: The hysteresis of angle is 5°. 10 ms delay is for Phase comparator
stability when the fault disappears.

Transformer differential (TR2PDIF/TR3PDIF, ANSI 87T)
Description
The transformer differential function is used for all transformers above 1 MVA to
help protect the transformer against internal faults. Most transformers have 2
windings, but 3 windings are often used for applications such as rectifiers above
20 MVA.
Principle of transformer differential, page 321 illustrates the principle of the
transformer differential. Current transformers on the primary side and secondary
side are summated after compensating for the vector group, transformer and CT
ratios.
Figure 149 - Principle of transformer differential
I2
I1
I3

Id>
P7121WA

Based on the primary current values adjusted for the winding voltage and phase,
currents are compared across all ends continuously. In normal operation
conditions or during external faults without CT saturation, the primary current at all
ends remain almost the same, so the comparison result is practically zero.
However, when faults occur within the protected transformer, the comparison
result is higher than the operational value, and as a result, the protection system
will trip. The protection zone is determined by the location of current transformers
around the transformer.
Bias is applied in the function to help ensure stability during external faults while
still allowing sensitive settings to pick up internal faults. This is facilitated by a 3slope bias differential element.
•

•

When operation mode is 3 windings
◦

For differential current: Idiff = |Í1 + Í2 + Í3|

◦

For restrain current: Ires = (|Í1| + |Í2| + |Í3|) /2

When operation mode is 2 windings
◦

For differential current: Idff = |Í1 + Í2|

◦

For restrain current: Ires = (|Í1| + |Í2|) /2

NOTE: For summation schemes such as 1.5CB, the restrain current
calculation also takes an additional bias current into account.
Trip area of the transformer differential, page 322 shows the operating
characteristic.

P7/EN M/02-004A

Tripping characteristic:
When Ir1 ≤ IBiasCross ≤ Ir2,
•

When IBiasCross > Ir2,
•

Itrip = Iset + (Ir2 - Ir1) × K1 + (IBiasCross - Ir2) × K2
NOTE:
•

IBiasCross is the maximum restrain current on three phases.

•

Start operation uses a counter to help ensure correct restraint.

Phase correction
To help ensure the correct operation of transformer differential protection, it is
necessary that the transformer primary and secondary currents measured by the
relay are in phase. In case of a delta/star transformer, the balanced three-phase
current has a phase shift of 30° between the primary and secondary windings. If
left uncorrected, this phase difference may lead to the relay detecting the through
current as an imbalanced fault current and result in an operation.
Wndg2/Wndg3 vector group sets the currents used in the differential element.
•

For even numbered windings, the appropriate secondary current is chosen.

•

For odd numbered windings, the current is determined by the current
difference between the appropriate secondary currents divided by √3.

The Phase correction, page 322 shows the pattern:
Table 195 - Phase correction

Zero sequence current filter
When enabled zero sequence filtering is applied, the filtered current is calculated
per end as:
I0 = (IA + IB + IC)/3
I A’ = IA - I0
Where,
•

IA: Phase A current of the respective CT input

•

IA': Phase A filtered current of the respective CT input

•

I0: Zero sequence amplitude matched current for the respective CT input

Otherwise, the filtered current is:
IA' = I A

Ratio correction
To help ensure the differential element operates correctly, the currents must be
balanced under load and fault conditions. As the primary and secondary line CT
ratios may not match the transformer rated winding currents exactly, a Matching
Factor (MF) is used to balance to the reference winding. The user calculates the
MF for each end using the transformer and CT ratings.
MF = Inomx ×√3 × Vnomx/Sref
Where,
•

MF: Match Factor of CT

•

Inomx: Rated current for the respective CT input

•

Vnomx: Reference voltage for winding x (usually rated or mid tap voltage)

•

Sref: Common reference power for all ends (usually the transformer VA)

The current used for the differential is:
IA" = MF × IA'

Inrush detection
Any abrupt change of magnetizing voltage may result in a magnetizing inrush.
This most commonly occurs during transformer energizing (initial inrush), but can
also occur upon voltage recovery after primary system failure (recovery inrush) or
voltage drop during energization of a parallel transformer (sympathetic inrush).

P7/EN M/02-004A

323

Protection and control device

Protection functions

This magnetizing inrush current usually flows into one transformer winding only.
As a result, it is observed as differential current by the protection. Inrush current
detection and prevention from maloperation is an inherent part of the differential
protection function.
H2 blocked blocks for 2nd harmonic blocking for inrush and is normally set to
cross block unless the transformer comprises three single phase units with a
grounded star connection on the energizing side.
•

When H2 blocked is set to Off, inrush detection is disabled.

•

When H2 blocked is set to Cross, the maximum calculated H2 ratio is used to
block all phases.

•

When H2 blocked is set to On, each phase is compared individually.

The inrush condition is determined by evaluating the ratio between the 2nd
harmonic and the fundamental current component, based on the phase
segregated differential currents. This signal analysis occurs only if the differential
is greater than the fixed minimum current of min (0.1, LoSet). The inrush block
signal is only activated if confirmed by a gap of more than 75° in the differential
waveform.
Serious internal faults may result in CT saturation, causing H2 ratio > H2/H1 ratio
block value. As a consequence, differential protection may fail to trip because it is
mistakenly blocked by 2nd harmonic. To resolve this problem, three methods are
used in addition to the unrestrained high-set.
1. If the maximum sample value of differential current in one cycle exceeds
Harmonic block limit, the inrush block logic will be ignored and the differential
protection will trip based on the operation characteristic.
NOTE: If Harmonic block limit is set to zero, inrush block logic works
without checking it.
2. Assuming inrush current would be on three phases, it cannot be on single
phase. If the H2 ratio of two phase currents are below 5% in all windings,
differential protection will not be blocked by inrush detection logic.
3. In one cycle, if the absolute instantaneous value of 39 samples is above a
fixed proportion of the differential peak value, it is regarded as fault current,
differential protection will not be blocked by inrush detection logic.

Overfluxing detection
If the transformer is supplied with a voltage more than its rated voltage factor,
saturation effects occur leading to short spikes of current at the voltage peaks.
Without stabilization, these would lead to differential protection tripping. The fact
that the current of the protected object under saturation conditions has a high
proportion of 5th harmonic serves as the basis of stabilization.
H5 blocked enables 5th harmonic blocking for ovefluxing.
•

When H5 blocking is set to Off, overflux blocking is disabled.

•

When H5 blocking is set to Cross, the maximum calculated H5 ratio is used to
block all phases.

•

When H5 blocking is set to On, each phase is compared individually.

Overfluxing condition is determined by evaluating the ratio of 5th harmonic to
fundamental current component based on the phase segregated differential
currents. This analysis is executed only if the differential is within reasonable
limits:
•

The fundamental of the differential current is greater than a fixed minimum
current of Min (0.1, LoSet).

•

All instantaneous differential samples are smaller than Harmonic block limit.

•

If Harmonic block limit is set to zero, the 5th harmonic logic works without
checking it.

When overfluxing detection is enabled and an overfluxing condition is determined,
the differential protection will be blocked.

324

P7/EN M/02-004A

Protection functions

Protection and control device

An alarm will be issued if a sustained overfluxing occurs for longer than H5 alarm
time.
NOTE: The alarm operates regardless of the H5 blocking setting and
Harmonic block limit setting.

CT saturation detection
During external faults, CTs are likely to saturate due to the slowly decaying DC
offset of the primary current, or remanent flux in the CT core. Appropriate
measures can detect these conditions and prevent false differential tripping.
The external fault detection considers the time to saturation of the CTs.
Immediately after an external fault occurs, the bias increases but the differential
does not increase until saturation occurs. When these conditions are detected the
relay will set the External fault output, blocking differential operation.
After the initial heavy saturation the CT will come out of saturation each cycle for a
short period. During this time the differential currents are near zero. If a
subsequent internal fault occurs, this period of balance will no longer occur and
external fault blocking is reset, allowing the fault to be cleared.

1.5 CT summation schemes (TR3PDIF only)
The device supports CT summation such as 1.5CB shown in the figure below. In
this example, the phase current for the transformer differential function comes
from a 3 phase CT summation group that summates I11 and I12. The restrain
current is calculated as follows:
•

When operation mode is 3 windings: Ires = (|Í1| + |Í2| + |Í3| + IAddBias) /2

•

When operation mode is 2 windings: Ires = (|Í1| + |Í2| + IAddBias) /2

Where,
Í1 and IAddBias come from the 3 phase CT summation group;
Í1 = Í11 + Í12;
IAddBias = |Í11| + |Í12| - |Í11 + Í12|
Figure 151 - 1.5 CB scheme

IN

CT11

CT12

P71236B

P7/EN M/02-004A

325

Protection and control device

Protection functions

Compensation for tap change (TR3PDIF only)
Transformers equipped with tap changers can adjust the turns ratio to regulate the
secondary voltage. As a result, the nominal voltage is not always the mid-tap
voltage.
Differential comparison is normally based on the mid-tap voltage. Whenever the
tap changer is moved away from the mid-tap position, a differential current
proportional to load will be created. If the tap position is known, this issue can be
compensated by adjusting the LoSet and MF1 settings with the following logic:
•

•

If the TapPos input is invalid:
◦

LoSet' = LoSet + Max(|NomVolTap − MaxVolTap|, |MinVolTap −
NomVolTap|) × TapStep

◦

MF1' = MF1

If the TapPos input is valid:
◦

LoSet' = LoSet

◦

When MaxVolTap < MinVolTap:
MF1' = MF1 × (1 + (NomVolTap – TapPos) × TapStep);

◦

When MinVolTap < MaxVolTap:
MF1' = MF1 × (1 – (NomVolTap – TapPos) × TapStep);

LoSet' and MF1' are then used in the differential formulas instead of LoSet and
MF1.
NOTE:
•

If MF1' < 0.2, or MF1' > 20, the differential function is blocked.

•

If TapStep = 0, no compensation is applied to MF1 and LoSet.

CT supervision
In PowerLogic P7, CTS offers a fast block output with no delay, which can be
linked to the inhibit input of transformer differential element to block the element
when the CT fails.

Transformer differential trip logic
The trip function of the transformer differential is impacted by the techniques of CT
saturation detection and harmonic detection.
When the 2nd or 5th harmonic blocking is asserted or external fault signal is
asserted, the differential protection will be blocked.

Busbar differential (BBPDIF/BZPDIF/BFPTRC/BCPTRC,
ANSI 87B/86)
Overview
The busbar differential protection function is used to help detect and isolate faults
within a busbar system. It operates on the fundamental principle of Kirchhoff’s law
that under normal conditions, the total current entering the busbar equals the total
current leaving it. In contrast, a discrepancy indicates an internal fault within the

P7/EN M/02-004A

331

Protection and control device

Protection functions

protected zone assuming no CT saturation. In such cases, all CBs associated with
the zone are tripped to clear the fault.
Normal condition: I1 = I2 + I3
Fault condition: I1 ≠ I2 + I3
I2
I1
I3

P7124QA

This function is phase-segregated and comprises four elements:
1. Busbar differential (BBPDIF): manages overall settings and the topology. One
BBPDIF instance can manage up to 8 main zones, 32 feeders, and 8
couplers. The total number of zones and bays that can be supported depends
on the number of CT groups that can be instantiated, which depend on the
application level and available flex points. There are also limitations on the
total number of streams when using sampled values. See Function table,
page 27 for details.
2. Busbar zone (BZPDIF): handles individual busbar zones. Zones are defined
by the CT locations of connected feeders and couplers. In complex
topologies, feeders and couplers can be dynamically switched between
zones based on isolator status. The maximum number of zones supported
depends on the application level and available flex points.
3. Busbar feeder (BFPTRC): handles individual feeder connections and logic.
The maximum number of feeders supported depends on the application level
and available flex points.
4. Busbar coupler (BCPTRC): handles individual coupler connections and logic.
The maximum number of couplers supported depends on the application
level and available flex points.
In scenarios where a feeder is connected to two zones, both of which contribute a
portion of the feeder current, the zones must be merged to maintain stability.
Otherwise an unwanted tripping might occur. BBPDIF manages such zone
merges and displays the merging status on the HMI.

Busbar differential (BBPDIF)
Description
The BBPDIF element manages the substation topology and provides settings for
all busbar zones (BZPDIF).
Depending on configuration, up to four conditions are required for a zone trip
signal to be issued:
•

A differential fault is detected within the zone.

•

No CT supervision block/latch-block is active.

•

No phase comparison block is active.

•

The fault is confirmed by the check zone.

When the first three conditions are met, a start signal is asserted for the zone. If
the signal persists for the configured Operate delay and the check zone confirms
the fault, a trip signal is issued for the zone.
NOTE: Trip signals can be latched by setting Lockout mode to On. A latched
trip can be reset using the Reset LO matrix input or control interfaces.

332

P7/EN M/02-004A

Protection functions

Protection and control device

The following diagram shows the characteristics of the busbar differential element.

Bias differential
The bias differential element helps to enhance stability during external faults
where CTs can saturate. It operates by comparing the differential current against a
bias current. A fault is detected only when the differential current exceeds both the
configured Start value and the bias current scaled by the Slope setting.
This method significantly raises the operating threshold during CT saturation,
reducing the risk of false tripping. BBPDIF provides bias differential settings for
main zones and the check zone. Each active main zone (BZPDIF) performs its
own calculations using all associated feeder and coupler currents based on the
global settings in BBPDIF.
The differential and bias currents of main zones are calculated as follows:
n

I diff (t ) = å I k
k =1
n

I bias (t ) = å I k
k =1

E71272A

A fault is detected in a zone if both of the following conditions are met:

I diff (t ) > StrVal
I diff (t ) > K ´ I bias (t )

E71273A

Where K is the Slope setting.

Check zone
The check zone serves as a secondary validation mechanism to help prevent
maloperation due to incorrect isolator status. Unlike main zones, the check zone
includes all feeder currents regardless of their zone assignments and is not
influenced by isolator switching.

P7/EN M/02-004A

333

Protection and control device

Protection functions

NOTE: Even feeders not assigned to any main zone are included in the check
zone unless their OOS input is active.
The check zone (located in BBPDIF) also takes a bias differential approach:
n

I diff _ CZ (t ) = å I k
k =1
n

n

I bias _ CZ (t ) = å I k - å I k
k =1

k =1

E71271A

Check zone operation is immediately issued if both of the following conditions are
met:

I diff _ CZ (t ) > StrVal
I diff _ CZ (t ) > K ´ I bias _ CZ (t )

E71274A

NOTE: The check zone shares the Start value and Slope settings with main
zones.

CT supervision
The inbuilt CT supervision element helps to detect open or shorted CT
connections which can produce a false differential current proportional to the load
current flowing in the circuit monitored by the faulty CT. It also operates when
there is a discrepancy between the isolator position and the primary status. Such
a discrepancy can occur on multiple bus applications where load current is
missing in one zone and added to a different zone causing the supervision to
activate for both zones.
CT supervision also uses the bias differential approach and applies to both main
zones and the check zone. The start value and slope settings for CT supervision
can be configured when CT supervision mode is not Off.
Upon the detection of a faulty CT, this element can indicate the situation, or block/
latch-block the operation of associated main zones and the check zone. A CT
supervision delay setting is provided to delay the indication or block. A Reset CTS
matrix input is provided to reset a latch-block by CT supervision.

Phase comparison
The phase comparison element is an additional stabilization method used only in
main zones to help prevent maloperations caused by external faults with CT
saturation. It compares the phase angles of currents from feeders and couplers
within a zone.
The Phase comparison value setting is a percentage of the rated primary current
for each CT input. When two or more phase currents within a zone exceed the
magnitude defined by Phase comparison value, the phase angles of these
currents are compared. If the maximum angle difference lies between 90° and
175°, then the fault is considered external, and the zone operation is blocked.
A smaller Phase comparison value setting allows smaller CTs to be used but also
reduces the resistive coverage for faults. On a fully loaded circuit, the Phase
comparison value represents the voltage needed to drop below for operation. On
solidly earthed systems, busbar faults are normally very low impedance allowing a
low setting. Low impedance earthed systems where the fault current is above load
will require a higher setting. For systems where the earth fault current is below
load, additional protection will be required to detect busbar earth faults.

334

P7/EN M/02-004A

Protection functions

Protection and control device

Topology processing
The BBPDIF continuously monitors the substation topology to determine the
correct zone configuration for each feeder and coupler. Topology processing
includes active zone assignment and zone merging.

Active zone assignment
Each feeder and coupler side can be connected to up to four zones. The
connections to these zones are determined by isolator status:
•

When only one connection is closed, the feeder/coupler side CT is directly
assigned to the corresponding zone. This zone becomes the active zone for
that feeder/coupler side (unless the zone is merged).

•

When multiple connections are closed, the feeder/coupler side CT is
assigned to the lowest-numbered zone. This lowest-numbered zone becomes
the active zone for that feeder/coupler side (unless the zone is merged).

The active zone of each feeder/coupler side is reflected in the following HMI
outputs of BBPDIF:
•

•

Feeder zones: each digit represents a feeder, and the digit value indicates the
active zone of that feeder.
Example: Feeder 1-8 zones shows Z11200000
◦

Zone 1 is the active zone of feeders 1 and 2 .

◦

Zone 2 is the active zone of feeder 3.

◦

Feeders 4-8 are not connected to any zones.

Coupler LH zones and Coupler RH zones: each digit represents a coupler
side, and the digit value indicates the active zone of that coupler side.
Example: Coupler LH zones shows Z13000000 and Coupler RH zones shows
Z43000000
◦

Zone 1 is the active zone of coupler 1 on the left side, and zone 4 is the
active zone of coupler 1 on the right side.

◦

Zone 3 is the active zone of coupler 2 on both sides.

Zone merging
When two zones are joined by isolators or circuit breakers without a CT, the
current through the connection cannot be measured and both zones are
destabilized by the current flowing in the connection. In these cases, a single zone
must be created by merging the two zones. This merged zone then has a CT on all
connections restoring balance.
When 2 zones are merged, the feeders and coupler sides associated with the
higher number zone are moved to the lower number zone. For example, if zones 1
and 3 are merged, all feeders/coupler sides connected to zone 3 become
connected to zone 1. The feeders/coupler sides will remain connected to zone 3
but their active zone becomes zone 1.
BBPDIF manages zone merging based on the connections of all feeders and
couplers. Zone merging status is continuously updated and displayed on the HMI
as Merged zones, which is an 8-digit string where each digit represents a zone,
and the digit value indicates the zone it is merged into.
Example: Z11105578
•

Zones 2 and 3 are merged to zone 1.

•

Zone 4 is inactive.

•

Zone 6 is merged to zone 5.

•

Zones 7 and 8 are standalone and not merged with any other zone.

Zone merging types
The following types of zone merging can occur:

P7/EN M/02-004A

335

Protection and control device

Protection functions

•

Feeder-based merging: if a feeder is simultaneously connected to multiple
zones, these zones are merged.

•

Coupler-based merging:
◦

Single-side merging: if one side of a coupler is simultaneously connected
to multiple zones, these zones are merged.

◦

Cross-coupler merging: if No CT is configured on both sides of a coupler,
or the Merge matrix input is activated, the zones on either side are
merged. In this case, any coupler CTs are excluded from zone
measurements.

Busbar zone (BZPDIF)
The BZPDIF element defines the main protection zones based on feeder/coupler
connections. The CTs associated with each zone are determined by the BBPDIF
element, which manages the overall topology.
Each zone has a dedicated HMI page displaying key information such as zone
merging status, calculated differential and bias currents, CT supervision status,
and start/operate status.
The BZPDIF element issues the start and operate signals for each zone.
Additionally, a Trip zone matrix input is available to initiate a backtrip. When
activated, this input immediately trips all feeders and couplers connected to a
zone.

Busbar feeder (BFPTRC)
The BFPTRC element provides zone connection settings, status indications, input
signals and tripping logic for each feeder. Current inputs are configured with
PowerLogic Engineering Suite.
Each feeder can be connected to up to four zones, either permanently or flexibly
using isolators. For isolator-based connections, the isolator status can be linked to
the corresponding Con# open matrix inputs to correctly determine Connected
zones and Active zone of a feeder.
NOTE: The Active zone of each feeder is also shown in BBPDIF through
Feeder zones.
In the following example, the feeder can be connected to zone 1 and zone 2 using
DS1 and DS2:

336

•

When DS1 and DS2 are both open, the feeder is not connected to any zones
so Connected zones shows Z________ and Active zone shows None.

•

When DS1 is closed and DS2 is open, the feeder is only connected to zone 1
so Connected zones shows Z1_______ and Active zone shows Zone 1.

•

When DS1 and DS2 are both closed, the feeder is connected to both zones
and zone 2 is merged to zone 1. Connected zones shows Z12______ and
Active zone shows Zone 1.

P7/EN M/02-004A

Protection functions

Protection and control device
Zone1
DS1

Zone2
DS2

CB1

P7124KA

A Trip zone matrix input is available for connection to the feeder CBF signal. When
activated, this input immediately trips all zones that the feeder connects to.
An OOS matrix input is available to mark a feeder as out of service. When this
input is activated, the feeder is excluded from all zone calculations, the Trip zone
input will not trip any zones, and Str/Op signals of this feeder are fixed to False.
The feeder current is set to zero amps with good quality in the check zone
calculation regardless of the measured current and quality.

Busbar coupler (BCPTRC)
The BCPTRC element provides zone connection settings, status indications, input
signals and tripping logic for each coupler. Current inputs are configured with
PowerLogic Engineering Suite.
One side of a coupler can be connected to up to four zones, either permanently or
flexibly using isolators. For isolator-based connections, the isolator status can be
linked to the corresponding LHCon# open or RHCon# open matrix inputs to
correctly determine LH/RH connected zones and LH/RH active zone of a coupler.
NOTE: The LH/RH active zone of each coupler is also shown in BBPDIF
through Coupler LH zones and Coupler RH zones.
In the following topology, if LH_DS1, LH_DS2, RH_DS2 are closed and the left
and right zones of this coupler are not otherwise merged, then the following will
show:
•

LH connected zones: Z12______

•

LH active zone: Z1_______

•

RH connected zones: Z____5___

•

RH active zone: Z____5___

RH_DS1

An OOS matrix input is available to mark a coupler as out of service. When this
input is activated, both sides of the coupler are excluded from all zone
calculations, the Trip zone input will not trip any zones, and Str/Op signals of this
coupler are fixed to False.

Zone extension
If BCPTRC receives a trip command or detects the coupler CB is open (with the
Open matrix input), zone extension will remove coupler CT(s) from all zone
measurements after the configured Zone extension delay. This can help clear end
zone faults or act as a CBF mechanism for the coupler:
•

For an end zone fault between a coupler and its sole CT, the wrong zone will
trip first, but then zone extension can help position the fault to the correct
zone. This is detailed with the topology example below.

•

When the coupler receives a trip signal but the coupler CB fails to trip, zone
extension can destabilize the adjacent zone and cause it to trip to clear the
fault.
NOTE: Set Zone extension delay to match the coupler CB fail time.

In the following topology, an end zone fault between the LH_CT and CB1 is
internal to zone 2 but external to zone 1. As a result only CB1 and CB2 in zone 2
are tripped after which the fault remains.

extension delay timer is started. Once this delay elapses, the current of LH_CT is

Line differential (L2PDIF, ANSI 87L)
Description
The line differential protection function provides fast and selective tripping by
detecting internal faults in the defined zone of transmission lines. It is typically
applied to two-ended lines and lines that include in-zone transformers. The
function receives remote end measurements using standard sampled values (SV),
allowing interoperability with any compliant remote device.

Protection principle
The line differential protection function operates on the principle that the sum of
currents entering and leaving a protection zone should be zero under normal
conditions. The function compares currents measured by current transformers
(CTs) at both ends of the zone. A significant imbalance indicates an internal fault.
The function trips for internal faults inside the zone and blocks tripping for external
faults outside the zone. The following diagrams illustrate the basic principle.
Figure 157 - External fault condition

I1

IED
P7L

Protection zone

Communication link

I2

IED
P7x SV publisher or equivalent

P711H5A

When a fault occurs outside the protection zone, the current entering and leaving
the zone remains balanced, therefore the function does not operate. However,
high through-currents from external faults can saturate the CTs. CT saturation can
create a false differential current large enough to reach the pickup threshold and
cause an unnecessary trip. The function uses a biased differential characteristic
and CT saturation detection algorithm to maintain stability during external faults.

346

P7/EN M/02-004A

Protection functions

Protection and control device

Figure 158 - Internal fault condition

I1

IED

Protection zone

Communication link

P7L

I2

IED
P7x SV publisher or equivalent

P711H4A

When a fault occurs inside the protection zone, the differential current exceeds the
pickup threshold. The function operates and sends a trip command to isolate the
faulted section.
Figure 159 - In-zone transformer
Protection zone

IED

Communication link

P7L

IED
P7x SV publisher or equivalent

P711HGA

If a transformer is in the protection zone, it can introduce phase shifts, ratio
mismatches, and transient conditions such as inrush and overfluxing. These
factors create differential currents if not correctly compensated. The function uses
dedicated logic to compensate for transformer behavior and configuration:
•

Phase correction

•

Zero-sequence current filtering

•

CT ratio correction

•

Inrush detection

•

Overfluxing detection

Communication and synchronization
The line differential protection element operates without proprietary
communications. For correct operation, line differential protection requires time
synchronization and remote measurements. Standardized time synchronization is
available using PTP or IRIG-B and remote measurements are available using
sampled values (SV). Current differential protection also often provides signaling
via proprietary communication for intertripping or status indication. These can be
provided by non-proprietary GOOSE signals.
Using standard communications means the remote IED does not need to be a
matched device. It only needs to provide synchronized SV and GOOSE if
required. If the SV is not provided by a P7 device, it must be IEC 61869-9
compliant (including frequency response) with a clip value of at least 60. The CT
supplying the merging unit (MU) must meet the requirements for the line
differential element. The MU must also meet the CT requirements as detailed in
IEC 61869-13.
To avoid reliance on GPS, the P7L can act as a master clock for the remote
device. The remote device then synchronizes to the P7L. It will then send its
samples synchronized to the P7L, allowing the current differential to work correctly
without proprietary communications.
In a typical installation, both devices are P7L and one would be the PTP master
and the other would be the PTP slave. The master device acts as a boundary
clock and synchronizes the slave either with global time or its local time,

P7/EN M/02-004A

347

Protection and control device

Protection functions

depending on whether the master is receiving a GPS or IRIG-B signal. The PTP
signals travel over the same fibers joining the two ends which also contains the
SV. In this arrangement, both ends receive remote measurements and trip
independently for a line fault. This arrangement is not dependent on GPS for
operation, as the master always synchronizes both ends.
In each P7L, the remote SV signals are mapped to a CT proxy (typically 3ph CT2)
and this is then used as one of the inputs to the line differential element with the
other current coming from the local CTs (typically 3ph CT1).
Since the scheme is non-proprietary, both ends require IEC 61850 configuration to
provide the required standardized signals. This requires each end to have an SCD
file which includes the remote device. This file will include the SV communications
between ends as well as any GOOSE required for signaling or intertripping.
As an alternative to SCD file, a zip package of the preconfigured P7L IED files are
available on www.se.com. The IED files include predefined PTP, SV, and GOOSE
configuration to provide current differential and signalling. Several schemes are
provided, with ends A and B representing the two line ends. To use the templates,
at one end, the corresponding IED file for end A is added based on the application
level of the installed device. Its hardware is then adjusted to match the device and
the required capability instantiated. The same is done for the other end. When the
two devices are connected on the redundant port, the line differential scheme is
active. Signalling is provided by routing signals to the first 16 outputs of FSL1. The
FSL1 outputs are then available for mapping at the remote end.

Operating characteristic
The following diagram shows the operating boundary and the three-slope biased
differential characteristic of line differential protection. The biased differential
characteristic increases the effective pickup level as the restraint current
increases.
Figure 160 - Biased differential characteristic

P7/EN M/02-004A

Protection functions

Protection and control device

The restraint current Ibias is the average of the magnitudes of end currents:

I bias =

(I + I )
1

2

2

E71290A

The maximum bias current on three phases IBiasCros is used for trip threshold
calculation on all three phases.
When Ir1 ≤ IBiasCros ≤ Ir2, the trip threshold Itrip = ILoSet + K1 × (IBiasCros - Ir1)
When IBiasCros > Ir2, the trip threshold Itrip = ILoSet + K1 × (Ir2 - Ir1) + K2 × (IBiasCros - Ir2)
NOTE: Start operation uses a counter and waveform checks to help ensure
correct restraint.

Operating time for teed loads
Many rural feeders have small fuse protected loads tapped off the line within the
protection zone. The teed load is usually a transformer protected by a fuse on the
transformer high-voltage side. The following diagram shows the use case of a teeoff transformer:
Figure 161 - Application of tee-off transformer
87L

P711HFA

When a fault occurs downstream of the fuse, the differential protection would see
differential current, and may trip before the fuse has a chance to blow. This can
cause unnecessary disruption to the rest of the system.
To prevent this, the operating time of the differential element can be time delayed
to grade with the fuse. The time delay can be either definite time (DT) or inverse
time (IDMT). The differential characteristic needs to grade with the fuse. When the
tee-off fuse is small, a DT delay of 150 ms is typically used to allow the fuse to
blow. Larger fuses typically require an extremely inverse curve with 150 ms min op
delay.
For multiple tee-offs, inrush restraint may be required since all transformers will be
energized at the same time. If the level of inrush exceeds the differential operating
curve then restraint is required. This can be achieved by using Normal feeder
mode with external blocking from an IDPHAR element. Alternatively, In-Zone
Transformer feeder mode can be enabled without vector groups or zero sequence
blocking but including the second harmonic restraint.

Phase correction
When a transformer is located inside the protection zone, its vector group
introduces a phase shift between primary and secondary currents. The function
applies phase correction to align the measured currents from both ends for
accurate differential comparison.
Winding 2 vector group (Wndg2) sets the currents used in the differential
element.
•

P7/EN M/02-004A

For even-numbered windings, the appropriate secondary current is selected.

349

Protection and control device

Protection functions

•

For odd-numbered windings, the current is calculated as the difference
between the appropriate secondary currents divided by √3.

(Cph - Bph)/√3

Zero-sequence current filtering
Zero-sequence currents can occur due to in-zone transformer’s winding
configuration (such as delta-wye) or from external ground faults. These currents
can introduce imbalance in the differential current calculation, which can cause
the device to misinterpret the condition as an internal fault. Zero-sequence current
filtering removes the zero-sequence components from the measured phase
currents before comparison.
When zero-sequence filtering is enabled, the filtered current at each end of the
line is calculated as:

I0 =

( I A + I B + IC )
3

I A¢ = I A - I 0

E71291A

Where:
•

IA, IB, IC: Phase current of the corresponding CT input

•

IA': Filtered phase A current of the same CT input

When zero-sequence filtering is disabled, the filtered current is:

I A¢ = I A

E71292A

.

CT ratio correction
CT ratio correction scales the measured secondary currents from CTs at both
ends of the line to a common reference to help ensure accurate differential
comparison.

350

P7/EN M/02-004A

Protection functions

Protection and control device

Line without transformer
CTs at both ends may have different ratios, meaning the measured secondary
currents may not represent the same primary current. The function scales the
measured currents at each end to a common reference based on the rated
primary current of each CT.

Line with transformer in zone
When a transformer is located inside the protection zone, it changes the primary
current magnitude according to its voltage ratio. The function provides additional
compensation by applying a matching factor (MF) for each winding. The matching
factor is a configurable setting and can be calculated using the transformer and
CT ratings.
Matching factor calculation

MFx =

I nom , x ´ 3 ´ Vnom , x
S ref

E71293A

Where:
•

MFx: Matching factor for winding x

•

Vnom,x: Reference voltage of winding x (usually rated or mid tap voltage)

•

Sref: Common reference power of transformer (usually the transformer VA)

•

Inom,x: Rated primary current of line CT x

The current used for line differential protection is:

I A, x¢¢ = MFx ´ I A, x¢

E71294A

Where:
•

IA,x' is the filtered phase A current of CT x.

•

IA,x'' will be used for the final differential and bias current calculation.

Inrush detection
A transformer generates magnetizing inrush current during energization. Inrush
current typically flows into only one transformer winding, which the protection
system might misinterpret as differential current. The inrush detection logic applies
second harmonic blocking and flat spot detection to distinguish inrush current from
fault current and block tripping during inrush conditions.

P7/EN M/02-004A

351

Protection and control device

Protection functions

Second harmonic blocking
Inrush condition are identified by evaluating the ratio of the second harmonic to
the fundamental component of the phase-segregated differential current. If this
ratio exceeds the blocking threshold H2/H1 ratio block value (H2Ratio), the
function identifies the condition as inrush and blocks tripping. If the ratio is below
the threshold, the function considers it a fault and allows tripping. This comparison
is performed only when the fundamental of the differential current exceeds
min(0.1, LoSet).
The H2 blocking (H2Blk) setting enables and configures the second harmonic
blocking. It is normally set to Cross block, unless the transformer comprises three
single phase units with a grounded star connection on the energizing side.
•

H2Blk = Off: Inrush detection is disabled.

•

H2Blk = Cross: The maximum calculated H2 ratio (H2Rat) is compared
against the threshold and blocks all phases.

•

H2Blk = On: Comparison and blocking are performed on a per-phase basis.

Severe internal faults can cause CT saturation, which can increase the second
harmonic ratio above the blocking threshold (H2Rat > H2Ratio). In this condition,
differential protection might fail to trip due to incorrect blocking by second
harmonic. To address this, the function applies three additional methods:
•

Harmonic release: If the maximum sample value of differential current in one
cycle exceeds the harmonic release threshold (HarmRel), inrush detection is
bypassed, and tripping occurs based on the operating characteristic.
NOTE: When HarmRel is set to 0, inrush detection logic works without
checking HarmRel.

•

Phase-based check: Inrush current is expected on all three phases. If the H2
ratio of two phases in all winding phase currents are below 5%, differential
protection will not be blocked by inrush detection logic.

•

Flat spot detection: This logic evaluates the waveform shape of the
differential current to distinguish inrush current from saturated internal fault
current.
NOTE: If any phase of FFT differential current is above LoSet/2, flat spot
detection will be performed.

Flat spot detection logic
A typical characteristic of the inrush current is the presence of a flat spot area in all
three phases at the same time. The following figure shows a typical inrush current:
Figure 162 - Typical inrush current waveform

Diff IA / IB / IC (p.u.)

3

0

0.7

0.8

0.9

Time (s)

1

1.1
P711HDA

The absolute values of the sample differential current in all three phases are
summed to form a flat spot identifier signal. To reduce the influence of DC offset, a
delta filter is applied to the flat spot identifier signal.
352

P7/EN M/02-004A

Protection functions

Protection and control device

The following figure shows a flat spot identifier signal. The threshold Cth divides
the signal into two regions. The blue shaded area is termed the flat spot area and
is used to identify inrush conditions.
Figure 163 - Flat spot detection
Idiff_sum

Ipeak

Cth
B

A

θflat = 75°

B

Flat spot area

t
P711H8A

If the calculated H2 ratio exceeds H2/H1 ratio block value (H2Rat > H2Ratio), the
current is identified as inrush current. In this case, differential protection is blocked
by inrush detection logic.
After inrush block signal BlkH2 is issued:
•

During the next three cycles, the inrush block signal can be reset by flat spot
detection logic or by H2Rat < 0.95H2Ratio;

•

After three cycles, the inrush block signal can only be reset by
H2Rat < 0.95H2Ratio.

Overfluxing detection
Transformer overfluxing can cause high magnetizing currents that resemble
internal faults. The overfluxing detection logic applies fifth harmonic blocking to
distinguish overfluxing from real fault conditions and blocks tripping during
overfluxing. An alarm output is generated if a sustained overfluxing condition is
detected.
Overfluxing introduces significant fifth harmonic content into the differential current
waveform, which is used to identify the abnormal operating condition. The
following figure shows a typical differential current waveform during an overfluxing
event.

P7/EN M/02-004A

353

P711HCA

Fifth harmonic blocking
The H5 blocking (H5Blk) setting enables and configures the fifth harmonic
blocking.
•

H5Blk = Off: Overfluxing detection is disabled.

•

H5Blk = Cross: The maximum calculated H5 ratio (H5Rat) is compared
against the blocking threshold and block all phases.

•

H5Blk = On: Comparison and blocking is performed on a per-phase basis.

Overfluxing condition is identified by evaluating the ratio of the fifth harmonic to
fundamental current component of the phase-segregated differential current. If
this ratio exceeds the blocking threshold H5/H1 ratio block value (H5Ratio), the
function identifies the condition as overfluxing and blocks tripping. If the ratio is
below the threshold, the function considers it a fault and allows tripping.
This comparison is performed only when the differential current is within
reasonable limits:
•

The fundamental of the differential current exceeds min(0.1, LoSet).

•

All instantaneous differential samples are below the harmonic release
threshold Harmonic block limit (HarmRel).
NOTE: When HarmRel is set to 0, the fifth harmonic blocking logic works
without checking HarmRel.

The H5Alarm output signal is issued when the overfluxing condition sustains
beyond the configured time delay H5 alarm time.
NOTE: The alarm operates regardless of the H5Blk setting and HarmRel
setting.

CT saturation detection
During external faults, CTs are likely to saturate due to the slowly decaying DC
offset of the primary current, or remanent flux in the CT core. Appropriate
measures can detect these conditions and prevent false differential tripping.
The external fault detection considers the time to saturation of the CTs.
Immediately after an external fault occurs, the bias increases but the differential

354

P7/EN M/02-004A

Protection functions

Protection and control device

does not increase until saturation occurs. When these conditions are detected the
relay will set the External fault blocking (ExtFltBlk) output, blocking differential
operation.
After the initial heavy saturation the CT will come out of saturation each cycle for a
short period. During this time the differential currents are near zero. If a
subsequent internal fault occurs, this period of balance will no longer occur and
external fault blocking is reset, allowing the fault to be cleared.

Instantaneous high-set unrestrained operation
The line differential protection function includes an independent high-set
differential element that complements the biased differential element. When the
differential current exceeds the HiSet threshold, the phase differential current
function accelerates tripping. In this case, it cannot be blocked by CT saturation
detection logic or any harmonic blocking logic. The level of bias is also ignored as
are the bias time delays. For tee-off transformer applications, the high-set
operation should be disabled by setting HiSet to 0.

CT supervision
CTSSCTR monitors the health of CT and provides a fast block output signal with
no delay, which can be linked to the inhibit input of L2PDIF to block the when the
CT fails.
See Current transformer supervision (CTSSCTR, ANSI 60P/60N), page 564.

Tripping logic
The trip function of the line differential is influenced by the CT saturation detection
and harmonic restraint techniques.
When the second or fifth harmonic blocking is asserted or external fault signal is
asserted, the differential protection will be blocked.
For details, see Block diagram, page 356.

Motor thermal overload (THMPTTR, ANSI 49M)
Description
The motor thermal overload protection function can be applied to help prevent
damage on the stator and rotor against overloading conditions due to balanced
and unbalanced currents.
P7/EN M/02-004A

359

Protection and control device

Protection functions

•

This function incorporates current based stator and rotor thermal levels, using
three-phase RMS currents and sequence currents to reproduce the heating
and cooling of the equipment to be protected.

•

The stator thermal level considers the overheating generated by three-phase
RMS currents and the negative sequence current. The average of the threephase currents is used, and the weighting of negative sequence current is
settable depending on motor characteristic.

•

Multiple time constants are used in stator thermal level calculation. The four
settable constants align with the four machine states. In addition, a four-point
curve can be defined for the overload state.

•

Temperature influence is provided to compensate for the reduction in the
motor thermal limits when the ambient temperature is above the rated
ambient. If ambient temperature measurement is not available, the factor is
ignored.

•

The rotor thermal level takes heating generated from the negative and
positive sequence currents and heating transfer from stator. Two calculation
methods are applied for starting and non-starting motor status separately.

•

The start thermal level of the stator is monitored, and the maximum start level
is memorized. It can be used to automatically adapt the thermal restart inhibit
based on the maximum of the last 5 starts.
NOTE: When this function is added to the device, a Machine page will be
available under Measurements > Bay x on the HMI.

Temperature data can come from RTDs, CLIO modules, or analog GOOSE
subscriptions. Configure the input Ambient temp (TmpAmb) in PowerLogic
Engineering Suite to select the appropriate temperature source for your
application.

Stator thermal level
In the motor thermal protection, an overall thermal model is used.
2

æ I eq ö
t
Dt
H s (t ) = ç ÷ ´
+ H s (t - Dt ) ´
t + Dt
è I s ø t + Dt

E71137A

Where:
•

Hs(t) is the stator thermal level at time t and the output is StaTL;

•

Hs(t - Δt) is the stator thermal level at time (t - Δt);

•

Δt is the calculation interval;

•

t is the time constant;

•

Ieq is the equivalent heating current;

•

Is is the overload current threshold.

Equivalent heating current
The equivalent heating current computes from the average value of the threephase RMS currents and the negative sequence current multiplied by an
adjustable coefficient. When the calculated Ieq is less than 0.05 × FLA, the
equivalent heating current is ignored.
2

360

Temperature influence factor
When ambient temperature is higher than the motor nominal working temperature,
thermal level will increase faster for tripping promptly. When ambient temperature
measurement is unavailable (invalid quality, RTD errors, no temperature source
and so on), the temperature influence factor Fa is set to 1.

Tmax - Tnom
Tmax - Tamb

Fa =

E71140A

Where:
•

Tmax is the maximum motor tolerance temperature from setting TmpMax;

•

Tnom is the nominal motor temperature from setting TmpNom;

•

Tamb is the ambient temperature from input TmpAmb.

When Tnom is higher than the ambient temperature Tamb, the factor is fixed to the
minimum value 1.

Overload current threshold
The overload current threshold Is is determined by the overload setting StrVal and
the temperature factor Fa.

Is =

StrVal ´ FLA
Fa

E71141A

Where:
•

StrVal is the overload threshold;

•

FLA is the full load current;

•

Fa is the temperature influence factor.

Time constant
The time constant t used in thermal level calculation depends on equivalent
current and whether programmable curve function is enabled.
•

P7/EN M/02-004A

When TmACrv = Thermal (programmable curve is disabled)
◦

If Ieq ≤ 0.05 × FLA, the time constant is from setting StpTC;

◦

If 0.05 × FLA < Ieq < Is, the time constant is from setting RunTC;

◦

If Is ≤ Ieq ≤ 2.8 × FLA, the time constant is from setting OlTC;

◦

If Ieq > 2.8 × FLA, the time constant is from setting StrTC.

361

Protection and control device

Protection functions

•

When TmACrv = 4 point curve (four point curve is applied)
The time constant values (TmACrvPt0, TmACrvPt1, TmACrvPt2 and
TmACrvPt3) of the fixed four equivalent heating currents at 1.4 × FLA, 1.6 ×
FLA, 1.8 × FLA, and 2.0 × FLA can be set. The time constant is the linear
interpolation between the fixed points.
This four-point curve provides a flexible tripping characteristic when Ieq is in
the range of Is to 2.8 × FLA.
◦

If Ieq < Is, the time constant is from setting RunTC;

◦

If Is < Ieq < 2.8 × FLA, the time constant is determined by the
programmable curve;

◦

If Ieq > 2.8 × FLA, the time constant is from setting StrTC.

The following two time constant curves are shown as examples.
NOTE: When Is is greater than 1.4 × FLA, TmACrvPt0 is ignored.

Rotor thermal level
The rotor mode setting RotMod is provided to enable or disable the rotor thermal
calculation. When the rotor mode is disabled, the rotor thermal is not calculated
and is equal to the stator thermal. When the rotor mode is enabled, two rotor
thermal levels are used according to the motor status.

Rotor thermal level when Starting
When the motor is in starting status, the rotor thermal level is calculated using the
following formula:

362

R2 = ( RLR - RN ) ´ (2 - s )+RN

E71144A

Where:
•

•

Hr(t - Δt) is the rotor thermal level at time (t - Δt);

•

I1, I2 is the positive and negative sequence phase currents;

•

Tstr is the start torque from setting StrTrq;

•

s is the machine slip (If it is not available, the default value will be 1);

•

ILR is locked rotor current from setting LckRotA;

•

RN is the nominal slip from setting NomSlip;

•

Top is the cold trip time from setting Tcold.

For short time starting, it is reasonable to consider the rotor is adiabatic (no heat
transfer between iron and copper and heat dissipation).

Rotor thermal level when Running and Stopped
When the motor is in running or stopped status, the rotor thermal level is
calculated using the following formula:
