Protection and control device

Protection functions

If the stator thermal level exceeds k and the calculated rotor thermal level is below
k, the rotor thermal level will be set to k.
If the stator thermal level is below k, the rotor thermal level will be equal to the
stator thermal level.

Additional features
Although PowerLogic P7 monitors the rotor and stator thermal levels separately,
the stator thermal level always dominates for resetting due to its higher time
constant. The remaining time to operate, start level calculation and blocking time
use the stator thermal level for calculation.

Remaining time to operate
When the equivalent current exceeds the overload threshold, the operate delay of
the thermal overload protection can be calculated using the following formula:

•

•

Is is the overload current threshold.

When the remaining time to operate is less than the remaining time alarm
threshold RsvVal, a remaining time to operate alarm is issued.

Start thermal level
Start thermal level is recorded when the machine status changes from Stopped to
Starting.

H str = H saftstr - H s prestr

E71152A

Where:
•

Hstr is the calculated start level and the output is StrLev;

•

Hsprestr is the thermal level of the moment when the machine starts;

•

Hsaftstr is the thermal level of the moment when the machine stops.

The output MaxStrLev is the maximum start thermal level of the last five
successful starts. The maximum start thermal level will be retained after a restart.

Blocking time
Starting will be inhibited when the stator thermal level is above the restart level to
prevent over heating during starting.
The blocking time is calculated as follows:

•

Thermal overload logic
The thermal level output ThmLev is the maximum value of stator and rotor thermal
level. The thermal overload function will be blocked if the Inhibit input is active.
When the thermal level exceeds the alarm setting AlmVal, an alarm signal is
issued. If the equivalent current exceeds the overload threshold, the start signal is
issued. When the thermal level reaches 100%, the operate signal issues.
The stator and rotor thermal levels can be reset to 0 via an input RstThm and a
control input RstThmCtrl. If the LimThm input is active, the stator and rotor thermal
levels will be reset to 90% if it is greater than 90% or stays as it is if less than 90%
and the block output is issued.

P7/EN M/02-004A

Protection functions

Protection and control device

Thermal overload (THFPTTR, ANSI 49)
Description
The thermal overload protection can be applied to help prevent the plant damage
caused by operating at temperatures exceeding design limits. Such damage
includes excessive sagging of overhead lines, destruction of the conductor
material, insulation deterioration in cables, overloads in transformers and so on.
A thermal model is used to calculate the thermal level of the plant, which can be
feeder, transformer and so on. The thermal model uses the maximum of the threephase RMS currents, configurable time constants, and optionally the ambient
temperature, as the main inputs to calculate the thermal level.
When the calculated thermal level reaches the configured Thermal alarm value
such as 90%, an alarm is triggered to prompt operator intervention. If operators do
not intervene and the calculated thermal level continues rising to 100%, a trip
signal is issued.
NOTE: When this function is added to the device, a Thermal page will be
available under Measurements > Bay x on the HMI.
Temperature data can come from RTDs, CLIO modules, or analog GOOSE
subscriptions. Configure the input Ambient temp (TmpAmb) in PowerLogic
Engineering Suite to select the appropriate temperature source for your
application.

Plant thermal level
Equivalent heating current
The equivalent heating current is calculated from the maximum value of the threephase RMS currents. When the calculated Ieq(t) is less than 0.05 × FLA, the
equivalent heating current is ignored.

(

I eq = max I ARMS , I BRMS , I CRMS

)

E71187A

Thermal level calculation
When Ieq is above 0.05 × FLA, the thermal level H is calculated using the following
formula:
2

I eq (t )
æ
ö
Dt
Dt
- H (t - Dt ) ´
H ( t ) = H ( t - Dt ) + Fa ´ ç
÷ ´
t + Dt
è StrVal ´ FLA ø t + Dt

E71188A

When Ieq is below 0.05 × FLA, the thermal level H will decay exponentially to zero
and is calculated using the following formula:

H ( t ) = H ( t - Dt ) - H (t - Dt ) ´

Dt
t + Dt

E71190A

Where:

P7/EN M/02-004A

•

H(t) is the thermal level at time t;

•

H(t-∆t) is the thermal level at time(t-Δt);

•

∆t is the calculation interval;

•

t is the thermal time constant at Ieq, as detailed in Time constant, page 370;

•

Fa is the temperature influence factor.

369

Protection and control device

Protection functions

Temperature influence factor
The temperature influence factor Fa is introduced because the thermal withstand
is reduced when the ambient temperature is higher than the plant nominal working
temperature. The factor is calculated using the following formula:

Tmax - Tnom
Tmax - Tamb

Fa =

E71189A

Where:
•

Tmax is the maximum working temperature of the plant from the Max
temperature setting;

•

Tnom is the nominal ambient temperature of the plant from the Nominal
temperature setting;

•

Tamb is the ambient temperature of the plant from input Ambient temp.

The minimum value of Fa is 1. When Tamb is unavailable, invalid, lower than Tnom,
or higher than Tmax, the value of Fa is fixed to 1.

Overload current threshold
The overload current threshold Is is determined by the start value StrVal and the
temperature influence factor Fa. When Ieq is above the overload current threshold,
the function activates and will trip when the thermal level reaches 100%.
NOTE: When the thermal level reaches the configured Thermal alarm value,
which is usually less than 100%, an alarm is triggered to prompt operator
intervention.
The overload current threshold is calculated using the following formula:

Is =

StrVal ´ FLA
Fa

E71141A

Time constant
The time constant t used in the thermal model depends on the equivalent heating
current and the Overload characteristic setting.
NOTE: The thermal overload characteristic is usually applied to plant with
single thermal mass such as an overhead line. The four-point curve overload
characteristic can be applied to items with multiple masses such as the oil,
insulation and windings in a transformer.
•

370

When Overload characteristic = Thermal (programmable curve is disabled):
◦

If Ieq ≤ 0.05 × FLA, the time constant is from setting Cooling time constant;

◦

If 0.05 × FLA < Ieq < Is, the time constant is from setting Running time
constant;

◦

If Ieq ≥ Is, the time constant is from setting Overload time constant.

P7/EN M/02-004A

Protection functions

Protection and control device

•

When setting Overload characteristic = 4 point curve (programmable curve is
enabled), you can set the time constant values (TmACrvPt0, TmACrvPt1,
TmACrvPt2 and TmACrvPt3) of the fixed four equivalent heating currents at
1.4 × FLA, 1.6 × FLA, 1.8 × FLA and 2.0 × FLA. The time constant is the
linear interpolation between the fixed points.
This four-point curve provides a flexible tripping characteristic when Ieq is in
the range of Is to 2.8 × FLA:
◦

If Ieq ≤ 0.05 × FLA, the time constant is from setting Cooling time constant;

◦

If 0.05 × FLA < Ieq < Is, the time constant is from setting Running time
constant;

◦

If Is ≤ Ieq < 2.8 × FLA, the time constant is determined by the
programmable curve;

◦

If Ieq ≥ 2.8 × FLA, the time constant is from setting Overload time constant.

The following two time constant curves are shown as examples:
NOTE: If Is is greater than 1.4 × FLA, TmACrvPt0 is ignored.

Blocking time to restart
After a trip, if the thermal level is above the Restart level setting, restarting will be
blocked for a calculated amount of time to prevent re-tripping.
The blocking time is calculated using the following formula:
NOTE: The following formula is only valid for zero current. When Ieq(t) > 0.05 ×
FLA or the three phase RMS currents are all invalid, the blocking time will be
zero.

•

RsvTL is the Restart level setting;

•

t is the time constant from setting Running time constant.

When H > RsvTL, a blocking signal is issued after a trip.
When H ≤ RsvTL, the blocking time will be zero and no blocking signal is issued
after a trip.

Overtemperature (STMP, ANSI 38/49RTD)
Description
The overtemperature function monitors external temperature and takes protective
actions if the temperature exceeds predefined thresholds.
NOTE: When this function is added to the device, a Temperature page will be
available under Measurements > Bay x on the HMI.
The temperature data can come from RTDs, CLIO modules, or analog GOOSE
subscriptions. Use PowerLogic Engineering Suite to map Temperature to different
temperature sources depending on actual requirements.
The function has two independent set points:
•

Alarm set point.
NOTE: Once temperature reaches this threshold, an alarm signal will be
issued.

•

Tripping set point.
NOTE: If temperature keeps rising and exceeds the operate temperature,
an operate signal will be issued to trip the circuit.

The measurement is valid from -31°C to 201°C. Outside of this range the
measurement is marked as invalid and the Blocked output is active.

(26) The Alm and Trip outputs are updated within 1 s after the temperature input meets the set thresholds. This update interval is independent

P7/EN M/02-004A

Protection functions

Protection and control device

Machine unbalance (NPSPTTR, ANSI 46)
Description
The machine unbalance protection function is applied to help protect motors or
generator rotors from overheating caused by negative sequence currents, which
are generated from unbalanced supply such as single phasing. When the negative
sequence current exceeds the start value, this function uses either an I2t curve or
a thermal curve to calculate the operate delay, after which the element trips. When
the negative sequence current drops below the start value, this function will reset
based on the reset characteristic.
NOTE:
•

To avoid operation during switching or other transient error currents, a
minimum operate delay is introduced for both curves.

•

To ensure the thermal level does not exceed the machine’s I2t
withstanding rating, a maximum operate delay is introduced for both
curves.

Time Constant and I2’
The time constant τ is calculated with the following equation:

t=

MaxThm
StrVal 2 E71198

MaxThm is the Nominal op delay setting.
I2’ is calculated with the following equation:

I 2' =

I2
FLA E71250

FLA is Bay full load amps set in the Bay configuration.
NOTE: The Min op delay and Max op delay settings are converted to I2max and
I2min, respectively.
•

When I2 > I2max, I2max is used in calculations.

•

When StrVal x FLA < I2 < I2min, I2min is used in calculations.

I2t curve
When Operating curve is set to I2t, the thermal level and operate delay are
calculated as below.

Motor monitoring (ZMOT)
Description
The motor monitoring function is available in PowerLogic P7.
380

P7/EN M/02-004A

Protection functions

Protection and control device

Motor state
There are three states for a motor: Stopped, Starting or Running.
PowerLogic P7 motor state is based on the Bay dead signal and the phase
currents.
Figure 174 - Motor state
Stopped

1

1

2

4

Running

Starting
3

P71144A

Criterion for motor in Stopped state
Motor is in Stopped state when the Bay dead signal is detected. ①
Criterion for motor in Starting state
•

Motor will change to the Starting state when motor is in Stopped state and the
Bay dead input changes from active to inactive. ②
NOTE: The maximum current during Starting is recorded as Start current.
The recording omits the first second of the Starting process to avoid
inrush.
If any of the three phase currents exceed the start current setting, motor
remains in the Starting state.

•

Motor will change to the Starting and Reacceleration state, when motor is in
Running state and the following conditions are met sequentially: ④
1. Reacceleration detection function is enabled.
2. Any of the phase-to-phase voltages drop below ReAccVVal for over
100 ms and then rise above ReStoVVal.
3. Any of the three phase currents exceed the start current setting within 5 s.
or,
1. Reacceleration detection function is enabled.
2. ReacAuth input is asserted.
3. Any of the three phase currents exceed the start current setting.

Criterion for motor in Running state
When motor is in Starting state, motor will change to the Running state if the
following conditions are met simultaneously: ③
•

Bay dead input is inactive.

•

All three phase currents are less than 95% of the start current setting for more
than 1 s.
NOTE: The time elapsed when changing from Starting to Running is recorded
as Start time.

Counters and alarms
Each start and reacceleration is counted. The counters can be reset individually. If
the number of starts counter value reaches the set threshold, an alarm is issued.
When motor is in Running or Starting state, the running hours is accumulated. The
running hours can be reset. Two running hours alarms will be asserted when
corresponding alarm is enabled and time exceeds its threshold setting.

Motor start supervision (PMSS, ANSI 48)
Description
The motor start supervision protection function includes two parts:

384

P7/EN M/02-004A

Protection functions

Protection and control device

1. Excessive starting time
After a motor start is detected, before starting supervision time expires, if
current successfully falls below the starting current threshold(27), it means the
motor start successfully, otherwise the operate signal will be issued after
starting time delay.
The excessive starting time delay uses DT or IDMT timer based on the
prolonged start mode setting. The IDMT operation curve is as follows:

t = SetTms

2. Locked rotor during starting
For certain applications, the stall withstand time is less than the starting time.
A Zero speed input is used to indicate the machine is starting and not stalled.
If the current exceeds the starting current threshold(27) and the speed of the
motor is equal to zero (zero speed switch input is active), the Operate signal
will be issued after the stall time.

Protection functions

Locked rotor (JAMPTOC, ANSI 51LR)
Description
Locked rotor protection function is used for motors. If a machine load becomes
jammed when running, it will cause start-up current to flow. As the machine is not
spinning, there is reduced windage, so the rotor rapidly overheats. The protection
will operate more quickly than the thermal element which allows a shorter time to
restart once the jam is cleared.
Following a successful start, if the current exceeds the stalling current threshold
and fails to fall below the threshold before the stall time delay elapsed, then the
operate signal is issued. The element will not operate when the motor is starting
and is protected by separate start protection. Reacceleration can be applied in the
motor monitor function to help prevent operation on voltage drops.

Motor restart inhibit (PMRI, ANSI 66)
The motor restart inhibition includes three functions: starts inhibition, time between
starts and anti-backspin.
The emergency restart effectively removes restart inhibition from thermal
overload, starts inhibition and time between starts inhibition.

Number of starts limitation
Starts inhibition
Any motor has a restriction on the number of starts within a defined period to avoid
overheating of the motor, mainly inside the rotor.
The maximum allowable number of starts per period is an auto-reset inhibit
function which monitors the number of motor starts in the set period MaxStrTmm.
At the end of the supervising period the number of starts is reset.
When the maximum number of starts MaxNumStr within the defined period has
been reached, therefore, the starts number inhibition signal StrNumInh is initiated.
The starts number inhibition signal will be present until the end of the InhTmm or
as long as the counter of the number of starts is above the maximum allowed
starts.

StrRteInh will be present until end of the MaxStrRteTmm.

Anti-backspin
The anti-backspin function is used in applications where the motor may spin
backwards after stopping due to the load such as inclined conveyors. If a start is
attempted the motor will draw excess current for an extended time which will likely
lead to an unsuccessful start. The function is used to detect when the rotor has
completely stopped, in order to allow restarting of the motor.
The anti-backspin protection is initiated by motor Stopped status from motor
monitor function. The restart inhibit signal is issued when the motor is stopped and
is present until the anti-backspin timer AbsTmms expires. Zero speed switch input
is used to reset restart inhibit signal after the time delay ZerSpdTmms.
The remaining time is based on the anti-backspin timer to indicate the left time
before the motor restart is allowed.
Figure 187 - Block diagram of the anti-backspin
Blk

Protection and control device

Protection functions

Emergency restart
Where a motor forms part of an essential process, it is sometimes desirable for it
to continue operation, even under severe overload conditions. This usually means
the motor being subjected to temperatures in excess of its design limits. Even
though this may decrease the life of the motor or burn the motor out, such
conditions may be justified in an emergency.
The emergency restart signal is issued when the motor is in Stopped status and
an emergency restart input is active. The emergency restart input can be enabled
through a binary input, HMI or via the remote communications.
An operation counter is provided to record the number of emergency restarts.
Figure 188 - Block diagram of the emergency restart
Blk
&

Inh

EmgOpCtrl

≥1

EmgcyStr
EmgOpCtrl
MotSt

= Stopped
EmgCounter

+

EmgOpCntRs

R
P71152B

The emergency restart effectively removes the start inhibits from thermal
overload, starts inhibition and time between starts functions. The emergency
restart limits the thermal level under 90% to enable the user to start the motor
immediately. The anti-backspin inhibition cannot be reset by emergency restart.
The general restart inhibition signal is issued.

Voltage check (VCPTUV, ANSI 47)
Description
The voltage check function is normally used in motor applications to help ensure
the machine has the correct voltage before attempting to start. The voltage should
come from a busbar VT for this function to operate. PowerLogic P7 monitors the
input voltage rotation and magnitude to determine both correct phase rotation and
sufficient supply voltage, prior to permitting motor starting.
For a good starting condition, the positive sequence voltage (V1) should be more
than the set threshold. If V1 drops under the threshold, start and operate signals
are generated and can be linked to CBCILO or CBCSWI via Matrix to inhibit CB
closing and block motor start.
If the negative sequence voltage (V2) is more than the threshold, the voltage is
considered to be reverse phase. A reverse indication signal is generated and can
be used to block motor start via matrix if reverse rotation is not allowed.
This function is normally linked to the CB control to inhibit closing and can be
inhibited when the bay is live.

Third harmonic undervoltage (STPTUV, ANSI 27TN)
Description
Neutral displacement protection measures the fundamental frequency voltage
component at the generator star point, and it operates when the fundamental
frequency voltage exceeds the preset value. By applying this principle,
approximately 95% of the stator winding can be protected. In order to help protect
the last 5% of the stator winding, close to the neutral end, third harmonic voltage
measurement can be performed. In STPTUV protection, either the neutral point
third harmonic undervoltage principle, or differential principle based on the
magnitude ratio of the neutral point third harmonic voltage to the third harmonic
differential voltage can be applied. However, differential principle is strongly
recommended. Combined with neutral displacement it provides coverage for
entire stator winding against ground faults.
If the terminal VT is wye-grounded, a third-harmonic measurement at the terminal
(V3T) is available. In this case, both V3T and the neutral third harmonic (V3N)
measured across a neutral grounding transformer are used.
If the terminal VT is wye ungrounded (V3T unavailable), the third harmonic neutral
undervoltage element which uses V3N is applied.

Operation equation of protection
The third harmonic undervoltage differential protection compares geometrically
the third harmonic voltages measured at both neutral (N) and terminal (T) side of
the generator. The algorithm of the third harmonic voltage differential protection is
as follows. The operating equation of the protection is:
Equation 1: Differential mode

V3N / V3HDiff < V3HRatio

Equation 2: Undervoltage mode

V3N < V3NStrVal

Where:
V3HDiff = |V3T - V3N| = |V3G| is the magnitude of total third harmonic voltage
generated by stator.
V3N and V3T are third harmonic phasors with real and imaginary parts.
Equation 1 defines the operate regions of the protection. The third harmonic
differential undervoltage protection operates, and after delay OpDlTmms trips the
faulty generator.
When the fault position is closer to neutral point, |V3N| will decrease, when fault
position is closer to terminal point, |V3N| will increase. As both the differential and
undervoltage elements look for reduced voltage at the neutral end they only work
for faults near the neutral point. This complements neutral displacement protection
which can detect faults in the remainder of the winding.
The advantage of the third harmonic undervoltage differential protection is that
this algorithm offers a protection which is independent of the changing load on the
protected generator.

396

P7/EN M/02-004A

Protection functions

Protection and control device

Whenever the generator is running, there will be a certain amount of third
harmonics present. V3HBlkVal should be set below this value to ensure the
element is only operational when the machine is running.

P7/EN M/02-004A

Interturn overcurrent (ITPDIF, ANSI 50SP)
Description
Introduction of generator inter-turn protection of split phase
For generators with multi-turn stator windings, there is the possibility of a winding
inter-turn fault occurring. Unless such a fault evolves to become a stator ground
fault, it will not otherwise be detected with conventional protection arrangements.
Hydro generators usually involve multi-stator windings with parallel windings.

Generator differential inter-turn protection
One differential scheme using bushing type CTs that is commonly used for interturn protection is shown in Generator inter-turn protection using separate CTs,
page 399. In this scheme the circuits in each phase of the stator winding are split
into two equal groups and the current of each group are compared. A difference in
these currents indicates an unbalance caused by an inter-turn fault. Since there is
normally some current unbalance between windings, the protection is set so that it
will not respond to this normal unbalance but will pick-up for the unbalance caused
by a single turn fault.
In some cases, the generator may run with a faulted turn until it is repaired and
therefore the current pick-up level should be increased to allow operation but still
be able to detect a second fault. The second group current inputs IA2/IB2/IC2 can
be used for this type of application and has independent settings per phase.
Therefore the current setting can be increased on the faulted phase only without
affecting the sensitivity of the protection on the other unfaulted phases. A time
delay is used to prevent operation on CT transient error currents that may occur
during external faults.
The current inputs IA2/IB2/IC2 cannot be used for other protection.
Figure 192 - Generator inter-turn protection using separate CTs

P71171A

The problem of CT transient error currents can be eliminated by using windowtype CBCTs as below in Generator inter-turn protection using separate CTs, page
400.
P7/EN M/02-004A

399

Protection and control device

Protection functions

Figure 193 - Generator inter-turn protection using separate CTs

P71172A

This method of inter-turn protection will detect phase and some ground faults in
the stator winding. However, because of the slow operating time of this protection,
it is common practice to provide standard high speed differential protection for
each phase and separate ground fault protection. If there are main 1 and main 2
protection devices, the IA2/IB2/IC2 inputs could be used for inter-turn protection
on the one device and used for standard differential protection across the
generator in the other device. The HIZPDIF element can be used for this
application without a series resistor.

Inadvertent energization (IEPIOC, ANSI 50/27)
Description
The inadvertent energization protection function is used to detect accidental
energization of an offline generator to prevent potential damage. If the circuit
breaker (CB) is closed while the generator is not running, the generator behaves
like an induction motor. In this condition, the rotor core surface and the rotor
winding slot wedges act as the rotor current conductors. The abnormal rotor
current can cause overheating and mechanical stress.

Generator sequence overview
The function distinguishes between a normal generator shutdown/starting
sequence and a generator shutdown/inadvertent energization sequence. See
Generator normal starting and inadvertent starting, page 403.
•

Generator shutdown
When the generator is de-energized, load current is absent and the generator
voltage collapses to zero over a short period during run-down.

•

Normal generator starting
The generator is started with the coupling CB open. The generator voltage
rises to the correct value. After synchronization with the remote system, the
CB is closed and the generator load current ramps up from zero to the target
level.

•

Inadvertent energization
If the coupling CB is closed while the generator is not running, the generator
voltage instantly equals the bus voltage and current rises when voltage is
applied.

402

P7/EN M/02-004A

P71155B

P7115AA

Activation and operation logic
The function determines the generator state using the generator-side VT. The
busbar VT is not used to avoid unwanted tripping on external bus undervoltages.
The function includes an instantaneous three-phase undervoltage detector based
on phase-to-phase voltages. It picks up when all three phases are below the
undervoltage start value. The function also includes a three-phase overcurrent
detector which picks up when any phase current is above the overcurrent start
value.
Activation
The function becomes active when all three phases are undervoltage and no
phase is overcurrent for the activation delay (T1). This indicates the CB is open
and the generator is de-energized. The activation delay makes the function
insensitive to short voltage sags.
During normal starting with the CB open, as the generator voltage ramps above
the undervoltage reset threshold, the undervoltage detector resets, and the
release delay (T2) starts. The function deactivates at the end of T2 if no
overcurrent occurs.
Operate
If overcurrent is detected during T2, the function operates and remains operated
until overcurrent disappears.
If the VT miniature circuit breaker (MCB) opens, undervoltage pickup can occur
without a real system undervoltage. The function blocks operation on
undervoltage alone and requires overcurrent within the operate window to
operate. This helps ensure correct behavior and avoids unwanted tripping.

P7/EN M/02-004A

The Active output indicates the function is ready to operate if overcurrent occurs. It
asserts when both voltage and current are below their set thresholds for the
activation delay, and it resets after the release delay expires following the reset of
undervoltage and overcurrent detection elements.
The Operate output resets only when overcurrent disappears. Reset has higher
priority than Set in the RS gate.

Overspeed (POVS, ANSI 12)
Description
The overspeed protection function is applied to motor or generator applications.
The rotation speed of the applications is measured using cams mounted on the
rotor, with the detection by means of a proximity sensor.
Overspeed protection is only available when speed detection function is enabled.
Speed data can come from remote speed sensors. Configure the Speed input in
PowerLogic Engineering Suite to select the appropriate speed measurement
source for your application.
Whenever the motor speed reaches the threshold, this function starts and an
instantaneous start signal is issued. If the fault remains active longer than the
operate delay setting, a trip signal is issued.

Underspeed (PZSU, ANSI 14)
Description
Based on the measurement using cams mounted on the rotor, with detection by
means of a proximity sensor, the underspeed protection function is applied to
motor or generator applications to detect the slow-down of rotational speed after
starting, resulting from the mechanical overloads or locked rotor.
The underspeed protection is only available when speed detection function is
enabled. Speed data can come from remote speed sensors. Configure the Speed
input in PowerLogic Engineering Suite to select the appropriate speed
measurement source for your application.
The logic is shown in figure Starting and operating logic of the PZSU, page 407.
Figure 198 - Starting and operating logic of the PZSU
1.05 StrVal
StrVal

0.05 StrVal

Start signal

T
Operate signal
P71170A

The function provides the start signal if the speed measured drops below the
speed threshold after the speed exceeds the set point by 5%, which avoids
tripping when the machine stopped. The underspeed protection is blocked when
the motor speed drops below 5% of threshold, to avoid unwanted tripping when
the motor is switched off.
If the start time of the underspeed protection function exceeds the operate delay
setting, a trip signal is issued.

Zerospeed (ZEROPZSU, ANSI 14)
Description
The zerospeed protection is only available when speed detection function is
enabled. Speed data can come from remote speed sensors. Configure the Speed
input in PowerLogic Engineering Suite to select the appropriate speed
measurement source for your application.
This function is active when the motor speed is less than the threshold, which
means the start signal will be asserted if the speed measured drops below the
speed threshold. This function is inactive when the speed exceeds 105% of the
threshold, which means the start signal will be deasserted.
If the start time of the zerospeed protection function exceeds the operate delay
setting, an operate signal is issued. This is usually used with other functions such
as PMSS or PMRI.
The logic is shown in figure Starting and operating logic of the ZEROPZSU, page
409.

Machine speed (TRTN)
Description
The speed detection function is applied to motor or generator applications where
the rotation speed is measured using cams mounted on the rotor, with detection
by means of proximity detectors. The output from the proximity sensor is a train of
electrical pulses, each pulse corresponds to the detection of an individual cam.
If a given application has R number of cams mounted 360º/R apart on a rotor, the
number of cams R is equal to the number of pulses per rotation of the rotor shaft.
The time interval ΔT (in seconds) between consecutive pulses is used to calculate
the revolutions per minutes (rpm).
The rpm is calculated as follows based on the time interval of the two pulses ΔT
from Binary Input (BI) and the setting of pulse per rotation (R).

ω=

60
60000
=
ΔT(s) x R
ΔT(ms) x R

NOTE: The proximity sensor output pulses are at a voltage level, and the
voltage level is compatible with the logic inputs (24...250 V DC). A logic input
is mapped to a given proximity sensor to perform the speed measurement.
The Mixed Input/Output (MIO) module and Trip Circuit Supervision Input/
Output (TIO) module contain two specific binary input (BI7 and BI8) to detect
the rotational speed. It recognizes the high voltage signal as status 1 with
pulse width greater than 200 µs, and the low voltage signal as status 0 with
pulse width greater than 120 µs.

NOTICE
IMPROPER EQUIPMENT OPERATION
If the counting input BI7 or BI8 on MIO or TIO is selected for speed detection,
both BI7 and BI8 cannot be used by any other protection functions.
Failure to follow instructions can result in equipment damage.

Field failure (FFPDIS, ANSI 40)
Description
Complete loss of excitation may arise as a result of accidental tripping of the
excitation system, an open circuit or short circuit occurring in the excitation DC
circuit, flashover of any slip rings or failure of the excitation power source.
When the excitation of a synchronous motor fails, not enough synchronizing
torque is provided to keep the rotor locked in step with the stator rotating magnetic
field. The machine would then be excited from the power system and hence be
operating as an induction motor.
In generator applications, if the field is lost the generator can slightly overspeed.
Without a field the stator windings will appear as an inductive load fed from the
power system.
These conditions will result in an increasing level of reactive power being drawn
from the power system. If the system cannot supply enough reactive power the
system voltage will drop and the system may become unstable. In order to ensure
fast tripping under these conditions, one of the impedance elements is used with a
short time delay. This can trip the machine quickly to preserve system stability.
This element has a small diameter to prevent tripping under power swinging
conditions. The second impedance element, set with a larger diameter and time
delay, can provide detection of field failure when permanent magnetism partially
maintains the field.

Field failure characteristic
The field failure protection function is based on an offset mho characteristic in the
negative reactance plane. It has two stages. Stage 1 is enabled by default and
stage 2 can be enabled or disabled by a setting Mho2Mod. Stage 1 will normally
detect any field failure but is delayed to avoid operation on power swings. If
required, stage 2 can be enabled to operate faster for a reduced impedance not
affected by power swings. PowerLogic P7 uses positive sequence impedance.
The mho characteristic in the negative reactance region of the R-X plane is shown
in the following figure.

P7/EN M/02-004A

413

Protection and control device

Protection functions

Operating area of stage2

Start logic
The function asserts the start output when the following conditions are
simultaneously present:
•

No inhibit input

•

The positive sequence impedance (with valid quality) falls within the offset
mho circle characteristic

•

The positive sequence current exceeds 10% In

•

The positive sequence voltage exceeds 10% Vn

The function resets the start output providing the positive sequence impedance
falls outside of an expanded offset mho circle, with diameter 105% of the set
threshold.

Operate logic
The function asserts the operate output providing the start output remains for a
duration of operate delay and the inhibit input is not presents.

Underimpedance (UZPDIS, ANSI 21)
Description
The underimpedance protection function is used to detect faults on either side of
the step-up transformer. The transformer is typically star-delta producing a 2-1-1
fault distribution on the generator (delta) side for a phase-phase fault on the grid
(star) side. The impedance is calculated using phase-phase voltage divided by
phase currents to detect this condition.
ZA=VAB/IA
ZB=VBC/IB
ZC=VCA/IC

416

P7/EN M/02-004A

Protection functions

Protection and control device

The start signal is issued when any of the magnitude of the calculated
impedances is under the setting threshold. After the operate delay has elapsed,
the operate signal is issued.
If the phase current is under 2% pu, the related impedances will be set to 0 and
will not trigger start and operate signals.
Two zones are available for the underimpedance protection function. Zone 2 can
be enabled or disabled separately. Stage 1 is normally set to see faults on either
side of the step-up transformer and must grade with the outgoing protection.
Stage 2 can be enabled to provide faster tripping for faults up to the transformer.

Out of step (OOSPPAM, ANSI 78)
Description
Out of step is detected by looking at the impedance loci travelling during an out of
step seen at the device measuring point.
•

In the case of a generator, the locus will travel from right to left.

•

In the case of a synchronous machine, the locus will travel from left to right.

Figure 206 - Out of step characteristic

All polygon characteristics are independent. Independent settings are available for
the respective reactance and resistive reaches.
A Z5” reach is used to distinguish between generator and system slips.
•

Zone 1 is Z5’, R5’, Z5” and R5.

•

Zone 2 is Z5’, R5’, Z5 and R5.

•

Outer zone is Z6’, R6’, Z6 and R6.

All four resistive blinders are parallel, using the common angle setting BldAng.
The measured positive sequence impedance is calculated as:
Z1 = V1/I1
Where,
V1 and I1 are positive sequence voltage and current derived from the measured
phase quantities.

P7/EN M/02-004A

419

Protection and control device

Protection functions

Operating principle
The OOSPPAM detection algorithm is based on measuring the speed of positive
sequence impedance passing through zones.
When the positive sequence impedance is in outer zone, the Outer start is
asserted. When the positive sequence impedance is in zone 2, the Inner start is
asserted.
When measured positive sequence impedance touches the outer zone, a timer is
started.
•

If it takes less than 25 ms from entering outer zone to entering zone 2, the
device will consider this to be a power system fault and not an out of step trip
condition.
NOTE: The timer of 25 ms is a fixed timer in the logic and not accessible.

•

If it takes more than 25 ms from entering outer zone to entering zone 2, Start
will be asserted.
Since zone 1 is in zone 2, so if it takes more than 25 ms from entering outer
zone to entering zone 1, both Start and Zone 1 start will be asserted.

•

If the resistive part of the positive sequence impedance leaves zone 2 with
the same polarity of entry, it is deemed a recoverable swing. No tripping will
be issued.

•

If the resistive part of the positive sequence impedance leaves zone 2 with
the opposite polarity of entering zone 2, an out of step condition is
recognized, counter for zone 2 is started.

•

If the positive sequence impedance leaves zone 2 and resistive part leaves
zone 1 with the opposite polarity of entering zone 1, counter for zone 1 is
started.

When the counter for zone 2 changes from 0 to 1, the reset timer will be started,
the impedance loci travelling occurring before the reset timer exceeds RsDlTmms
will increase the counter. When the reset timer expires, the counter resets.
The Operate will be asserted after OpDlTmms when any of the out of step
counters exceeds the setting for zones.

Overpower (PPDOP/QPDOP, ANSI 32P/32Q)
Description
The overpower protection (PDOP) is used to detect overpower conditions to help
protect power plants. It is applied to limit total power output (active or reactive) or
for reverse power protection of generators depending upon the directional mode.
Reactive overpower can also be used to detect underexcitation.

422

P7/EN M/02-004A

Protection functions

Protection and control device

When the absolute value of power exceeds the set threshold, the Start signal is
issued if the power direction equals the set directional mode for more than 15 ms.
The Operate signal is issued after the set operate delay.

Underpower (PPDUP, ANSI 37P)
Description
The underpower protection (PDUP) is used to detect underpower conditions to
help protect power plants. It can be used to detect loss of load for motors and also
for low forward power shutdown of generators.
When the absolute value of power is below the set threshold, the Start signal is
issued if the power direction equals the set directional mode. The Operate signal
is issued after the set operate delay. By default the BayDead signal will be linked
to the Inhibit input blocking operation when the bay is dead. This matrix link can be
removed if operation is required when the bay is dead.

424

Capacitor bank (ZCAP)
Description
The Capacitor bank monitoring function provides a CB close block to avoid closing
during capacitor discharging. A Block closing signal is provided to ensure reclose
only occurs when the capacitor has been discharged sufficiently. This function
also monitors the hours run of the capacitor bank. It counts the running hours of
the capacitor and raises alarm signals when the hours run reach alarm thresholds.
The status and settings of capacitor bank monitoring can be viewed from the HMI
under Protection > Bay 1 > Capacitor bank > Stage 1.

Capacitor status detection
The function determines the capacitor status based on current flow. If current is
detected on any phase above 0.05 pu, the Capacitor status on the HMI displays
True, indicating the capacitor is closed. If no current is detected, the capacitor
status displays False, indicating the capacitor is open (switched off).

Block closing logic
When the capacitor is switched off, the Block closing (DschBlk) signal is issued to
allow the capacitor to discharge. This signal remains active for the duration of the
configured reclose inhibit time (RecDlTmms) or until the capacitor is switched on
again.
A countdown timer tracks and displays the remaining time until the DschBlk signal
resets, shown as DschBlk reset remaining time (BlkTms) on the HMI. If an inhibit
signal is triggered, BlkTms is reset to the value of RecDlTmms.

Hours run counter and alarm
The hours run counter records and displays how long the capacitor stays in
running status. The counter can be preset using the control output HrRunCntRs.
When the counted running hours reach the configured alarm thresholds
HrRAlm1Val or HrRAlm2Val, the corresponding alarm signals HrRunAlm1,
HrRunAlm2 are asserted. Setting an alarm threshold to zero disables the

426

P7/EN M/02-004A

Protection functions

Protection and control device

corresponding alarm. If an inhibit signal is triggered, both HrRunAlm1 and
HrRunAlm2 alarms are disabled, and the hours run counter is frozen.

Capacitor overvoltage (CAPPTOV, ANSI 59C)
Description
The capacitor overvoltage protection is to protect the capacitor from accelerated
aging or failure due to sustained peak overvoltages. The current across the
capacitor is measured and integrated to calculate the voltage across the capacitor,
and the peak value of the voltage is used to determine the voltage factor (PVF)
applied to the capacitor.
An Alarm output is provided to warn of continuing peak overvoltage. An inverse
time operate stage with a definite time (DT) reset is available for peak overvoltage
above the capacitor continuous withstand. An OpHi output is provided to quickly
trip the capacitor bank when higher peak voltages are present.

PVF calculation
The peak voltage factor (PVF) is calculated from the peak of the integration of the
per unit current over the last cycle. The PVF is calculated for each phase and
indicated in percentage.
The calculated PVF is used to compare against the thresholds AlmVal, StrVal, and
HiSet.

Inverse-time operate characteristic
The capacitor overvoltage function operates with an inverse-time characteristic
based on IEEE C37.99 and IEC 60871-1 recommendations. These give the
withstand time at various voltage factors starting at 110% of rated value as shown
in the following table.
Table 313 - Standard characteristics for the inverse-time characteristic curve
PVF (StrVal = 1.1)

Multiple of StrVal

Withstand time (s)

1

1.182

60

1.272

1.545

1

200%

1.818

0.3

220%

2

0.12

As shown above, operation only occurs above 110% of rated value which
corresponds to the pickup (StrVal) of the curve. The StrVal is then normally set to
110% and TmMult set to 1 to give operating delays in accordance with the
withstand times. The StrVal is a multiple of the FLA (set in Bay config) which is set
to the rated capacitor current. This is calculated from the capacitor rated
capacitance and voltage using the formula:

E71269A

The overvoltage withstand characteristics are based on a daily limit. The function
uses a 24 hour reset timer to accumulate the intermittent overvoltage duration. If
the total time exceeds the allowed daily limit, the function operates. The timer
resets when no pickup is present for 24 hours.
The inverse-time characteristic curve is defined by the following equation:

Protection and control device

The Op output signal is issued when the calculated PVF is above StrVal for longer
than the operate time t(G) based on the inverse-time curve and resets when PVF
is below StrVal. The calculated time to operate (RsvTmm) is displayed on the HMI.
The DropO signal resets automatically after 24 hours or earlier if triggered by a
control input RstDropOCtrl or a matrix-mapped input RstDropO.
The FLA setting comes from Bay info. It is a multiple of the full load current which
is defined in bay configuration.

Capacitor unbalance (UIBCPTOC, ANSI 51C)
Description
The capacitor bank unbalance protection function helps detect capacitor faults in
split protection banks.

432

P7/EN M/02-004A

Protection functions

Protection and control device

A split bank can be either of the following two cases:
•

For double-star capacitor banks, single-phase configurations are used, with
the neutral points connected through a CT that measures the imbalance.
Figure 213 - Double-star capacitor bank

iA

IA

INunb

P7122WA

NOTE: The fundamental frequency component of an unbalance current
INunb is measured on the common neutral connecting the two balanced
parts of double-star capacitor banks.

P7/EN M/02-004A

433

Protection and control device

Protection functions

•

For H-bridge capacitor banks, three-phase configurations are used, with CTs
for imbalance detection located near the midpoint of each phase.
Figure 214 - H-bridge capacitor bank

iA

IA

IAunb

IBunb

ICunb

P7122XA

NOTE: The three-phase unbalance currents (IAunb, IBunb, and ICunb)
are measured at the common points of H-bridge capacitor banks.
Any capacitor mismatch creates a standing unbalance current which needs to be
compensated to provide adequate protection. The displacement angle of the
unbalance current determines which leg of the capacitor is faulted. For internally
fused, externally fused, and fuseless applications, the indications are different.

Compensation
The following compensated currents are the measured values added to the
balance values. A phase current IA is a reference for the compensation. When IA
is less than 0.05 pu, the compensated current values are forced to zero.
NOTE: At switch on (IA > 0.05 pu), compensated values will have a 40 ms
delay for the measurement to avoid the effect of inrush.
•

Double-star mode:
◦

•

Compensated IN

H-bridge mode:
◦

Compensated IA

◦

Compensated IB

◦

Compensated IC

The following balance values are updated whenever the compensation control is
activated. The Compensate control can be activated from the HMI under
Protection > Bay 1 > Capacitor Unbalance > Stage x.
The balance magnitude is determined from the unbalance current magnitude and
the balance angle is based on the inverted angle difference between the reference
current (IA) and the unbalance current.

434

P7/EN M/02-004A

Protection functions

Protection and control device

•

Double-star mode:
◦

•

IN balance and IN balance angle

H-bridge mode:
◦

IA balance and IA balance angle

◦

IB balance and IB balance angle

◦

IC balance and IC balance angle

NOTE: Balance values are initially zero. After compensation, they are
memorized through power cycles.
The compensated current used by the element is the measured unbalance current
added to the stored balance current, and the Compensated Ix angle (φx) which is
synchronized using the phase current IA as a reference are shown in the following
figure.

Protection and control device

Protection functions

When Compensated Ix exceeds
•

AlmVal for longer than AlmDlTmms (Alarm delay), OpLo alarm signal is
issued.

•

StrVal, Str is immediately issued. If Str is active for longer than OpDlTmms
(Operate delay), Op output signal is issued.

•

HiSet for longer than HiDlTmms (High set op delay), OpHi output signal is
issued.

Directional logic
The indication of affected phase and direction for the location of the faulty element
is based on the compensated current angle at the time of operation.
•

Double-star mode: The indication is based on the angle of the compensated
current relative to the reference current.
◦

A_forward is indicated if the angle is 0° ± 30° of IA. A_backward occurs
when the angle is 180° ± 30°.

◦

B_forward is 240° ± 30°, and B_backward is 60° ± 30° for positive phase
rotation.

◦

C_forward is 120° ± 30°, and C_backward is 300° ± 30°.

441

Protection and control device

Measurement functions

Measurement functions
CT/VT groups and mapping
CT group
Description
The CT group is a virtual data model, comprised of measurement function blocks
(RMS, fundamental and sequence), supervision (CTSSCTR) and CT model
(PHTCTT or TCTS).
The CT group maps the physical channels from the Low Power Analog Input
(LPAI) module or the digital channels from SV subscriptions to the group’s current
channels for use in installed application functions. See CT/VT matrix, page 463 for
more information.
On the HMI, the CT group measurements can be viewed under General > Bay x
> 3ph CT x or 1ph CT x > Measurements. This includes tabs for RMS and
fundamental measurements as well as settings.
NOTE: 3ph CT 1 measurements are used as the inputs for Measurements >
Bay x > General.
Figure 220 - Function block of 3ph CT group

CTSSCTR

AMSQI

LPAI (5CT/6CT) or
LSVS (SV channels)

PHTCTT

VECAMMXU

RMSAMMXU

P711E1B

Figure 221 - Function block of 1ph CT group

VECAXMMXU
LPAI (5CT/6CT) or
LSVS (SV channels)

TCTS
RMSAXMMXU

P711ETA

Each bay can have multiple CT groups. Two types of CT group are available.
•

442

3ph CT group is used to handle multiple phases with or without neutral
current signals.

P7/EN M/02-004A

Measurement functions

Protection and control device

•

1ph CT group is used to handle single phase current signal, usually used for
neutral measurements using a toroidal CT. An angle correction can be
applied to compensate for the external CT angle error for functions like the
ground admittance protection. It does not have CTSSCTR or AMSQI
functions.
NOTE: From the HMI or PowerLogic Engineering Suite, the polarity for both
types of CT group can be set as Standard or Opposite in case of incorrect
wiring.

Rating
For the CT groups mapped to physical channels, the LPAI’s input is a secondary
value, the LPAI’s output is a PU value according to the secondary configuration in
the associated PHTCTT or TCTS. Primary values are calculated based on the PU
value and primary rating. PU values and primary values are available for use in
protection and measurement functions.
For the CT groups mapped to SV channels, the PU value is calculated based on
the primary rating in the associated PHTCTT or TCTS and the primary value,
which is received from SV subscriptions.

Connection type
Different connection types are given for the different CT connections available:
•

3ph (IA IB IC) should be used with three phase CTs.

•

3ph+N (IA IB IC IN) should be used with three phase CTs which are then
paralleled to provide the neutral current in a residual (Holmgreen) connection.

•

2ph (IA IC) is for two phase CTs.

•

2ph+N (IA IC IN) is for when two phase and ground relays are being replaced
which use a residual connection and the wiring is difficult to change.

CBCTs should not be used in 3ph CT groups. Depending upon which signals are
available, the module will provide all valid measurements. Invalid measurements
will be indicated via quality.
The connected phase or neutral can be selected for 1ph CT groups. It will be
marked as valid and other signals marked as invalid. Connected functions will
check the quality and will be blocked if the required signals are not available.
Detailed quality output is shown in following truth tables.
NOTE: In the table, Measured or Derived indicates the quality of the signal is
GOOD. Measured means that the output signal is directly calculated from the
input signal, Derived means that the output signal is calculated from multiple
measured signals.

CT application instance
A typical CT application scenario is shown in the figure below. To implement this
application, two 3ph CT groups and one 1ph CT group are required. The first 3ph
CT group corresponds to the three-phase current of generator terminal, the
connection type is 3ph+N. The second 3ph CT group corresponds to the threephase current of the generator star point, the connection type is 3ph. The 1ph CT
group corresponds to the residual current of generator terminal and the
connection type is Nph.

x:

The first slot used by the CT/VT analog module takes 2 slots. For example, if the analog
module is fitted in slots B & C then x9 becomes B9.

y:

The CT/VT analog module takes 2 slots. For example, if the analog module is fitted in
slots D & E then y9 becomes D9.

Phase current
PowerLogic P7 measures the fundamental and RMS values of phase currents.
The calculation is performed as follows and is computed over a frequency tracked
cycle with n=48.

Neutral current
IN can be measured directly if the neutral input is provided. Otherwise, the neutral
current is calculated by the vector sum of the three-phase currents.

VT group
Description
The VT group consists of measurement function blocks (RMS, fundamental and
sequence), voltage transformer supervision (VTSSVTR) and VT model (PHTVTT
or TVTS). The VT group supports both phase-to-ground and phase-to-phase
inputs and measurements.
The VT group maps the physical channels from the Low Power Analog Input
(LPAI) module or the digital channels from SV subscriptions to group’s voltage
which are used by the installed application functions. See CT/VT matrix, page 463
for more information.
On the HMI, the VT group measurements can be viewed under General > Bay x >
3ph VT x or 1ph VT x > Measurements. This includes tabs for RMS and
fundamental measurements as well as settings.
NOTE: 3ph VT 1 measurements are used as the inputs for Measurements >
Bay x > General.

448

P7/EN M/02-004A

Measurement functions

Protection and control device

Figure 223 - Function block of 3ph VT group

VTSSVTR

VMSQI

LPAI (5CT/6CT) or
LSVS (SV channels)

PHTVTT

VECVMMXU

P711E2B

Figure 224 - Function block of 1ph VT group

VECVXMMXU
LPAI (5CT/6CT) or
LSVS (SV channels)

TVTS
RMSVXMMXU

P711EUA

Each bay can have various numbers of VT groups dependent on the application
level. Two types of VT group are available.
•

3ph VT group is used to handle multiple phases with or without neutral
voltage signals.

•

1ph VT group is used to handle single phase voltage signals. It does not have
supervision or sequence measurements.

Rating
For the VT groups mapped to physical channels, the LPAI’s input is a secondary
value, the LPAI’s output is a PU value according to the secondary configuration in
PHTVTT or TVTS. Primary values are calculated based on the PU value and
primary rating. PU values and primary values are available for use in protection
and measurement functions.
For the VT groups mapped to SV channels, the PU value is calculated based on
the primary rating in the associated PHTVTT or TVTS and the primary value,
which is received from SV subscriptions.

Connection type
Different connection types are given for the different VT connections available:

P7/EN M/02-004A

•

3ph (VA VB VC) should be used with three single phase VTs or a five limb VT.

•

3ph+N (VA VB VC VN) should be used with VTs with one star and one broken
delta secondary.

•

2pp (VAB VCB) is for two winding or vee connected VTs.

449

Protection and control device

Measurement functions

•

2pp+N (VAB VCB VN) is for two winding VTs when a separate neutral
measurement is available such as a generator star point.

The connected phase, phase-to-phase or neutral can be selected for 1ph VT
groups. It will be marked as valid and other signals marked as invalid. Connected
functions will check the quality and will be blocked if the required signals are not
available. Detailed quality output is shown in following truth tables.
NOTE: In the table, the Measured and Derived symbols indicate the quality of
the signal is GOOD, Measured means that the output signal is directly
calculated from the input signal, Derived means that the output signal is
calculated from multiple Measured signals.

A typical VT application scenario is shown in the figure below. To implement this
application, one 3ph VT group and one 1ph VT group are required. The 3ph VT
group corresponds to the 3ph voltage and the open-delta voltage at the generator
terminal, and the connection type is 3ph+N. The 1ph VT group corresponds to the
neutral voltage at the generator neutral point and the connection type is Nph.

x:

The first slot used by the CT/VT analog module takes 2 slots. For example, if the analog

Phase voltages
PowerLogic P7 measures the fundamental values and RMS values of phase-tophase voltages and phase-to-ground voltages.

P7/EN M/02-004A

451

Protection and control device

Measurement functions

The calculation is performed as follows and is computed over a frequency tracked
cycle with n=48.
n

V RMS =

1 ∑V2
n k=1 k

E71184A

×

Neutral voltage
VN can be measured directly if the main transformer includes a broken delta
secondary. Alternatively, VN is calculated by taking the internal sum of the three
phase-to-ground voltages.
VN = VA + VB + VC

Sequence voltage
The sequence voltage value is based on three-phase voltages connected to the
VT.

P7/EN M/02-004A

455

Protection and control device

Measurement functions

3 phase CT summation
Description
The 3 phase CT summation function is a phase-by-phase summing function for up
to four 3ph CT groups. It performs the summation based on the polarity of each
selected CT group and calculates an additional bias current to help ensure
differential protection behaves correctly. The outputs of a 3 phase CT summation
group are similar to that of a 3ph CT group and can be used as normal current
input for current-related functions.
To define a 3 phase CT summation group, use PowerLogic Engineering Suite to
select the Connection type, specify the desired CT groups and their polarities
under Bay x configuration > Three phase CTs > 3ph CT summation x >
Configuration.
The 3 phase CT summation group consists of the measurement function blocks
(RMS, fundamental and sequence) and the CT summation model (CSTCTT).
NOTE: The sequence current accuracy of a 3 phase CT summation group
degrades proportionally with the number of CT groups involved. For example,
summimg two CT groups typically results in an accuracy of ±1% (twice that of
a normal CT group).
Figure 226 - Function block diagram of 3ph 3 phase CT summation group
The CSTCTT calculates summated values, biased currents and other analog
values.
The measurement function blocks, which include ACSMSQI,VECACSMMXU, and
RMSACSMMXU, output all the calculated analog values through IEC 61850.
3ph CT summation group

3ph CT1 group
CTInfo

CT1Info
CT2Info
ACSMSQI

CT3Info
3ph CT2 group
CT4Info
CTInfo

CSTCTT

VECACSMMXU

RMSACSMMXU

P711ESA

CT summation logic
The CT summation is performed based on the connection type and the polarity of
each selected CT group.
The connection type can be any of the following, which dictates the currents to be
summated:
•

CT1 + CT2: I1 + I2

•

CT1 + CT2 + CT3: I1 + I2 + I3

•

CT1 + CT2 + CT3 + CT4: I1 + I2 + I3 + I4

The summated current Isum is calculated as follows, using CT1 + CT2 + CT3 +
CT4 and IA as the example:
IAsum = IA1 × Polx1 + IA2 × Polx2 + IA3 × Polx3 + IA4 × Polx4
Where,
Pol is dictated by the CT polarity. It equals 1 for standard polarity and -1 for
opposite polarity.
NOTE: Before the summation, all currents from CT2, CT3, and CT4 are
scaled based on the primary rating of the Reference current (CT1).

456

P7/EN M/02-004A

Measurement functions

Protection and control device

Additional bias current
For TR3PDIF, PHPDIF, and RGFPDIF that use the 3 phase CT summation group
as input, an additional bias current Iaddbias is calculated on a per-phase basis to
help these functions work normally in some fault conditions, such as when the
fault flows from one summated CT to another one within the same 3 phase CT
summation group.
The additional bias current is calculated as follows:
Iaddbias = |I1| + |I2| + |I3| + |I4| − |I1 + I2 + I3 + I4|

Protection and control device

Measurement functions

VT switching
Description
The VT switching function allows switching between two VT groups. A delay time
is provided to help ensure that the linked protection functions are not responding
to a switching transient. The VT switching group consists of the measurement
function blocks (MMXUs) and the VT switching model (VSTVTT/VSTVTS).
Figure 227 - Function block diagram of 3ph VT switching group
VSTVTT switches the analog values (FFT, RMS, sequence), and contains the
primary rated voltage value (VRtg). VECVVSMMXU, RMSVVSMMXU and
VVSMSQI output all analog values through IEC 61850.
3ph VT switching group

3ph VT1 group

VECVVSMMXU

3ph VT2 group

RMSVVSMMXU

P711EQA

Figure 228 - Function block diagram of 1ph VT switching group
VSTVTS switches the analog values (FFT and RMS), and contains the primary
rated voltage value (VRtg). VECVVSXMMXU and RMSVVSXMMXU output all
analog values through IEC 61850.
1ph VT switching group

VT2Info
1ph VT2 group

VT2active

RMSVVSXMMXU

VTInfo
P711ERA

VT switching logic
The VTNum output signal indicates which VT is selected. VT1 is selected as
default.
The primary voltage (VRtg) of VT1 and VT2 must be the same. When the primary
voltage of VT2 is different from that of VT1, the Blk output signal is issued which
makes all measurements (FFT, RMS, sequence, 3rd harmonic value) invalid.
When VT switching is successfully performed, the output signals will be marked
invalid for 80 ms, during which time the linked functions will be blocked.

Frequency tracking
When the voltage source of frequency tracking is a VT switching group, during the
switching delay time, frequency tracking will be frozen with quality questionable.
When the VT switching group Blk output signal is asserted, frequency tracking will
not track voltages.

458

P7/EN M/02-004A

Measurement functions

Protection and control device

Angle reference
When the voltage source of the reference angle is a VT switching group, during
the switching delay time or when the Blk output signal is issued, the reference
angle will depend on currents instead of voltage.

Phase RMS voltage

Virtual VT
Description
The virtual VT function provides corrected phase-to-phase voltages in applications
where only the voltages on one side of a transformer are available, but protection
or control functions require voltages from the opposite side. This function adjusts
both the magnitude and phase angle based on user configurations so that the
output voltages appear as if a VT were installed at the desired location.
This function uses a 3ph VT group as input source. The corrected output voltages
can be used by any voltage-related functions.
NOTE: This function does not output valid phase-to-neutral voltages, as the
neutral voltage at the virtual VT side might not be zero.
The virtual VT group consists of the measurement function blocks (RMS,
fundamental and sequence) and the virtual VT model (VIRTVTT).
Figure 229 - Function block diagram of 3ph virtual VT group
3ph virtual VT group
3ph VT group
VTInfo

VIRTVTT

VVIRMSQI

VTInfo
MF
VirVTLoc
VectGrp
VRtg

VECVVIRMMXU

RMSVVIRMMXU

P711EVA

Virtual VT logic
The voltage correction includes phase correction and VT ratio correction.
Phase correction

460

P7/EN M/02-004A

Measurement functions

Protection and control device

A phase shift between the source VT and the virtual VT might be introduced by the
transformer conditions. Phase correction compensates for this shift by adjusting
the source voltages as follows:
•

For even vector groups, each corrected voltage is derived directly from the
appropriate source voltage.

•

For odd vector groups, each corrected voltage is calculated from the
difference between two appropriate source voltages divided by √3.

The phase-corrected voltages are then used in VT ratio correction to generate the
final outputs.
The following table shows all the possible cases:
Table 369 - Phase correction formulas
Transformer vector group

Virtual VT location

Correction formula

AB' = (ab – bc) / √3
BC' = (bc – ca) / √3
CA' = (ca – ab) / √3

5

HV

7

HV

LV

HV

6

LV

HV

2

LV

2

HV

10

LV

HV

0

BC' = (ab – bc) / √3

AB' = (bc – ca) / √3
BC' = (ca – ab) / √3
CA' = (ab – bc) / √3
AB' = (ab – ca) / √3
BC' = (bc – ab) / √3

VT ratio correction
Unmatched or slightly erroneous VT or transformer ratios might introduce
magnitude differences between the LV and HV sides of a transformer. The
differences are usually small, but can accumulate and become significant enough
to affect the accuracy of protection and control functions.
Such magnitude differences can be compensated by the Matching factor, which is
calculated as follows:

P7/EN M/02-004A

461

Protection and control device

Measurement functions

MF =

Vrtg _ src
Vtx _ src

´

Vtx _ vir
Vrtg _ vir

P711EWA

Where,
Vrtg_src is the primary rating of the source VT;
Vtx_src is the transformer tap voltage at the source VT side;
Vtx_vir is the transformer tap voltage at the virtual VT side;
Vrtg_vir is the Rated voltage setting for the virtual VT.
In the following example, Vrtg_src = 33 kV, Vtx_src = 32.5 kV, Vtx_vir = 11 kV, Vrtg_vir =
11 kV, so MF = 1.015.

33 kV/100 V
Source VT

32.5 kV/11 kV
Transformer

11 kV/100 V
Virtual VT

P711EXA

The final output voltages are obtained by multiplying the phase-corrected voltages
by this Matching factor.

NOTE: All phase-to-neutral voltage outputs in the following tables are fixed at
zero and marked as invalid.

CT/VT matrix
Before any analog signal from the CT/VT module(s) can be used, the related
hardware or Sampled Value (SV) channels need to be mapped to the virtual
channels of the CT/VT group(s). This can be done with PowerLogic Engineering
Suite only. The mapping relationship should match the external wiring of
substation CTs and VTs to the different physical inputs of the CT/VT module.
Configure different physical analog channels to different groups.
NOTE: The value displayed on Setting parameters, page 446 for NbChIA/
NbChIB/NbChIC/NbChIN/NbChIx and the value displayed on Setting
parameters, page 454 for NbChV1/NbChV2/NbChV3/NbChV4/NbChVx
corresponds to the IEC 61850 data model. This value reflects the
communication-model representation of channel associations, independent of
the local configuration performed in PowerLogic Engineering Suite.
In PowerLogic Engineering Suite, the mapping of the analog channel is performed
within the CT and VT group. For example, the mapping of the three phase CTs can
be configured under Bay x configuration > Three phase CTs > 3ph CTx >
Configuration. The signals are mapped by reference to the instance number of
the module and the signal of the voltage or current input on the board; for
example, 6CT1 VT1.
NOTE: In PowerLogic Engineering Suite, these parameters are configured
using a selection matrix. The matrix defines the relationship between physical
CT/VT input channels or SV channels and the virtual channels of each CT/VT
group.
NOTE: If SV subscriptions are configured:
•

The CT/VT groups can be mapped to the digital channels of received SVs
with PowerLogic Engineering Suite. The group settings of Rated current
or Rated voltage must match the received SVs.

•

The mix of channels from different SV streams, or the mix of physical
acquisitions and SV channels are not allowed within the same three
phase CT/VT group.

•

The start time of all protection functions will increase by 1-10 ms.

On the HMI, the connected hardware/SV channel is indicated in the CT/VT group
settings. For example, the connected hardware channel of three phase VT 1 can
be viewed under > General > Bay 1 > 3ph VT 1 > Measurements > Settings.

P7/EN M/02-004A

463

Protection and control device

Measurement functions

NOTE: On the HMI, the connected hardware or Sampled Value (SV) source is
shown as the slot and channel for local CT/VT inputs, or as the SV source
associated with corresponding current or voltage channel. This display reflects
the actual acquisition point rather than the IEC 61850 representation.
The following figure illustrates a application scenario for the configuration:
•

The CT1 sensor connection type should be set as 3ph (IA IB IC), mapping IA
with PHCT1, IB with PHCT2, IC with PHCT3, single CT is mapped with
PHCT4.

•

The VT1 package connection type should be set as VA VB VC, mapping VA/
VAB with VT1, VB/VCB with VT2, VC with VT3.

Sampled values
Sampled value publication
Description
The SV publishing function is available on all P7S devices which can be
configured to publish Sampled Values (SV) in accordance with IEC 61869-9 class
d standard. Other devices in the PowerLogic P7 range may also be able to publish
SV depending upon their main application and application level.
When configured as an SV publisher, PowerLogic P7 converts analog current/
voltage signals from conventional CT/VTs into SV frames. The device time-tags

464

P7/EN M/02-004A

Measurement functions

Protection and control device

each sample and transmits the SV stream over Ethernet to subscribing IEDs for
protection, control, measurement, and monitoring purposes.
Figure 231 - SV publication on PowerLogic P7

VT

Ethernet link

PowerLogic P7

CT

SV subscriber

Sampled Values

Time synchronization

Optional time synchronization
M71117B

SV publisher properties
SV acquisition
The multicast sampled value control block (MSVCB) defines the sampling
parameters and includes the dataset to be published, which specifies a selection
of current/voltage channels with their individual quality. The number of SV streams
and publishing rate are dependent on the device type.
At every sampling instant, the MSVCB collects the values of all current/voltage
channels in the dataset and places them into an Application Service Data Unit
(ASDU). A published SV frame can include up to six ASDUs.

SV synchronization
When an SV subscriber compares SV from different publishers, or compares SV
with local CT/VT acquisitions, all the SV must be synchronized. PowerLogic P7
uses an external IEEE 1588 (PTP) or IRIG-B clock as the synchronization
reference. Other methods of time synchronization are not sufficiently accurate.
NOTE: PowerLogic P7 can also act as the PTP time source for SV
synchronization. See PTP master, page 171 for details.
Each SV sample or ASDU is tagged with:
•

SmpCnt – an incremental value indicating the sampling instant

•

SmpSynch – the synchronization status of the ASDU

The synchronization status can be:
•

Globally synchronized – the ASDU is synchronized with a global area clock
that meets the accuracy requirement.

•

Locally synchronized – the ASDU is synchronized with a local area clock.

•

Not synchronized – the ASDU is not synchronized with any of the clocks
mentioned above, or the publishing device has lost the signal from a qualified
global or local area clock for longer than the holdover time.
NOTE:
◦

During the holdover time, which starts after the external clock is lost,
the device can maintain the time accuracy of the time-tagging for the
published samples.

◦

When the device switches to a new source clock, the published SVs
are re-synchronized to this new source. During the transition, there
might be a short period when the SVs are tagged as not synchronized.

Each subscribing device evaluates the synchronization status of received SV data
and decides whether to accept the data as valid. See Relative synchronization,
page 468 for details.

P7/EN M/02-004A

465

Protection and control device

Measurement functions

SV transmission
PowerLogic P7 publishes SV in one or more multicast streams over Ethernet.
Each stream corresponds to a configured MSVCB published by the MU01 logical
device which follows the associated bay behavior. To publish test SV, it is
necessary to put the bay into test mode under Control > Bay x > Bay mode of
the HMI.
PowerLogic P7 publishes SV over its redundant interface (Port 2/3).

Available SV variants
The SV variants that can be published are dependent on the main application and
application level:
Application

Available SV variants

Level 0 of SAMU application and level 1/2 of the
following applications:

•

F4800S2I0-24U0-24

•

F4000S1I4U4(28)

•

Motor

•

F4800S1I4U4

•

Generator

•

F5760S1I4U4(29)

•

Feeder

•

Transformer

•

Busbar

•

Line differential

•

Bay controller
•

F4800S2I0-24U0-24

•

F4000S1I4U4(28)

•

F4800S1I4U4

•

F5760S1I4U4(29)

•

F14400S6I0-8U0-8

Level 1/2 of SAMU application

•

F is the digital output sample rate expressed in samples per second.

•

S is the number of ASDUs (samples) contained in an SV frame.

•

I is the number of current quantities contained in each ASDU.

•

U is the number of voltage quantities contained in each ASDU.

•

F4000S1I4U4, F4800S1I4U4, and F5760S1I4U4 variants are provided for
backward compatibility in accordance with IEC 61869-9.

SV and local sampling rate rules
1. If a device subscribes to SV, the publishing rate must match the subscription
rate which also sets the local sampling rate.
2. If a device publishes multiple SV streams, all streams must use the same
sample rate with one exception: on level 1/2 P7S devices, one stream can be
14400 Hz providing any other published streams are 4800 Hz.
3. If no SV subscription is configured, the local sampling rate follows the
publishing rate with the following exceptions:
•

If one CT/VT board is fitted and all published streams are 4800 Hz, the
local sampling rate is 14400 Hz.

•

On level 1/2 P7S devices, if one published stream is 14400 Hz (all other
published streams are 4800 Hz), the local sampling rate is 14400 Hz.

4. If no SV publishing or subscription is configured:
•

The local sampling rate is 14400 Hz with one CT/VT board.

•

The local sampling rate is 4800 Hz with two or more CT/VT boards.

(28) Only for 50 Hz systems
(29) Only for 60 Hz systems

466

are also required for configuring new MSVCBs.

MSVCB parameters, see PowerLogic P7 Communication User Manual.

SV publishing supervision (LSVP)
The LSVP is used to monitor and diagnose SV publications. One SV publication
corresponds to one LSVP instance.
After configuring SV publications, the following information can be viewed on an
IEC 61850 client connected to the device:
NOTE: Some of the information below can also be viewed on the HMI under
