Indicates internal, local or global sync

Sampled value subscription
Description
PowerLogic P7 can acquire current/voltage signals from the installed
CT/VT boards (local acquisition), subscribed SV streams from a remote device
(remote acquisition), or a mix of both. Both local and remote acquisitions are

P7/EN M/02-004A

467

Protection and control device

Measurement functions

processed in the same way by the device’s protection, control, measurement, and
monitoring functions.
Remote acquisition offers flexibility and cost-savings in substation deployment. By
using digitalized current/voltage signals, the need for complex copper wirings and
multiple primary sensors is greatly reduced. However, this approach also
introduces higher requirements for the network, since a link or switch failure can
expose the system to a great outage. Redundancy is typically used to help
maintain system availability.
Figure 232 - SV subscription on PowerLogic P7

VT

Ethernet link

CT

Merging unit

Time synchronization

PowerLogic P7

Sampled Values

Time synchronization
P711EID

SV subscriber properties
Using Port 2/3, the device can subscribe to the following SV variants:
•

F4000S1I4U4 (only for 50 Hz systems)

•

F5760S1I4U4 (only for 60 Hz systems)

•

F4800S1I4U4

•

F4800S2I0-24U0-24 (the sum of current and voltage channels cannot exceed
24)
NOTE: All SV streams that a device subscribes to must use the same sample
rate. For more information about SV rate rules, see SV and local sampling rate
rules, page 466.

A maximum of six streams of up to eight channels can be used. Each CT/VT
board and SV subscription is counted as a stream. More streams or larger
streams can be used providing the following formula is met:
Channels + 6 × CTVT/SV streams ≤ 90
Where,
•

A channel is a physical or SV channel (current or voltage measurement). The
total channels = number of CT/VT boards × 9 + all subscribed SV channels.

•

CTVT/SV streams is the total number of CT/VT boards plus the total number
of subscribed SV streams.

For example, a device that is equipped with three CT/VT boards and also
subscribes to three 4I4V SV streams will have nine channels per board and eight
channels per SV stream, for a total of 3 × 9 + 3 × 8 = 51 channels. There are three
CT/VT boards and three SV streams making the count 51 + 6 × (3 + 3) = 87. Since
this is less than 90 this configuration can be used.

Relative synchronization
Certain functions are sensitive to phase angle differences between current/voltage
sources. When the sources include multiple MUs or a combination of MUs and
local CT/VT, signals from these sources must be synchronized to a common time
reference using PTP or IRIG-B.
NOTE: PowerLogic P7 can also act as the PTP time source for SV
synchronization. See PTP master, page 171 for details.
468

P7/EN M/02-004A

Measurement functions

Protection and control device

Relative sync indicates whether the synchronization level of subscribed SV
streams matches a reference, which is determined as follows:
•

When there are local CT/VT channels mapped to a CT/VT group, the
reference is the device system clock (LTMS).

•

When no local CT/VT channels are mapped to a CT/VT group, the reference
is the SV stream mapped to the highest-priority group in the following order:
3ph CT group 1-n, 3ph VT group 1-n, 1ph CT group 1-n, 1ph VT group 1-n.

The following table lists all the cases where the Relative sync of a subscribed SV
stream is Locked:
SV stream
sync level

Reference
sync level

Parent clock ID condition

Relative sync

Global

Global

–

Locked

Local

Local

The SV stream and the reference
have identical parent clock ID

Locked

Local

Local

No parent clock ID is specified for the
SV stream, the reference, or both

Locked

Relative sync of the SV stream is Unlocked for all other cases.
NOTE:
•

Relative sync of the reference SV stream is always Locked.

•

If the reference SV stream is not received or lost, Relative sync of all SV
streams will be Unlocked.

•

The parent clock ID of SV streams is indicated by the optional
SynchSourceId field, which can be enabled during SV publishing
configuration. See PowerLogic P7 Communication User Manual for
details.

SV sync validation
The SV sync validation setting, available under General > System info on the
HMI or PowerLogic Engineering Suite, determines whether the Relative sync
status of a subscribed SV stream affects its validity:
•

When SV sync validation is set to On, the SV stream is valid only if its
Relative sync status is Locked.

•

When SV sync validation is set to Off, the SV stream validity is unaffected by
the Relative sync status.

By default, SV sync validation is set to On. If set to Off, check and block any
functions that are critically dependent on the SV time synchronization status.

SV mapping
Local and remote acquisitions can be mapped to the available CT/VT groups,
which vary depending on the application and are shared between local and
remote acquisitions. If a CT/VT group is mapped to one type of acquisition then it
cannot be mapped to another.
NOTE: For more information about SV mapping, see CT/VT matrix, page 463.
A three-phase CT/VT group can be mapped to four channels of one single SV
stream. The mix of channels from different SV streams, or the mix of local and
remote acquisitions are not allowed in a three-phase CT/VT group.
A single phase CT/VT group can be mapped to any channel of an SV stream.

Data quality
An SV stream indicates its quality, synchronization level, and sample capturing
time with different tags. PowerLogic P7 actively monitors these tags and other
attributes to determine if the incoming SV stream is acceptable or not.
In the following cases, the SV stream is treated as invalid and the functions
requiring the problematic SV stream are blocked:
P7/EN M/02-004A

469

Protection and control device

Measurement functions

•

The quality flag of the SV stream changes to Invalid.

•

The SV stream is lost.

•

Relative sync of the SV stream is Unlocked providing the SV sync validation
setting is On.

•

Relative to the reference, the SV stream has been delayed beyond the SV
max delay setting under General > System info.

•

The sample rate or ASDU of the SV stream is invalid.

The LSVS is used to monitor and diagnose SV subscriptions. One SV subscription

Simulated SV
During commissioning or testing, the device can be configured to process
simulated SV streams by setting Simulation accepted to On (LPHD.Sim = True)
under Control > Device mode of the HMI.
When in simulation mode, the device continues treating a normal SV stream as
valid until its simulated SV stream is received, after which the device only accepts
the simulated SV stream and treats the normal one as invalid until Simulation
accepted is set to Off.
NOTE: Such behavior applies to each subscribed SV stream independently.

470

P7/EN M/02-004A

Measurement functions

Protection and control device

Bay measurements
By default, PHTCTT 1 and PHTVTT 1 are used as the inputs for the bay
measurements. These are used to display the load measurements, power and
energy.

Bay routing
Bay routing enables different CT/VT group to be used for bay functions. The bay
CT and bay VT allow a rapid remapping of all connected functions. Bay routing can
change the routing for all bay functions together.
To configure the bay routing function through PowerLogic Engineering Suite:
1. Expand Bay x configuration > Basic info.
2. Select an option from CT bay routing drop-down to specify the CT group
(3ph CTx or CTsum1) as the input for the bay CT.
3. Select an option from VT bay routing drop-down to specify the VT group
(3ph VTx or 3ph VTsw1) as the input for the bay VT.

Bay characteristics
The following electrical characteristics of a bay can be defined under Bay x
configuration > Basic info on PowerLogic Engineering Suite:
•

Full load amps (FLA): The rated continuous current of the equipment. This is
used in thermal functions such as THMPTTR and THFPTTR.

•

Nominal frequency: The rated frequency of the power system. This also
decides the sample rate of published SV when using the Samples per period
option.

•

Phase rotation: The order in which the device expects the three-phase
system to rotate.

•

Power flow: This defines how the device assigns the sign of power for the
circuit. See Power direction, page 473 for details.

•

Reversal phase swap: For reversible motors, this defines which phases are
swapped during reversal.

Load measurements (BAYMMXU)
Bay vector
The HMI provides a graphical view of the voltage and current vectors using a
phasor diagram. Go to > Measurement > Bay x > General and select the
Vector tab. Two options are available to view the voltages. You can select to view
either as phase-to-ground or as phase-to-phase values.

RMS power measurement (PRMSGAPC)
PowerLogic P7 calculates the active, reactive and apparent Root Mean Square
(RMS) values of the power system based on the connected currents and voltages.
This RMS power measurement function is used in energy measurement and
demand value.

472

Fundamental measurements
The frequency and fundamental values can be viewed from the HMI under
Measurement > Bay x > General > Fundamental.

>

See also Input and output signals, page 476.

Frequency (FTGAPC)
PowerLogic P7 supports 50 Hz and 60 Hz rated frequency. This function
determines the frequency based on samples from the bay CT/VT. The frequency
is automatically adapted based on the availability and quality of the related signal
inputs:
•

If V1 ≥ 0.17 pu, the frequency tracking algorithm is based on V1.

•

If V1 ≤ 0.17 pu and I1 ≥ 0.2 pu, the frequency tracking algorithm is based on
I1.

•

If both V1 < 0.17 pu and I1 < 0.2 pu, the frequency is set to the rated
frequency.
NOTE:
•

V1 is the positive sequence voltage calculated from down sampled
voltage values.

•

I1 is the positive sequence current calculated from down sampled current
values.

When the calculated frequency is out of the range of 10 Hz to 72 Hz, the
frequency is set to the rated frequency.

474

P7/EN M/02-004A

Measurement functions

Protection and control device

This function also uses the determined frequency to calculate the frequency rate
of change (ROCOF) within a fixed 50 ms measuring window.

Power measurement (PWRGAPC)
The power measurement function provides the calculation of active, reactive and
apparent powers based on Fourier filter outputs from the voltage and current. This
calculation is made based on connected voltages and currents.
This function is used in power measurement based on phasors and power
protection (PPDOP/QPDOP and PPDUP) and apparent overpower.
A compensation angle can be entered which corrects all fundamental power
measurements for known CT and VT angular errors. To set the compensation
angle:
•

From the HMI, go to > Measurement > Bay x > General, select the
Settings tab, and set Power compensation angle.

•

From PowerLogic Engineering Suite, go to Bay x measurement >
Measurements > Bay measurements, and set Power compensation
angle.

Fundamental power calculation
•

Apparent power

SA = |VA| x |IA|
E71129A

The vector of VA and the vector of IA represent the phase-to-ground voltage
and the phase current complex. SB and SC can be calculated in a similar
fashion.
•

Active power

PA = VA x IA x cos(φ - CmpAng)

E71130A

φ represents the angle of the vector of VA ahead of the vector of IA. PB and PC
can be calculated in a similar fashion.
•

Reactive power

QA = VA x IA x sin(φ - CmpAng)

E71131A

QB and QC can be calculated in a similar fashion.
•

Total 3ph active power

P3ph = VAB x IA x cos(θ1 - CmpAng) + VCB x IC x cos(θ2 - CmpAng)

E71132A

θ1 represents the angle of the vector of VAB ahead of the vector of IA, θ2
represents the angle of the vector of VCB ahead of the vector of IC. This is a
classical two element wattmeter method which assumes the system is 3 wire.
•

Total 3ph reactive power

Q3ph = VAB x IA x sin(θ1 - CmpAng) + VCB x IC x sin(θ2 - CmpAng)

E71133A

This assumes the system is 3 wire.
•

Total 3ph apparent power

S3ph = P3ph2 + Q3ph2
E71134A

Demand (DVALMMXU)
Description
PowerLogic P7 calculates the average demand values of phase currents IA, IB, IC
and power values S, P and Q. They are calculated over an adjustable demand
time in a range from 1 to 100 minutes. If the calculated demand is more than the
stored maximum demand, it will replace that value. If it is less than the stored
minimum demand, it will replace that value. If the power is lost, the minimum and
maximum values are retained. The demand values can be viewed from the HMI
under > Measurement > Bay x > Demand.
The maximum and minimum, demand values can be reset by control command.
This will set all measurements to zero and restart the integration period. At the end
of the next integration period the minimum and maximum values will be set to the
demand values.

478

P7/EN M/02-004A

Measurement functions

Protection and control device

Energy measurement (EMMTR)
The energy measurement values are calculated on basis of the three-phase active
power RMS (P3PhRMS) and three-phase reactive power RMS (Q3PhRMS). The
energy values can be viewed from the HMI under > Measurement > Bay x >
Energy.

Active and reactive energy calculation
•

Active energy calculation
If the sign of P3PhRMS is positive,
ΔWhDmd = 0 and ΔWhSup = P3PhRMS × ΔT(30)
If the sign of P3PhRMS is negative,
ΔWhSup = 0 and ΔWhDmd = |P3PhRMS| × ΔT
And the accumulated active energy is:
WhSup = WhSup + ΔWhSup (kWh)
WhDmd = WhDmd + ΔWhDmd (kWh)

•

Reactive energy calculation
The same algorithm is used for reactive energy calculation:
If the sign of Q3PhRMS is positive,
ΔVArhDmd = 0 and ΔVArhSup = Q3PhRMS× ΔT
If the sign of Q3PhRMS is negative,
ΔVArhSup = 0 and ΔVArhDmd = |Q3PhRMS| × ΔT
And the accumulated reactive energy is:
VArhSup = VArhSup + ΔVArhSup (kVarh)
VArhDmd = VArhDmd + ΔVArhDmd (kVarh)

In case of power failure, these energy values will be stored in the device and used
when next initializing.
When an energy value exceeds 2147483647, it will reset and restart from zero.

Input and output signals

Busbar measurement
For busbar applications, a dedicated Busbar measurement page is provided to
display phase-segregated bias and differential currents of the check zone and
enabled main zones. All displayed currents are taken from BBPDIF and BZPDIF.
See Busbar differential (BBPDIF/BZPDIF/BFPTRC/BCPTRC, ANSI 87B/86), page
331 for details.

Measured value supervision
Each measured value has range and quality attributes.
The available states of the range attribute are:
•

normal

•

high

•

low

•

•

low-low

The determination of the range value is based on the boundary values:
•

•

•

High Limit

•

High-High Limit

The boundary values can be configured through Bay x measurement > Ranges
> Configuration in PowerLogic Engineering Suite.
The available values of the validity in quality are:
•

good

•

invalid (usually due to a VTS/CTS condition)

•

questionable (usually value outside of minimum/maximum range)
NOTE: The measured value with quality invalid will be shown in gray on HMI.

Analog deadband
The deadband values can be configured from PowerLogic Engineering Suite
under Bay x measurement > Range > Configuration. For IEC 61850
communication, the deadband can be set separately for each measurement
function block. If the changed analog does not exceed the original data plus/minus
deadband value, the device will consider the analog has not changed.
NOTE: These deadbands do not apply to DNP3 and IEC 60870-5-103.

Harmonic measurements (MHAI)
Description
The harmonic measurements function is applied for power quality analysis,
maintenance, and troubleshooting.

482

P7/EN M/02-004A

Measurement functions

Protection and control device

This function calculates the magnitude, phase angle, RMS value, and total
harmonic distortion (THD) value for three-phase voltage and three-phase current
of the 2nd-20th harmonics. Among the calculated results, RMS values and THD
values are accessible from the HMI under Measurements > Bay x > Harmonics,
PowerLogic Engineering Suite, communication ports and part of logs.
NOTE: Only if the frequency falls within the range of 80% to 120% of the rated
frequency are THD values calculated and displayed:
•

If the fundamental current is above 1% of the rated current, the THD
values of overall, odd, and even harmonics for the 2nd-20th harmonic
currents are calculated.

•

If the fundamental voltage is above 10% of the rated voltage, the THD
values of overall, odd and even harmonics for the 2nd-20th harmonic
voltages are calculated. Both line and phase voltage THD values are
available.

All calculations are based on three-second measuring windows. Each window
itself is aggregated from three one-second slots, each of which comprises one 10cycle or 12-cycle measurement period depending on the fundamental frequency.
If the line or phase voltage THD value exceeds the configured threshold, an alarm
is raised.
A resettable counter records the number of raised THD alarms. If the counter
value exceeds the configured threshold, the THD counter alarm is raised.
NOTE: The harmonic measurement of array type is not supported in
IEC 61850 Report and GOOSE function, Modbus protocol and DNP3 protocol
of P7 devices. For more information, see the Protocols used and data
exchanged section of PowerLogic P7 Communication User Manual.

Harmonics and THD calculation
Harmonics calculation
The aggregated value for a harmonic current or voltage is calculated with the
following equation:

RMSi3s =

( ∑ 2n = 0 (Si)2 )
3

E71199B

Si is the magnitude of each harmonic in every 10/12 cycles.

THD calculation
The THD is calculated with the following equation:
2

THD =

∑ 20
i = 2 (RMSi3s)
RMSH1

× 100%
E71199A

RMSi3s is the RMS value of the 2-20th harmonic within 3s.
RMSH1 is the RMS value of the fundamental.
NOTE: THD calculations for odd and even harmonics are performed in the
same manner.

P7/EN M/02-004A

Control functions

Protection and control device

Control functions
CB and switch packages
PowerLogic P7 provides circuit breaker (CB) and switch control package functions
for controlling and monitoring CBs and switches. The number of CBs and switches
that PowerLogic P7 can control and monitor depends on the main application and
its application level.
Figure 236 - Bay control overview

P711D1B

The CB package includes the following functions for three-pole CB control:
•

CB control (CBCSWI), page 499
Processes local and remote open/close commands.
Manages opening and closing operations.
Publishes the calculated CB position.

•

CB proxy (CBXCBR), page 488
Processes commands from protection functions and CB control.
Makes the CB position available.

•

CB interlocking (CBCILO), page 505
Checks interlocking conditions.

P7/EN M/02-004A

485

Protection and control device

Control functions

•

CB supervision (CBSCBR), page 570 CBSCBR (with current-based
supervision and abrasion)
Monitors the CB condition, including the mechanical wear of breaker
mechanism and electrical wear of the contacts.

•

CB failure (RBRF), page 506
Retrips or backtrips when a fault condition is not cleared because the CB fails
to operate.

•

CB three phase recloser (TRIRREC), page 510
Recloses the CB after a protection trip and keeps the CB open if all reclosing
attempts fail.

The basic CB package is available only in the P7C application that do not include
protection functions. It operates without current inputs and excludes RBRF and
abrasion supervision. It includes the following functions:
•

CB control (CBLCSWI), page 499
Behaves identically to CBCSWI. It receives local or remote open and close
commands and manages breaker operation sequences without using current
inputs.

•

CB proxy (CBLXCBR), page 488
Behaves identically to CBXCBR. It receives commands from control functions
and makes the breaker position available for automation logic.

•

CB interlocking (CBLCILO), page 505
Behaves identically to CBCILO. It checks interlocking conditions to allow or
block breaker operation based on bay and network status.

•

CB supervision (CBLSCBR), page 570 (without current-based supervision
and abrasion)
Compare to CBSCBR, it provides a simplified supervision function that
excludes abrasion monitoring, and monitors breaker position feedback to
confirm commanded operations and detect abnormal states.

The switch package contains the following functions for switch control:
•

Switch control (SWCSWI), page 499
Processes local and remote open/close commands.
Manages opening and closing operations.
Publishes the calculated switch position.

•

Switch proxy (SWXSWI), page 492
Processes commands from switch control.
Makes the switch position available.

•

Switch interlocking (SWCILO), page 505
Checks interlocking conditions.

•

Switch supervision (SWSSWI), page 582
Monitors the switch condition.

Bay control authority (BayLDLLN0)
Description
Before a control command is executed, the control function will check whether the
interface issuing the command is consistent with bay control authority. Bay control
authority is applicable to all CB, switches and tap change controls in one bay.
Settings can be viewed and modified on the HMI under Control > Bay x >
Control authority.
Bay control authority is determined by the combination of the following settings:

486

P7/EN M/02-004A

Control functions

Protection and control device

•

Device Local mode (LocKey). On the HMI, this setting can be changed under
Control > Device L/R, or changed with the device mode button on the top
right corner of the Home page. This activates the Local input status output of
the HMI key status function, and Local input status is linked to the Local input
of all bays by default.

•

Multiple level control (MltLev). If set to True, more than one level of control
commands are accepted at the same time. That is, Local, Station and
Remote operations are allowed when Local is not forced.

•

Station level control (LocSta). This determines if station level control is
accepted.

Table 397 - Bay control authority
Bay control

N/A = not applicable (position does not affect operation)
AA = always allowed
NA = not allowed
The Operation block (CmdBlk) control is used to block all controls of primary
equipment belonging to the bay.
The Local bypass (SwModKey) control is used to bypass interlocking check for
locally issued controls. If SwModKey is true, then local controls of any primary
equipment belonging to the bay are not checked against interlocking. The bypass
duration (permanent or time limited) can be set with the cmdQual and onDur
settings, which can be found on PowerLogic Engineering Suite under Bay x
configuration > Basic info.
NOTE: LocSta, CmdBlk, and SwModKey are control objects. Ensure local
control is active (BayLD0/LLN0/LocInd=True) first to allow them to be changed
via the HMI. Matrix inputs are also available for these controls which are not
subject to L/R or control configuration.

Concurrent bay control
The Multiple operation (MltOp) setting dictates whether concurrent control
operations are allowed in the bay:
•

If this setting is False, then only one control operation is allowed at a time for
all primary equipment monitored by the Control in progress (CtlPrg) and
External operation (ExtCtlPrg) signals.

•

If this setting is True, then multiple simultaneous control operations are
allowed.

The Control in progress (CtlPrg) signal monitors control operations of primary
equipment within the current bay.
When mapped to external CtlPrg signals, the External operation (ExtCtlPrg) signal
can monitor control operations from other bays, or other IEDs within the same bay.

Circuit breaker proxy (CBXCBR/CBLXCBR, ANSI 52)
Description
The functions of the circuit breaker proxy are shown below:

488

•

Issue CB state information based on CB auxiliary contacts.

•

Block control operations when trips are present. The trip signal is also
provided as an output allowing trips to be inhibited when required.
P7/EN M/02-004A

Control functions

Protection and control device

•

Process the closing/opening request coming from CB control function.

•

Count CB operation number.

CBLXCBR is functionally identical to CBXCBR for position, command processing,
and operation counting.

CB opening and closing logic
1. Trip logic
Trip command is issued to CB when the Trip input is active and neither of the
following is active: Inhibit and BlkOpn.
2. CB opening logic
Open command is issued to CB when the OpOpn input is active and none of
the following is active: Inhibit, BlkOpn, and CB severe problems (EEHthAlm).
3. CB closing logic
Close command is issued to CB when the OpCls input is active and none of
the following is active: Inhibit, BlkCls, Protection trip, CB minor problems
(EEHthWrn), and CB severe problems (EEHthAlm).

P711D3C

4. Other logic
If Inhibit input is True, CB function is blocked, Blk output is set to True.
If EEHthAlm input is True, severe problems are present in the CB, EEHealth
status is set to Alarm.
If EEHthWrn input is True, minor problems are present in the CB, EEHealth
status is set to Warning.

Opening and closing pulse generation
PowerLogic P7 provides two pulse modes for CB opening and closing. Protection
trip pulse is implemented in PTRC, it is not covered in the circuit breaker function.

P7/EN M/02-004A

489

Protection and control device

Control functions

In dwell mode, the minimum pulse length depends on setting CBClsTmms/
CBOpTmms. The maximum pulse length follows the input open/close signals. If
the input signal is latched, the output signal will also be latched.

CB position logic
When inhibit input is True, CB position is forcibly set to Open (off), the quality of
CB position is valid/substituted. This case assumes the inhibit input is mapped to
CB truck status and the status signals will be lost when the breaker is racked out.

P7/EN M/02-004A

Control functions

Protection and control device

CBAuxM is None, the CB position is based on the routed CT, if the current of the
routed CT < 0.05 pu, CB position is Open, otherwise CB position is Closed, the
quality is questionable.
When setting CBAuxM is 52a, CB position depends on the status of 52a, the
quality of CB position will inherit the quality of 52a.
When setting CBAuxM is 52b, CB position depends on the status of 52b, the
quality of CB position will inherit the quality of 52b.

Switch proxy (SWXSWI, ANSI 89)
Description
The functions of the switch proxy are shown below:

492

•

Issue switch state information based on the switch auxiliary contacts.

•

Process the closing/opening requests coming from switch control function.

•

Count the number of switch operations (OpCnt).

•

Directly control the switchgear motor

P7/EN M/02-004A

Control functions

Protection and control device

Switch opening and closing logic
1. Control opening logic
Switch opening will be blocked by Inhibit, BlkOpn and switch severe problems
(EEHthAlm). The logic diagram of switch control opening is the same with CB
control opening.
2. Control closing logic
Switch closing will be blocked by Inhibit, BlkCls, switch minor problems
(EEHthWrn) and switch severe problems (EEHthAlm). The logic diagram of
switch control closing is the same with CB control closing.
3. Other logic
If Inhibit input is True, switch proxy function is blocked, Blk output is set to
True.
If EEHthAlm input is True, severe problems exist in the switch, EEHealth
status is set to Alarm.
If EEHthWrn input is True, minor problems exist in the switch, EEHealth
status is set to Warning

P711DBA

P7/EN M/02-004A

493

Protection and control device
A

Control functions

Position detection

Opening and closing pulse generation
PowerLogic P7 provides two pulse modes for switch opening and closing. The
opening and closing pulse generation is the same as CBCSWI.

Switch position logic
When inhibit input is true, switch position is forcibly set to Open (Off), the quality of
switch position is valid/substituted. This case assumes the inhibit input is mapped
to CB truck status and the switch status signals will also be lost when the breaker
is racked out.
When setting SwAuxM is None, switch position is set to Closed, the quality of
switch position is questionable/inconsistent.
When setting SwAuxM is 89a, switch position is decided by the status of 89a, the
quality of switch position will inherit the quality of 89a.
When setting SwAuxM is 89b, switch position is decided by the status of 89b, the
quality of switch position will inherit the quality of 89b.
When setting SwAuxM is Both, switch position refers to the following table.
Table 406 - The SWXSWI position (SwAuxM = Both)
89a status

89b status

Switch state detected

False

True

Open

True

False

Closed

False

False

Intermediate

True

True

Bad state

The quality of switch position refers to the following table.
Table 407 - The SWXSWI position quality (SwAuxM = Both)

89b quality

Valid

Valid

Valid

Oscillatory

Valid

Questionable/oscillatory

Oscillatory

Oscillatory

Invalid/oscillatory

Open counter
The switch proxy records the number of switch openings.

Switchgear motor direct control
The switchgear motor direct control function is used to directly drive the isolator
motor open or closed to change the isolator status. The motor is driven in one
direction by positive voltage and the other direction by negative voltage.
This function is enabled by the SwMotCtlEna setting. The inversion can be easily
performed using the Direct Motor Control board which has dedicated motor
outputs to invert the applied voltage. For more information, see Slot B, C, D, E, F,
G: additional direct motor control (DMC) module (7I6O + HSHB) , page 77.

494

P711DVB

Opening and closing pulse generation
Two signals (SwMOpnDir/SwMClsDir) select the switchgear motor direction.
These signals are used to select whether positive or negative voltage is applied to
the motor. Each signal can drive 2 standard contacts or dedicated motor contacts
on the direct motor control board. After the Motor op delay the Power contactor
signal is raised. This is used to drive an external power contactor or to drive the
high break contact on the direct motor control board. These are in series with the
direction contacts and are used to make and break the DC current of the isolator
motor.
The motor will be driven in the selected direction for a period of time dependent
upon whether dwell or status reset mode is selected. In dwell mode, the minimum
pulse length depends on setting SwOpnTmms/SwClsTmms. The maximum pulse
length follows the input open/close signals, which would need to be set longer
than the maximum possible isolator travel time.
Figure 242 - The time sequence for dwell mode, input length < pulse setting

SwMOpnDir (SwMClsDir)

Pulse Setting = SwOpnTmms (SwClsTmms)

P711DGA

After the opening/closing pulse resets, the power contactor will drop out removing
voltage across the motor. After the Motor reset delay, the direction contacts are
opened and operation is completed. If the Power op input does not drop out within
the Motor reset delay after pulse resets, the Power contactor reset fail alarm is
raised.

Supervision
The motor voltage can be supervised by connecting the switched voltage to the
Power op input. The switched voltage is after the contactor or high break contact
used to make and break the motor current. The Motor control timeout can then be
set to alarm if the switched voltage does not pick up or drop out as expected.
•

If SwMOpTmOut is set to 0, the SwMInPwrCtr input is ignored, warnings and
alarms are not processed.

•

If SwMOpTmOut is set to values other than 0 and the power contractor stays
off, after the Power contactor output is energized, the Power contactor op fail
alarm is raised and the control operation is canceled. The Power contactor
output resets and the direction output resets after the reset delay. The alarm
resets when a new OpOpen or OpCls signal is set.

•

If SwMOpTmOut is set to values other than 0 and the power contractor stays
on, after the Power contactor output is de-energized, the Power contactor
reset fail alarm is raised. The alarm resets when the SwMInPwrCtr input
returns to False.

CB and switch control (CBCSWI/CBLCSWI and SWCSWI)
Description
Control interface
The control function is used to process and manage the CB and switch command
issued from any interface:
•

Local control from the bay station level using HMI interface.

•

Substation control using supported protocols.

•

Remote control using supported protocols.

•

Direct control via matrix inputs.

Check condition
The following consistency should be verified before sending the command to the
local CB and switch.

P7/EN M/02-004A

•

Enable opening/enable closing inputs are set by the interlock function
(CBCILO/CBLCILO) or SWCILO.

•

Primary equipment control not blocked by process or user command.

499

Protection and control device

Control functions

•

Block opening/block closing and EEHealth inputs (EEHthAlm and EEHthWrn)
are inactive from (CBSCBR/CBLSCBR) or SWSSWI.
NOTE: When CBLSCBR is used, abrasion-related warnings/alarms are
not available and therefore do not contribute to blocking logic (linked in
matrix by default). Control behavior in CBCSWI is otherwise unchanged.

•

No protection function is tripped.

Command logic
Opening command is issued when CB/switch position is Closed, Inhibit inputs are
inactive, BlkOpn is inactive, and EnaOpn is active.
Closing command is issued when CB/switch position is Open, Inhibit inputs are
inactive, BlkCls is inactive, and EnaCls is active.
Blk output is set to active when Inhibit input is active.
Commands are subject to the configuration settings table Configuration of the
(CBCSWI/CBLCSWI/SWCSWI).Pos control, page 504
Figure 246 - Command logic
Inh

≥1

BlkOpn
&

Pos = Open
EnaOpn

≥1

SwgControl

≥1

BlkCls

&

Pos = Closed
EnaCls

≥1

Closing

SwgControl

Blk
P711D2B

For the control via matrix manual inputs, the bay mode must be
Local or Local+Remote, the control command will be issued on the rising edge of
opto input OptoOpn/OptoCls, the condition check is same as above logic for HMI
control and protocol control.

CB/switch position management
This function is used to provide CB/switch position for reporting and logging.
Generally, it will pass the device position received from (CBXCBR/CBLXCBR) or
SWXSWI to the output, except when CBPosSup is set to Yes, VirtualPos output
of the device only provides Open or Closed, intermediate status is suppressed.
Take closing control for example, the initial CB position is Open, if the closing
command is executed successfully, CB position is set to Closed. Otherwise, CB
position remains Open during the oper Timeout time interval.

500

P7/EN M/02-004A

Control functions

Protection and control device

Failure reason
This function is used for providing acknowledgements and AddCause for control
command.
The authorization sequence is:
1. Authorization from Inhibit input check.
2. Authorization from interlocking check.
3. Authorization from protection checks (not applicable to switch).
4. Authorization from blocking inputs (BlkOpn and BlkCls) check.
5. Authorization from position checks (position valid/position reached/
maintenance position).
AddCause conditions are shown in the table below:
Table 412 - Control function AddCause
AddCause

Description

Blocked-by-Mode

Device mode is different with control command.

None

The result of control is successful.

Time-limit-over

Exceed the oper Timeout, control function failure.

Position-reached

The position of object is same with control target.

Invalid-position

CBBadPositon/SwBadPosition or CBIntermediate/
SwIntermediate.

Blocked-by-interlocking

Fail to fulfil the Interlocking condition.

Abortion-by-trip

Protection trip during control process.

Blocked-by-process

The status of BlkOpn/BlkCls is True.

Not supported

P7/EN M/02-004A

501

Protection and control device

Control functions

Table 413 - Input signals of the CBCSWI/CBLCSWI control (Continued)
Name

Short label

Infotip

AutoOpn

Auto open

Open from automation function

OpPrg

Operating in progress

Operating in progress

CmdBlk blocks operator control of all controllable objects.
Rel (Sync release) input allows to authorize close command according to voltage
conditions of both sides of the circuit breaker. This can be from an internal or
external synchro-check element.
SynChkByp allows to bypass synchro-check for local control.
SynOoLim (Sync fail) input indicates that synchro-check conditions are out of
limits to be able to be synchronized and hence bypasses the synchro-check time
out setting. If false (condition ready) at the close command request, CSWI
launches the synchro-check timer. This is usually applied when a long synchrocheck timer is used for applications such as asynchronous closing and can also
be used to provide anti-pumping. It is normally connected using an inverted link to
the synchronizer start signal.
SwModKeyIn (Local bypass) bypasses the interlock for local control only, it is
limited to Manual Close or Manual Open (Matrix input OptoCls/OptoOpn).
Interlock bypass from user control (SPC) is permanent or time limited according
the SwModKey Control Pulse configuration that can be configured as Persistent or
Pulse.
NOTE:
•

If SynOoLim = true and Sync release = false, then the close command is
immediately rejected (acknowledge negative with add cause = blocked by
synchro-check)

•

If SynOoLim = false and Sync release remains false at the end of
synchro-check timeout, then the close command is rejected
(acknowledge negative with add cause = blocked by synchro-check)

•

If the SynOoLim input is not connected, it is considered as false (wait for
the Sync release up to the time out).

•

If the Rel input is not connected is considered as false.

•

If SynChkEna is set to false, then the synchronism is not checked
regardless of whether the synchro-check is requested in control
command (control synchro flag is not considered).

•

If any of these inputs are invalid, they are considered as false.

Interlocking (CBCILO/CBLCILO or SWCILO)
Description
The switching commands to the controllable switching devices in the bay are
enabled only after interlocking conditions have been checked.
Boolean signals can be mapped to the block inputs via matrix.
The interlocking function only blocks control actions and has no effect on the
operation of protection functions.
CBLCILO is functionally identical to CBCILO, configuration and interlocking matrix
usage are unchanged.

Interlocking matrix
With PowerLogic Engineering Suite, navigate to Bay 1 control > Circuit breaker
> CB interlocking to set up the interlocking matrix.
Interlocking is used to prevent control objects operations based on the status of
other equipment. The interlocking function only blocks control actions and has no
effect on the operation of protection functions.
The interlocking matrix is used to link binary inputs, virtual inputs, function buttons,
protection stage outputs, CB status, grounding switch status, logic outputs, alarm
signals and GOOSE signals to inhibit the control of a control object, e.g. a circuit
breaker or a grounding switch.
Typical signals to inhibit the control of the objects like circuit breaker are active
protection stages, status of other objects, interlocking signals received from the
programmable Logic or GOOSE. These and other signals are linked to objects
through the object block matrix.

Input and output signals
Table 422 - Input signals of the (CBCILO/CBLCILO) or SWCILO
Name

Short label

Infotip

BlkCls

Block close

Block close

BlkOpn

Block open

Block open

< 20 ms

Circuit breaker failure (RBRF, ANSI 50BF)
Description
The Circuit Breaker Failure (CBF) protection function operates when a fault
condition is not cleared due to failure of the circuit breaker to operate when a
protection-initiated tripping order is sent. In this case, the CBF function sends a
tripping order to the upstream or adjacent circuit breaker to help clear the fault.
In applications where a circuit breaker has two sets of trip coils, the CBF function
may send a tripping order to the second set of trip coils (retripping) and if this does
not result in fault clearance, the CBF function will then send a tripping order
(backtripping) to the upstream or adjacent circuit breaker.
PowerLogic P7 CBF function has two stages. The retrip stage can be enabled or
disabled via a setting and the backtrip stage is always enabled.
The input signals AInit and CBInit (configured by Matrix) are used to initiate the
CBF function depending on the CBF mode setting. By default the trip conditioner
output is linked to both inputs and reset based on current or breaker status is
determined by the breaker failure detection mode. Selective reset by function can
be achieved by setting the detection mode to Current and CB status (52a & I<)
and changing the matrix links. For example, AInit is configured to the trip signals
from the current related protection functions (e.g. PHPTOC, EFPTOC,
NPSPTOC). CBInit is mapped to the trip signals from the non-current related
protection functions (e.g. PHPTOV, PHPTUV, PTOF, PTUF). The
AInit and CBInit are pulse inputs and the CBF initiation will be latched once the
signals are present for more than 20 ms.
NOTE: RBRF is included only in the standard CB package. The Automation
CB package which is for bay-control applications exclude protection functions.
If CBF is required, use the standard CB package with RBRF.

CBF mode
The CBF function may optionally be reset from undercurrent or CB position or a
combination of both by means of setting FailMod.
•

Undercurrent criterion only mode I<
The CBF is initiated by AInit and CBInit is ignored. The CBF timers will be
reset by undercurrent check.

•

CB position only mode 52a
The CBF is initiated by CBInit and AInit is ignored. The CBF timers will be
reset by CB position.

•

506

Undercurrent and CB position mode 52a & I<
The CBF can be initiated by AInit or CBInit. When the CBF is initiated by AInit,
it will be reset by undercurrent check. When the CBF is initiated by CBInit, it
will be reset by CB position.

P7/EN M/02-004A

Control functions

Protection and control device

Current zero crossing check
For high offset faults after the breaker operates a subsidence current can occur.
The secondary current is a decaying DC value with no primary current. The CBF
must not operate during these conditions and this is a achieved using a zero
crossing check. When current zero crossing detects one sample current is above
1.414 × 0.015 In and another sample is below -1.414 × 0.015 In in 3/4 cycle time,
a zero crossing is detected. The CBF can only operate when the zero crossing
detector is active indicating the secondary current has crossed zero in the last 3/4
cycle.

CBF logic
The CBF retrip timer and backtrip timer are started by AInit or CBInit.
While the timers are running, the CBF function can be reset by undercurrent check
(initiated by AInit) or CB position (initiated by CBInit).
When the retrip timer or backtrip timer is expired, some checks are needed before
issuing retrip or backtrip output signal.
•

•

For CBF initiated by AInit, the fault current clearance is checked. Following
conditions should be met simultaneously:
◦

The current zero crossing is detected. (Output signal StrZCDPhCur is
True).

◦

Any of the overcurrent checks fulfill. The phase current exceeds the
threshold PhChkValA (Output signal StrOvPhCur is True), or the neutral
current exceeds the threshold NeutChkValA (Output signal StrOvNeutCur
is True), or negative sequence current exceeds the threshold I2ChkValA
(Output StrOvI2Cur is True). If one of the current threshold is set to 0,
related checks are all ignored.

◦

The phase current exceeds the undercurrent check threshold DetValA
(Output signal StrA is True).

For CBF initiated by CBInit, the CB position is checked.
CB is in Close status.

A reset delay timer is provided to ensure a minimum trip pulse is issued. This
allows direct tripping of breaker trip coils. Alternatively, contacts used for retripping
and backtripping can be set as latching. The block diagrams give an overview of
the CBF functionality, based on consideration of phase A current for simplicity.

Protection and control device

Control functions

CB three phase recloser (TRIRREC, ANSI 79)
Description
Dependent on the application, a high percentage of faults on the electrical network
are transient, such as lightning or insulator flashovers. The auto-recloser (AR)
function is designed to automatically restore power to a feeder or overhead line
after a protection trip has occurred by a trip/close sequence (auto-reclosing cycle),
to minimize the down-time of the network power in case of a transient fault.
This means that in most fault incidents, if the faulty line is immediately tripped,
reclosure of the CB will result in the line being successfully reenergized (such as
Closed/Open/Closed cycle).
But, as permanent faults can also occur, an AR scheme must be also designed, to
allow elimination of this fault case by, after a defined number of auto-reclosing
cycles, leaving the circuit breaker in the open state (such as Closed/Open/Closed/
Open cycle).

Auto recloser function
The AR function can provide up to 4 reclosing attempts or shots. A reclosing shot
is initiated by the Initiate AR matrix input which is linked to the appropriate
protection elements or trip signals.
The appropriate reclose time (Rec3Tmms) of the cycle is launched when the trip
appears or when the trip disappears, depending on the setting (RecStrMod). At
the end of the reclose time, the closing sequence is started. If the shot has sync
check, it will check the release signal and close the breaker when it is received. If
the shot does not have sync check, the close pulse (OpCls) is generated, which
leads finally to a CB close command by the CBXCBR to the CB, if all CB closing
conditions are met.
The reclaim time (RclTmms) starts with the CB Closed state detection. If the CB
has not been tripped again, the AR logic is reset at the end of the reclaim time, to
move the AR back to start a new cycle again from the first shot (for future faults).
If the protection re-trips during the reclaim time, the relay either advances to the
next shot in the programmed AR cycle, or if all programmed reclose attempts have
been made, goes to lockout.
The following diagram summarizes the auto recloser procedure for a successful
reclose.
Figure 249 - Successful reclose
Trip
Open
CB Pos

Close

Reclose time

Close pulse

Reclaim time
P711DHA

The following diagram illustrates a second trip command before the end of the
reclaim time:

P711DIA

Settings
The user can define:
•

If the AR is enabled or disabled (SetMod)

•

The maximum number of cycles (UseCyc)

•

The timeout window for synchro check (SyncTmms)

•

The timeout window after the CB operates (CBOpTmms)

•

The timeout window after a Manual Close (ManClsTmms)

•

If the protection trips initiate the AR cycle with rising or falling edge
(RecStrMod)

For each cycle, the user could define:
•

The reclose dead time (time to reclose command after trip, Rec3Tmms1-4)

•

The reclaim time, after the CB close (RclTmms)

•

If the synchrocheck conditions are mandatory or not (SyncChk1-4)

Protection and control device

Control functions

•

Pos: CB Position, input link from the circuit breaker proxy CBXCBR

•

BlkCls: CB Block close, input link from the circuit breaker proxy CBXCBR

•

CBCILO

•

Pos.stSeld: CB Position control selected or running, input link from the circuit

•

OptoCls: Manual close command from the circuit breaker control CBCSWI

•

OpCls: Operate close command impulse, output link to the circuit breaker
proxy CBXCBR
NOTE: The connections to the synchronizer are made with the matrix to allow
internal or external sync check to be used.

AR ready, AR lock, and AR unlock
The AR is ready, when all conditions are fulfilled to activate the function, for
example the CB position must be closed and no event shall have locked the
function.
The AR is locked in the following cases:
•

If there is still a trip during the reclaim time of the last cycle

•

If a slow trip occurs and the used number of reclose cycles is 1

•

If the AR receives a lockout trip

•

If CB operation fails

•

If the CB closing conditions are not reached

•

If the Synchro-check prevents any closing

•

If the dedicated input LckAR is set to 1

P711DKA

When the AR is not Out of service but Locked, the AR cannot send any Close
order OpCls to the CBXCBR. The current AR status AutoRecSt is then set to Not
Ready, LckCond is filled with the reason of the lock and the AR logic is reset:
•

StrCyc and OpCls are set to 0

•

RecCyc is set to 0

•

All timers are reset. NxtCyc is set to None

The auto-recloser can be unlocked by a user or an automation function:

512

•

With an unlock control input (RstLckCtl) from the HMI or communication link

•

With an unlock input assigned to RstLck in the matrix

•

Following a successful manual close

P7/EN M/02-004A

Control functions

Protection and control device

•

After removal of the Out of service state with control command (OutServCtl)
or binary input (OutOfServ)

Trip configuration
The recloser is initiated by linking the protection operate signal to the Initiate AR
(MainTr) input. This provides a standard 1-4 shot auto-reclose for the connected
functions.
It is also possible to vary the number of shots depending upon which protection
operates by using the Initiate AR and Skip shot AR (SlowARTr) inputs. In these
schemes, the first shot is normally connected to instantaneous protection and has
a short dead time and is referred to as high speed or fast auto-reclose.
Subsequent reclose shots have a longer dead time and are referred to as time
delayed or slow auto-reclose. Non-instantaneous protection skips the fast stage
and only initiates the slow stages.
The trip(s) which shall initiate the fast AR cycle are configured in the input matrix
using the Initiate AR input signal. The trip(s) to trigger directly a slow and therefore
2nd AR cycle is to be assigned to the Skip shot AR input signal. All trip(s) which
shall lock the AR, are assigned to the Lockout trip (LckARTr) input signal which
immediately locks the recloser function. The following figure shows the principle of
the trigger or lock logic for the AR function.
Figure 253 - Trips to trigger or lock AR cycles
LckARTr
PHPTOC5.Op.General
≥1

MainTr
PHPTOC1.Op.General
PHPTOC2.Op.General

Fast
AR
Cycle 1

Slow
AR
Cycle 2

Slow
AR
Cycle 3

Slow
AR
Cycle 4

≥1

SlowARTr
PHPTOC3.Op.General
PHPTOC4.Op.General

P711DLA

Depending upon the number of cycles configured as maximum with setting
parameter Number of shots (UseCyc), only a fast (UseCyc = 1), a fast and a slow
(UseCyc = 2), a fast and up to 2 (UseCyc = 3) or a fast and up to 3 slow (UseCyc =
4) cycles are provided.
Several trips can occur for the same fault in parallel. Therefore, a priority between
the trips has been defined:
•

LckARTr has the highest priority: if such trip occurs, the AR is immediately
locked

•

MainTr has the second priority: if there is no LckARTr trip but there is MainTr,
the AR is launched with all the cycles

•

SlowARTr has the lowest priority: if there is only SlowARTr trips, the AR is
launched with the first cycle skipped

CB operation check
Immediately after the trip is detected, a time-out (CBOpTmms) is launched to wait
for the CB opening. The AR is locked if the CB has not opened at the end of this
time.
At the end of the shot dead time, the CB close signal is sent and the same timeout is launched to wait for the CB closing. The AR is locked if the CB is not closed
at the end of this timer.

P711DMB

CB closing check
The AR conducts checks prior to initiating the Close signal which are dependent
on the application. The checks ensue that:
•

The CB position is Open at the end of the CB operation timeout (CBOpTmms)
or at the end of the Reclose Time (Rec3Tmms)

•

There is not a CB Close blocked input present from the Circuit Breaker
(CBXCBR.BlkCls)

•

No Position Control is selected or running (CBCSWI.Pos.stSeld)

•

The external switchgear is in a suitable state for closing the CB (interlock
checks)

If the CB closing conditions are not reached at the end of the Reclose Time, the
AR is locked.
Figure 255 - CB closing conditions not reached logic
CSWI.Pos.StSeld = 1

XCBR.Pos ≠ OPEN
≥1

CB Closing Conditions
not reached

XCBR.BlkCls = 1

CILO.EnaCls = 0

P711DNA

AR with synchronism check
For operation with synchonism check, the sync Release input must be present
when the synchro-check is set for the shot to allow reclose. This input is
connected to the release output of the relevant synchro-check stage. This can
allow a different synchro-check configuration for manual and auto reclosing by
varying which synchro-check element release is connected.
With a setting for each cycle, the user can choose to check or not the
synchronism.
When this setting is set to disabled, no system checks are required that may be
preferred when high speed AR is applied to avoid the extra time for a system
check.
When this setting is set to enabled, an extra delay (SyncTmms) is introduced after
the reclose time. At the end of this delay, if the synchro-check is not OK (Rel = 0),
the AR fails and is locked. If a release is received within this timeout, then it will
issue a CB Close signal.

514

P7/EN M/02-004A

Control functions

Protection and control device

The Sync fail (SynOoLim) input can also be used to ensure the synchronizer is
seeing valid closing conditions during the synchronizer timeout period. This is
normally applied when this period is quite long and it is an advantage to lockout
quickly if synchronism will not be met. For example, if asynchronous closing is
being used, an inverted link can be made to the synchronizer start signal to ensure
it is seeing valid voltage and frequency conditions and will close at the next
voltage alignment. If the start is not present the at the end of the reclose time the
AR will fail immediately and lockout without waiting for the synchrocheck to time
out.

515

Protection and control device

Control functions

Operator control
No automation or operator control, except Out of service, is allowed during an AR
closing sequence.
The output signal Close in progress (StrCls = 1) signals this critical phase, which
can be used as required to block manual CB control. The signal is active from the
start of the AR sequence, including synchro-check phase when used, until the CB
has reached the closed state or fail to close has been recognized. By default, this
is linked to the External op (AutoOpPrg) of the CB control function (CBCSWI) to
block operator actions during reclosing.
Figure 260 - Status of StrCls
Trip
Open
CB Pos

Close

Reclose time

Close pulse

StrCls
P711DSA

Manual close
After a manual close, the AR is inhibited by a configurable time delay (for safety
reasons if the line has a permanent fault after a long opening) and the current auto
reclosing status (AutoRecSt) is set to Not Ready.
At the end of this delay, the AR is ready to start a new cycle from the first shot and
the current Recloser status (AutoRecSt) is set back to Ready.
If the AR was locked before closing, it is unlocked after this time delay.
Figure 261 - Unlocked due to Manual Close
Manual Close
AR Status

Not Ready
ManClsTmms

Ready

AR Locked
P711DTB

Out of service
The auto-recloser function can be set to Out of service by the user using the HMI,
a control command OutServCtl via the communication link or by a signal assigned
to the input OutOfServ.
If the auto-recloser is out of service, no cycle is authorized. The current Recloser
status is set to Not Ready and the Next shot is None.
If an Out of service request (command or binary input equal to 1) is received
during an AR cycle, the cycle stops immediately and the AR logic is reset.

516

P7/EN M/02-004A

Control functions

Protection and control device

Monitoring
All stages of the AR operation are monitored to check the AR sequences:
•

If the function is enabled or disabled (Beh)

•

Which reclose cycle is running (RecCyc)

•

Which reclose cycle is coming next (NxtCyc)
NOTE: The NxtCyc signal can be linked to protection elements to
change/block/enable functions depending upon whether the recloser is
active or the shot number. Typical applications are fuse saver schemes
where the first shot trip is fast followed by slower shots.

•

Which AR state is running (AutoRecSt)

•

If CB close is in progress or in operation (StrCls, OpCls)

•

If AR is dynamically blocked (Blk)

•

Which event caused the lockout (LckCond)

•

How many three phase reclosing operations are made since the previous
reset (Rec3PhCnt)

Position indication (DPGGIO)
Description
The position indication function determines the position of a circuit breaker or
switch based on two status inputs. It outputs a position status that can be mapped
to a mimic symbol or the input of a function that requires clear differentiation
between equipment states. The equipment name can be changed with the Name
setting from PowerLogic Engineering Suite.
Position logic
The position is determined based on the status of Closed and Open according to
the following logic:

P7/EN M/02-004A

521

Protection and control device

Control functions
Closed

Open

Position

False

True

Open

True

False

Closed

False

False

Intermediate

True

True

Bad state

NOTE: If only one input is available, the other input must be connected using
an inverted signal to maintain correct logic. For example, if only 89a is
available for a disconnector, connect 89a to Closed and its inverted signal to
Open.
Equipment health logic
Equipment health (EEHealth) status indicates whether the position is in a normal
state:
•

EEHealth is set to OK when Position is Open or Closed.

•

EEHealth is set to Alarm when Position remains Intermediate or Bad state for
1 s. The alarm resets 10 s after Position returns to Open or Closed.

Synchrocheck (RSYN, ANSI 25)
Description
The synchro-check function checks the synchronization of the electrical networks
on either side of a circuit breaker to avoid shocks to the system or breaker
flashover during closing.
The RSYN function monitors the voltage amplitude, frequency and phase angle
difference between two voltages.
The function operates in different conditions according to four synchronization
modes:
•

In case of Off mode, the circuit breaker is not released when the voltage is
present on both sides.

•

In case of Synchronous mode, the conditions to release are defined as:

•

•

◦

The phase angle difference is less than the setting.

◦

The frequency slip is less than the setting.

◦

The voltage magnitude difference is less than the setting.

In case of Asynchronous mode, the conditions to release are defined as:
◦

The phase angle difference is less than the circuit breaker operate time
multiplied by the slip and the phase angle difference is reducing.

◦

The frequency slip is less than the setting.

◦

The voltage magnitude difference is less than the setting.

In case of Generator mode, the conditions to release are defined as:
◦

The phase angle difference is less than the setting.

◦

The frequency slip is less than the setting, and the frequency of the line is
above the bus.

◦

The voltage magnitude difference is less than the setting, and the
magnitude of the line is above the bus.

For Synchronous and Generator modes, when the conditions for closing are
met, a Start signal will be issued. After the conditions are met for Sync check time,
the Release signal will be issued.
For Asynchronous mode, a Start signal will be issued when the voltage and
frequency slip are less than the settings for Sync check time. The Release signal
will be issued when the angle difference is predicted to be zero based on the
current frequency slip and angle measurements.

Voltage check
When one of the two voltages is absent, coupling may be authorized according to
one of the seven checking modes:
•

VBus and VLine absent (DLDB)

•

VBus absent and VLine present (LLDB)

•

VBus present and VLine absent (DLLB)

•

VBus absent regardless of VLine state (present or absent) (DLDB OR LLDB)

•

VLine absent regardless of VBus state (present or absent) (DLDB OR DLLB)

•

One voltage is present while the other one is absent (LLDB OR DLLB)

•

At least one voltage is absent (DLDB OR LLDB OR DLLB)

The absence of the bus/line voltage is detected by comparing the voltage to the
limit setting DeaBusVal/DeaLinVal.
The presence of the bus/line voltage is detected by comparing the voltage to the
limit setting LivBusVal/LivLinVal.
When voltage is present and the frequency slip or the frequency difference
between system frequency and nominal frequency exceeds 3 Hz, the check
voltage will be inhibited.
When the required live/dead conditions are met, the Start signal will be issued.
Providing the conditions are maintained, the Release signal is then issued after
Volt check time.

P7/EN M/02-004A

525

Protection and control device

Control functions

Phase selection
Eight voltage types are available in phase selection function for synchro-check or
voltage check.
•

VA: phase A voltage

•

VB: phase B voltage

•

VC: phase C voltage

•

VAB: phase A to phase B voltage

•

VBC: phase B to phase C voltage

•

VCA: phase C to phase A voltage

•

3VPN: VA for synchro-check, minimum voltage among VA, VB and VC for
voltage presence checking, maximum voltage among VA, VB and VC for
voltage absence check.

•

3VPP: VAB for synchro-check, minimum voltage among VAB, VBC and VCA
for voltage presence checking, maximum voltage among VAB, VBC and VCA
for voltage absence check.
NOTE: 3VPN and 3VPP phase selection can only be chosen when the check
voltage is mapped from three phase VT, if not, this function will be blocked.

Synchro-check bypass
In PowerLogic P7, the synchro-check can be bypassed via the Bypass input. This
will force a Release.

Tap changer control (YLTC, ANSI TCC)
Description
The tap changer control function is used to control the on-load tap changer
(OLTC) built inside the transformer structure. The YLTC acquires tap position from
the OLTC via matrix inputs, and issues tap position control commands to the
OLTC.
The function supports manual control (via matrix and control inputs) and
connection to automatic control (via matrix inputs).
NOTE: To disable control for maintenance, the TCC Local (LocKey) input can
be energized to block all control outputs from this function.

532

P7/EN M/02-004A

Control functions

Protection and control device

Figure 273 - Transformer regulation system with OLTC and YLTC

OLTC

YLTC

Local
Local Remote

↑

à Tap position
à Tap health
à Tap change in progress

↓

ß Raise/Lower commands

M

Blocking

Settings

Manual
Raise/Lower

Alarms

Primary Voltage V1

Secondary Voltage V2

Status

P711E0A

Tap position acquisition
The YLTC can read tap positions from matrix inputs with Binary, BCD, or Grey
encoding. This is defined by the TPI type setting.
The Position min and Position max settings define the valid tap position range:
•

When the acquired tap position reaches the end of the defined range, the end
position reached signal (Highest position reached or Lowest position
reached) is asserted.

•

When the acquired tap position falls outside the defined range, this position is
considered invalid and Position health will change to Alarm. In this case the
corresponding end position reached signal will also be asserted.

Tap position acquisition from binary inputs
TPI0-6 inputs are used to acquire the tap position value, and the TPI sign input is
used to acquire the sign of the position value.
The least significant bit (LSB) and most significant bit (MSB) are different
depending on the TPI type:
•

Binary or Grey: TPI0 is the LSB and TPI5 is the MSB. TPI6 is not used.

•

BCD: TPI0 is the LSB and TPI6 is the MSB.

The actual tap position is calculated from these inputs and updated to Position
(TapChg.valWTr.posVal) if valid.
NOTE: If any of the TPI0-6 inputs have invalid quality or TPI fault is True,
TapChg.valWTr.posVal will indicate invalid quality.

P7/EN M/02-004A

533

Protection and control device

Control functions

Tap position filtering
The TPI time (TPITmms) setting is used to filter out transient position values.
The TPI time is launched when a new tap position is detected, or the quality of an
existing tap position changes from invalid to valid (indicated by TPI fault changing
to False). The new tap position is accepted only after the TPI time elapses.

Tap position control
To control the OLTC, the YLTC issues raise or lower commands associated with
the following signals:
•

Manual mode: the Manual raise, Manual lower inputs or the TapChg control.
This mode can be blocked by the Block manual operations input.

•

Automatic mode: the Auto raise and Auto lower inputs. This mode can be
blocked by the Block auto operations input.
NOTE:
•

The Block raise and Block lower inputs block raise and lower commands
from all control modes.

Blocking logic of YLTC
Full blocking
The YLTC blocks all control commands to the OLTC if any of the following
conditions are met:
•

Operation blocked is True.

•

The OLTC is in local mode (LocKey = True).

•

Tap changer health is Alarm.

•

A tap change is in progress (indicated by YLTC.TapChg.valWTr.transInd),
including:
◦

A tap position change by the YLTC

◦

A spontaneous tap position change (runaway)

◦

An external tap position change (TapChgTrans = True)

•

The Intertap delay has not elapsed after the last tap change.

•

The control of another primary equipment in the bay is in progress providing
Independent operation is set to True

Partial blocking
The YLTC blocks control commands of one direction, control mode, or control
level if any of the following conditions are met:
•

Raise commands are blocked if Block raise or High position reached is True.

•

Lower commands are blocked if Block lower or Lowest position reached is
True.

•

Manual control mode is blocked if Block manual operations is True.

•

Automatic control mode is blocked if Block auto operations is True.

•

Control commands from a control level are blocked if it fails the control
authority check.

Tap change control interface
Tap control commands can be issued through any of the following interfaces:

534

P7/EN M/02-004A

Control functions

Protection and control device

•

The HMI:
◦

Control > Bay x > TCC > Control > Position

◦

Function keys mapped to the Manual raise and Manual lower signals.

•

Binary inputs or subscribed GOOSE mapped to the manual or automatic
raise and lower signals.

•

Substation or remote control using supported protocols.
NOTE: Only one tap change control command can be active at a time.

Control authority check and independent operation
Manual tap changer controls of the YLTC are subject to the combination of
general local/remote control constraints, TCC local (LocKey), and L/R check
(LRChk):
•

When LocKey is False and LRChk is True, the YLTC follows general local/
remote control authority check as described in Bay control authority
(BayLDLLN0), page 486.

•

When LocKey is False and LRChk is False, the YLTC does not check control
authority and allows commands from all control levels.

•

When LocKey is True, no control level can issue commands to the OLTC via
the YLTC.

By default, the YLTC can issue tap changer control commands even if other
primary equipment controls are happening in the bay. The behavior can be
disabled by setting Independent operation to False.
When the tap changer control is being selected or executed, the Control in
progress signal is activated. This could affect other primary equipment controls in
the bay.
NOTE: Automatic tap changer controls issued via Auto raise and Auto lower
inputs are never checked against control authority (except LocKey) or the
Independent operation setting.

Supervision
The YLTC monitors tap changer health and the tap changer control process.
Alarms are generated if the alarm conditions are detected.
In the following cases, the Tap changer health (EEHealth) will be set to Alarm and
all tap changer controls are blocked:
•

The TCC alarm input is True.

•

An invalid tap position is indicated by TPI (Position health shows Alarm).

In the following cases, Tap changer health will be set to Warning and an alarm is
raised if applicable, but tap changer controls are still allowed:

P7/EN M/02-004A

•

The TCC warning input is true and the TCC alarm input is False.

•

The rate of tap changes exceeds the set threshold and the Op rate warning is
raised.

•

The operation count exceeds the set threshold and the Op count warning is
raised.

•

The operation time of a tap change exceeds the set threshold and the Op
time warning is raised.

P7/EN M/02-004A

Logging and recording functions

Protection and control device

Logging and recording functions
Time tagging
PowerLogic P7 stores time tagged logs and records which can be used for fault
analysis. These data can be read from the device and analyzed using the
corresponding tools.
The items that are time tagged and logged are:
•

Sequence of events (SOE)

•

Disturbance record

•

Operation log

•

Communication record

•

Fault record

•

Trend log
NOTE: All these logs can be available for third party SFTP clients to extract
and view. For details about SFTP connection and configuration, see the SFTP
section of PowerLogic P7 Communication User Manual.

PowerLogic P7 time stamp resolution is 1 ms.

Sequence of events (GENGLOG)
Description
PowerLogic P7 is capable of logging and recording of all events that happen
during power system operation. All operationally relevant signals, each fully
tagged with date and time at signal start and signal end, are registered and stored
in chronological order. For example, an event can be, start-on, start-off, trip-on or
trip-off of any protection stage, alarm-on or alarm-off, a binary input change, etc.
Table 446 - Example of SOE record

events.

The maximum size of the buffer is 10000 events. All events are stored in nonvolatile memory inside PowerLogic P7. SOE is stored in Data Object level. There
may be multi Data Attribute status in one single SOE.
When the buffer is filled, the oldest event will be overwritten when a new event
occurs. The shown resolution of a time stamp for an event is one millisecond, but
the actual resolution depends on the particular function creating the event. For
example, most protection stages create events with a 1 ms, 5 ms or 20 ms
resolution. The absolute accuracy of all time stamps also depends on the
protection relay's time synchronization.

541

Protection and control device

Logging and recording functions

Maintenance record
PowerLogic P7 provides a detailed diagnostic record, which can be used by the
Schneider Electric technical support team to investigate issues and troubleshoot
the device.
The diagnostic record file can be downloaded from the PowerLogic Engineering
Suite under Device logs > Maintenance records > Diagnostic record. This file
in .gz format is encrypted and can only be read by the support team.

Communication record
Description
The communication record function is used to capture network traffic of the
Ethernet interfaces on PowerLogic P7. The captured traffic can then be analyzed
and help troubleshoot networking problems.
The capturing can only be manually triggered, either from the HMI under Logs >
Communication records > Status > Comms capture, or by a control command
from supported communication protocols. The Ethernet interface, protocols, and
record time can be altered in the accompanying settings.
The size of one communication record is limited to 20 MB. When this limit is
reached, the capturing process stops and generates a record file regardless of the
configured record time. Otherwise, the capturing stops after the configured record
time.
The generated communication records are PCAP files. Each interface can
generate and store up to 3 records. Once this limit is reached, the oldest record
will be overwritten by a new one.
NOTE:
•

For HTTPS, the saved PCAP files are composed of raw TLS packets and
a pre-master key file for decryption. The saved key file is limited to 64 KB.

Output signals

Fault records (TCRGLOG)
Description
Fault record provides fault signals including the measured fault data captured
during a fault sequence. The fault records are logged in chronological order with
reference to the specific fault.
Each bay has independent fault record. The fault record is triggered by any start
and any trip signals which are connected by default in the matrix to PTRC. The
fault record details any protection operations when the PTRC is started.
When any start or any trip picks up, the fault record starts to record. A new fault
record is generated if any protection function operates during the recording period.
This record is discarded if no protection function operates during the recording
period.

P7/EN M/02-004A

Logging and recording functions

Protection and control device

Fault locator (RFLO)
Description
The Fault locator function is applied to determine the distance to the fault location.
The fault locator samples data from the analog current and voltage inputs which is
fixed to TCTT1/TVTT1 and writes it to a cyclic buffer until a fault condition is
detected. The data in the input buffer is then held to allow the fault calculation to
be made and to determine the distance to fault location. The calculated location of
the fault is sent to the fault record for the fault.

Fault point scan
The fault locator stores the sampled data within a 20-cycle cyclic buffer at a
resolution of 48 samples per cycle. The fault location is calculated based on the
sampled data in the buffer. When the fault location calculation is triggered by the
Start signal from the PTRC, the data in the buffer is frozen so that the buffer
contains 10 cycles of pre-fault data and 10 cycles of post-fault data. Fault
calculation starts shortly after this trigger point.
To identify the exact fault point, a total of four cycles of sample data will be
scanned, which includes two cycles of pre-fault data and two cycles of post-fault
data. If two consecutive sample points meet the following condition, the second
sample point will be identified as the fault point.

i ph (n) - i ph (n - 48) ³ 0.125 ´ i ph (n - 48)

E71268A

Where:
iph (n): Sample value of Phase A, B, C
iph (n–48): Sample value of the previous cycle of Phase A, B, C

Faulted phase selection
The selection of the faulted phase(s) is performed by comparing the magnitudes
of the three phase-to-phase delta currents. The delta current is FFTPost-fault minus
FFTPre-fault.
A single phase-to-ground fault produces the same delta currents on two of these
signals and zero on the third. A phase-to-phase or double phase-to-ground fault
produces one delta current which is larger than the other two. A three-phase fault
produces three delta currents which are the same size. A delta current is deemed
to be large enough to be included in faulted phase selection only when it’s greater
than 80% of the maximum delta current.
The following diagram takes a single phase-to-ground fault of Phase C as an
example to show how the delta currents can be used to select the faulted phase
(s), where,
DeltaBC > 0.8 × max (DeltaAB, DeltaBC , DeltaCA)
DeltaCA > 0.8 × max (DeltaAB, DeltaBC , DeltaCA)

P7/EN M/02-004A

545

Protection and control device

Logging and recording functions

From the diagram, DeltaBC and DeltaCA are considered in determining the faulted
phase, and a single phase-to-ground fault of Phase C is identified.

Fault location calculation
The calculation works by obtaining the vectors, estimating the phase of the fault
current If, and solving Equation (1) for fault location in percentage m at the instant
of time where If = 0.

Basic theory of fault location
A two-machine equivalent circuit of a faulted power system is shown below.
Figure 274 - Two-machine equivalent circuit diagram

From the diagram, the fault location in percentage m can be calculated by
estimating the fault current If and solving the following equation for fault location.
Vp = Ip × mZ + If × Rf

(1)

Obtaining the vectors
Different sets of vectors are chosen depending on the type of fault identified by the
phase selection algorithm. The calculation using Equation (1) is applied for either
a phase-to-ground fault or a phase-to-phase fault.
•

For phase-to-ground fault, take Phase A for example:
Vp = VA
Ip × Z = IA × Z1 + IN × Z1 × kZn
If =ΔIA – 1/3 × ΔIN

•

For phase-to-phase fault, take Phase A to Phase B for example:
Vp = VA – VB
Ip × Z = IA × Z1 – IB × Z1
If = ΔIA – ΔIB

Where:

546

•

VA, VB, VC: Phases voltage ( Post-fault FFT value) detected

•

IA, IB, IC: Phases current (Post-fault FFT value) detected

•

ΔIA, ΔIB, ΔIC: Delta current of three phases, Post-fault FFT current minus Prefault FFT current

•

IN = IA + IB + IC

•

ΔIN: Delta IN, Post-fault FFT IN minus Pre-fault FFT IN

•

Z1: Positive sequence line impedance

•

Z0: Zero sequence line impedance

•

kZn = (Z0 – Z1)/3Z1: Residual compensating factor

P7/EN M/02-004A

Logging and recording functions

Protection and control device

Solving the equation for the fault location
When the sine wave of instantaneous fault current If passes through zero (IfRf=0),
the instantaneous values of the sine waves Vp and Ip can be used to solve
Equation (1) for fault location in percentage m. This is determined by shifting the
calculated vectors of Vp and IpZ by the angle If, and then dividing the imaginary
component of Vp by the imaginary component of IpZ.

V p' = V p ´

-j ( I f )
If

E71200A

( I p ´ Z )' = ( I p ´ Z ) ´

m=

-j ( I f )
If

E71201A

imag (V p' )

(

imag ( I p ´ Z )

'

)

E71202A

The fault percentage is then given by m. The fault location in impedance FltZ can
be calculated by multiplying m by the line impedance setting Z1. The fault location
in distance FltDiskm can be calculated by multiplying m by the line length setting
LinLenkm. FltDisMile is also provided to indicate the distance in miles.
FltZ = m × Z1
FltDiskm = m × LinLenkm
FltDisMile = 0.62137 × FltDiskm

400 kV transmission line

Logging and recording functions

Protection and control device

Characteristics
Table 462 - Characteristics of the RFLO
Characteristics

Values

Impedance/FltZ
Accuracy

±2%(31)

During testing, the fault values should be retained for >50 ms to simulate a
breaker opening time and create the correct pre-fault and post-fault values for
accurate fault location.
To improve the accuracy during transient state, the fault location value will be
calculated several times (maximum 4 times). Each calculation of the fault location
value is based on different Post-fault FFT values, each time moving 1 cycle to get
a new Post-fault FFT value. Only the final value with a deviation of less than 0.01
to the previous value will be considered a valid output.

Fault measurement (RFMX)
Description
The pre-fault data and fault data comes from the RFMX. Pre-fault data is the
general analog data two cycles before the rising edge of any start. Fault data is
the general analog data one cycle after the rising edge of any trip. A subsequent
trigger is only possible when the record is complete. The fault record is generated
and saved after any start and trip reset. Each fault record has a maximum of 50
protection events.

P7/EN M/02-004A
