Protection and control device

Logging and recording functions

Voltage variation (QVVR)
Description
The voltage variation function is applied to monitor and analyze voltage quality.
Voltage can have disturbances such as periods of voltage swell, voltage dip, or
voltage interruption, all of which can cause equipment damage. This function has
three elements to monitor these disturbances.
This function measures the three-phase RMS voltage including harmonics up to
20th order and supports measurement modes of Phase-neutral and Phase-phase.
If, for a configured duration, the measured voltage exceeds the swell start value,
drops below the dip start value (but still above the interruption start value), or
drops below the interruption value, a power quality record is generated to log the
voltage swell, dip or interruption event. All these power quality records include
phase, start time, duration, maximum or minimum voltage.
NOTE:
•

When a voltage interruption event happens, the voltage dip element is
blocked.

•

When Interruption operating delay is set to 0, the voltage interruption
element is disabled.

Three counters are available to separately record the number of voltage swell,
voltage dip, and voltage interruption events.
Counters and records are accessible via communication ports or the HMI under
Logs > Bay x > Voltage variation.
Records can be downloaded from PowerLogic Engineering Suite under Bay x
logs > Power quality records > Voltage variation.

550

Disturbance recorder (DRRDRE)
Description
PowerLogic P7 provides a disturbance recording with the sampled analog values
of all used analog currents and voltages within a bay before, during and after a
fault event.
Triggering the recording
The recording can be triggered by any start or trip signal from any protection
stage, by a binary input, logic output or GOOSE signals. The triggering signal is
selected in the matrix. The recording can also be triggered manually. All
recordings are time-stamped. The record will start Pre-trigger time before the rise
of the input and finish Post-trigger time after the fall of the input or expiration of
Max pulse length timer or expiration of Max record time (whichever occurs first).
Setting Max pulse length to zero provides fixed length records with a length of Pretrigger + Post-trigger times.
The Max record time (ms) depends upon the storage rate and number of channels
in the bay. It can be calculated by:
Max record time = 3275736000 / [(n + 3) × Storage rate]
Where n is the number of CT and VT channels mapped in the CT/VT groups.
Reading recordings
The recording is in COMTRADE format. This also means that other programs can
be used to view and analyze the recordings made by PowerLogic P7. The records
are stored in 2013CFF format with binary data.
Number of channels
A maximum of 200 records are stored with a mix of analog and binary signals:
•

All analog channels used in the bay are recorded. Frequency and ROCOF
are recorded. If differential elements are present, differential and bias values
are recorded. At least one analog channel must be mapped to create a
record.

•

All digital channels within the bay are recorded if they are active or changed
during the recording period. All device binary signals (for example, binary
input and output status) will also be included if active or changed during the
recording period.

All records are stored in non-volatile memory in PowerLogic P7.
When storage is full, the oldest record will be overwritten. (Max storage size is 300
MB).
Downloading disturbance records
The available disturbance records can be selectively downloaded to local PC from
PowerLogic Engineering Suite under Bay 1 logs > Disturbance records. Sorting
options such as File name and Time stamp are provided to aid the selection
process.

P7/EN M/02-004A

553

Protection and control device

Logging and recording functions

Disturbance record triggering matrix
With PowerLogic Engineering Suite, navigate to Bay 1 logs > Disturbance
records to set up the disturbance record matrix.
By default, the disturbance recorder is connected to the trip conditioner start signal
to generate a record for any trip. It can also be connected to any other signal in the
matrix to allow triggering for other conditions such as operation of other protection
devices. The triggering signal is selected in the disturbance record triggering
matrix.

Trend log (TRGLOG)
Description
PowerLogic P7 allows a configurable triggered log to record user-defined events
and measurements. The trend log initiates recording upon a configurable trigger
event or a manual trigger by user. Once triggered, the configured data points are
recorded at the designated intervals for a designated period. All recordings are
time-stamped.

Dataset mapping
The data points to be recorded are configured and stored in a dataset. You can
select one of the datasets by dataset mapping to allow recording of the designated
data values.
To select and map a dataset to trend log, go to PowerLogic Engineering Suite and
navigate to Bay 1 logs > Trend logs.
To create a new dataset, navigate to Communication > IEC 61850 > Sent data >
Datasets.
NOTE: The trend log uses the data attributes instead of data objects. When
editing the dataset, map the data point at the data attribute level.

Triggering the recording
The recording can be triggered by trigger event matrix input. The trigger event can
be mapped to binary input, logic output, GOOSE signals, or any other signal
available in the matrix. The recording can also be triggered manually from the
HMI.
To configure the trigger signal in the trigger, go to PowerLogic Engineering Suite
and navigate to Bay 1 logs > Trend logs to set up the trigger.
Record time
The record will start Pre-trigger time before the rise of the input and finish Posttrigger time after the fall of the input or expiration of Pulse time timer or expiration
of the Max record time (whichever occurs first). Setting Pulse time to zero
provides fixed length records with a length of Pre-trigger + Post-trigger times.
The Max record time (TrTms, in ms) is calculated as follows:

P7/EN M/02-004A

555

Protection and control device

Logging and recording functions

æ 13106944 - 256n
ö
TrTms = min ç
´ IntvalSamp - PreTmms, 200000000 - PreTmms ÷
è 8(n + 2) + 2m
ø
E71295A

Where,
n is the number of analog channels mapped in the dataset;
m is the number of digital channels mapped in the dataset;
IntvalSamp is the configured Sample period;
PreTmms is the configured Pre-trigger time.
Number of channels
The number of channels is the number of available analog and digital channels in
the selected dataset. A maximum of100 records are stored with a mix of analog
and binary signals:
•

All analog channels available in the dataset are recorded.

•

All digital channels available in the dataset are recorded regardless of
whether they are active or not.
NOTE: Power systems measurements (e.g. A, V, W, VAr) determined by the
device are provided as per unit values.

All records are stored in non-volatile memory in PowerLogic P7. When storage is
full, the oldest record will be overwritten. (Max storage size is 300 MB).

Reading the recordings
Two files are generated for each recording, a COMTRADE format (.cff) file and a
Comma Separated Value (.csv) file. For the COMTRADE file, the records are
stored in 2013CFF format with Float32 data. This means that other programs can
be used to view and analyze the recordings made by PowerLogic P7.
The available trend records can be selectively downloaded to local PC from
PowerLogic Engineering Suite under Bay 1 logs > Trend logs. Sorting options
such as File name and Time stamp are provided to facilitate the selection process.

Input and output signals

Alarms (CALH)
Description
In the event of abnormal system condition or device failures, PowerLogic P7 will
turn on the alarm LED ! on the front panel and display the alarm icon on the
HMI. Alarms are configured with PowerLogic Engineering Suite under Device
configuration > Alarm.
The alarm function is modelled by Logical Node CALH in IEC 61850 to store the
alarm status, settings and control data objects.
Up to 32 group alarms are available. Each group alarm can be given a category,
severity, label and latch setting.
Up to 128 signals can be mapped to a group alarm. Each mapped signal will
create an individual alarm which inherits all the settings of the group alarm.
NOTE:
•

The number of signals mapped to a group alarm must not exceed 128.
Otherwise the device will enter safe mode.

•

The number of signals mapped to the 32 group alarms must not exceed
2048. Otherwise the device might enter safe mode.

If one or more signals mapped to a group alarm activate, alarm(s) will be raised
with:
•

a header showing the user-defined label, category, and severity (shown as a
colored indicator) of the group alarm.

•

an entry for each individual signal that initiated the group alarm, showing the
signal name and the time of operation. All these entries are collected under
the group alarm header.

Alarms can be selected between self-reset and latched. Latched alarm status can
be reset separately or all together with reset buttons on the HMI or control
commands.

P7/EN M/02-004A

557

Protection and control device

Logging and recording functions

Default alarm mapping
The default mapping includes 12 predefined group alarms. When equipment or
functions are added, the appropriate alarm links will be created automatically. This
ensures correct indication for element operation or alarm conditions.
Retaining these default groups helps ensure any functions added in the future will
have appropriate group alarm indication.

Protection and control device

Monitoring functions
Trip conditioner (PTRC, ANSI 86)
Description
The Trip conditioner function provides the combination of all protection functions’
start outputs and operate outputs to a general start and a general operate
respectively, according to the start and operate I/O mapping of PTRC for each
protection. The general operate will trigger the general trip signal which can be
directly connected to a binary output to trip the breaker.
The trip signal has a settable minimum dwell or can be set to always latch. It also
has a latch input which will force a latched trip if active with an operate input. A
latched trip can be cleared by the reset button, or a matrix or control input
providing the operate signal is not still active.

Trip conditioner matrix
The trip conditioner is used to group all the protection start and operate signals
together to provide an overall start and trip signal for the bay. The start signal is
normally connected to the fault log and disturbance recorder. The operate signal is
also connected to the fault record as well as operating the trip LED. The start and
operate signals directly follow the connected inputs. A trip output is also provided
by the trip condition. This is for connection to circuit breaker trip coils and will
provide a dwell or latch on according to settings and inputs. Setting the Lock out
mode to enable will cause the trip output to latch for any element operation.
The start and operate input signals are linked by default to all the protection
functions which are normally used for tripping. If a link is removed the element will
still function but will not initiate trips through the conditioner. An additional Lockout
input is available which will latch the trip signal if present when the Operate signal
is active. This allows individual protection functions to latch the trip signal.
The block and reset input signals of PTRC can also be connected through the
matrix. The block input will block all PTRC’s outputs. The reset input can reset
lock out mode of PTRC trip output. The PTRC is also reset when the Reset button
is pushed or a SCADA reset is issued.
The PTRC matrix can be configured under Bay 1 protection > Trip > Trip
conditioner from PowerLogic Engineering Suite.

Trip LED
PowerLogic P7 will turn on the trip LED when an operate signal is issued. The
label setting for the trip LED can be configured with PowerLogic Engineering
Suite. The trip LED status is latched and can only be reset via the Reset trips
matrix input or control commands in CALH. By default the matrix input is
connected to the reset button on the front panel.
The trip LED also affects the display. If the display is off when the trip LED turns
on, the display will turn on and show the LED list page. If the display is active
when the trip LED turns on, the display will switch to the LED list page after the
defined screen timeout or a maximum of 15 minutes has elapsed. When the trip
LED is reset, the display will time out if not being used.

P7/EN M/02-004A

Current transformer supervision (CTSSCTR, ANSI 60P/
60N)
Description
The current transformer supervision (CTS) feature is included with each 3ph CT
group and is used to detect failure of a phase current input to the protection
device. Failure of a phase CT or an open circuit of the interconnecting wiring can
result in:
•

Incorrect operation of protection functions

•

High CT secondary voltages

The CT connection types 3ph, 3ph+N and 2ph+N are monitored.
By default the CTS will mark all CT measurements as invalid. This is set via a
matrix link between the CTS block (CTSSCTRx.CTSBlk) and the CT fault
(PHTCTTx.Fault) input. This matrix link can also be removed to allow the blocking
of the selected functions only (elements mapped to CTSBlk). Elements to be
blocked should have a minimum operate delay of at least 50 ms.
CTS should not be applied to CT groups used by the high impedance differential
(HIZPDIF) elements. These elements include dedicated inbuilt CT supervision
functions that are specifically designed for their application.
CTS supports instantaneous operation and provides a Fast block output signal
with no delay. CTS can be applied to the following differential protection elements:
•

Bias differential element (PHPDIF): Only when the Saturation time constant
setting is set above 100 ms.

•

Busbar differential (BBPDIF) (BBPDIF)

•

Line differential element (L2PDIF)

•

Transformer differential elements (TR2PDIF, TR3PDIF: Linked to CTS by
default.

Supervision of the phase currents
The CT failure is detected when the following conditions are simultaneously
present:
•

The magnitude of one phase current falls below 1% In.

•

The magnitude of the other two phase currents is within 5%...120% In.

•

The angle between the other two phase currents is from 110° to 130°. The
angle determination for phase loss detection is listed in the table below.
Table 483 - Angle determination for phase loss detection
Phase
rotation

Loss of phase A

Loss of phase B

Loss of phase C

ABC

110° < φ (IB, IC) < 130°

110° < φ (IC, IA) < 130°

110° < φ (IA, IB) < 130°

ACB

110° < φ (IC, IB) < 130°

110° < φ (IA, IC) < 130°

110° < φ (IB, IA) < 130°

When the CT failure is detected without a blocking condition, the function issues a
block signal after 20 ms and a delayed alarm after the operate delay has elapsed.
The CT failure alarm resets after 10 s if one of the following conditions applies:

564

•

The maximum phase current falls below 10% In.

•

The minimum phase current exceeds 10% In.

P7/EN M/02-004A

Monitoring functions

Protection and control device

Supervision of the neutral current
If the delta IN operation mode INdiffMod is enabled and the CT connection type is
3ph+N, the CTS function triggers when the difference of the calculated neutral
current and the measured neutral current exceeds the set threshold. This should
only be enabled when IN is connected to a residual (Holmgreen) connection of the
three phase CTs.

Blocking conditions
CTS will indicate a Blocked status when not fully functional under the following
conditions:
•

The Inhibit input is present.

•

The CT connection type is 3ph and INdiffMod is enabled.

•

The CT connection type is 2ph+N and INdiffMod is enabled.

•

The CT connection type is 2ph and INdiffMod is enabled.

•

The VN is over the 10% Vn and the quality is valid.

Block diagram
When the CT failure is detected without a blocking condition, the function issues a
block signal and a delayed alarm after the operate delay has elapsed.

Voltage transformer supervision (VTSSVTR, ANSI 60)
Description
The voltage transformer supervision (VTS) feature is included with each 3ph VT
group and is used to detect failure of the voltage inputs to the protection device. If
there is a fuse in the voltage transformer circuitry, the blown fuse prevents or
distorts the voltage measurement. Therefore, a VTS alarm should be issued.
Furthermore, in some applications, protection functions using voltage signals
should be blocked to avoid false tripping.
•

Neutral overvoltage and ground direction using calculated neutral voltage

•

Phase undervoltage

•

Phase direction

•

Directional overpower and underpower

VT failure is detected for the following conditions:
•

Loss of one or two phase voltages (for 2pp+N, 3ph and 3ph+N VT connection
types)
The VTS feature operates on detection of the presence of negative sequence
voltage and the absence of negative sequence current. The following
conditions are simultaneously present:

•

◦

No block V2 input

◦

V2 exceeds the relevant threshold

◦

Bay I2 below the relevant threshold

◦

Maximum bay phase current exceeds the IminStrVal setting

Loss of all three phase voltages under load conditions or upon line
energization (for 2pp+N, 3ph and 3ph+N VT connection types)
When the maximum phase bay current is above IminStrVal but below
ImaxStrVal, and the three phase-to-phase voltages are less than VmaxStrVal,
a VT failure is detected.
NOTE: When ImaxStrVal is set to 0, 3ph loss detection is disabled.

•

Supervision of the neutral voltage (for 3ph+N VT connection type)
If the VN diff mode is enabled, the VTS function triggers when the difference
of the calculated neutral voltage and the measured neutral voltage exceeds
the set threshold.

•

DI for external VT failure indication (for all VT connection types)

When the VT failure is detected without an inhibit input, the function issues a block
signal and a delayed alarm after the operate delay has elapsed.
By default the VTS will mark all VT measurements as invalid. This is set via a
matrix link between the VTS block (VTSSVTRx.VTSBlk) and the VT fault
(PHTVTTx.Fault) input. This link can be removed and selected functions blocked if
required.
The VTS alarm (caused by V2 and loss of all three phase) resets after 10 s if one
of the following conditions applies:

P7/EN M/02-004A

567

Protection and control device

Monitoring functions

•

The inhibit input is present.

•

The maximum phase current falls below the load current and the maximum
phase-to-phase voltage is below VmaxStrVal.

•

The V2 falls below the relevant threshold and the maximum phase-to-phase
voltage is above VmaxStrVal.

Circuit breaker supervision (CBSCBR/CBLSCBR)
Description
Periodic maintenance of Circuit Breakers (CB) is necessary to ensure that the trip
circuit and mechanism operate correctly and the interrupting capability is
uncompromised due to the previous fault interruptions.
The circuit breaker supervision function is included in each CB package and
records various statistics related to each CB operation, allowing an accurate
assessment of the CB condition. Statistics are recorded to allow the evaluation of
both the electrical wear of the breaker contacts and the mechanical wear of the
breaker mechanism.
All statistics are not incremented when PowerLogic P7 is in test mode. The CB
supervision data are managed as follows:
•

Cumulative broken current: not incremented

•

CB open counter: not incremented

•

Protection trip counter: not incremented

•

Operating time (open and close time): recorded

•

Days without operation: unaffected

•

Spring charging time: recorded

CBLSCBR variant (no current logic)
CBLSCBR is a streamlined breaker supervision variant for applications that
operate without local current measurement. The function focuses on breaker
status and mechanical-wear monitoring, and excludes current-dependent
supervision features, such as electrical-wear assessment. By using only
non-current-based supervision, CBLSCBR provides a lightweight option optimized
for bay-control architectures and supports a significantly higher number of breaker
instances within the device compared to CBSCBR.

570

P7/EN M/02-004A

Monitoring functions

Protection and control device

Electrical wear (not applicable to CBLSCBR)
Cumulative broken current
The cumulative broken current function calculates the accumulated fault current
following the trip order input per phase basis if CB is open later. The calculation
formula is ΣI y , the factor y depends on the type of the CB.
NOTE: On the HMI, the cumulative broken current of each phase can be
separately reset to zero or a specified non-zero value.
The greatest broken current of three-phase is used as the operation quantity. If the
operation quantity is greater than the warning threshold, this function will issue an
abrasion warning. If the operation quantity is greater than the alarm threshold, this
function will issue an abrasion alarm.
When the broken current threshold for warning is set to 0, the warning function is
disabled.
When the broken current threshold for alarm is set to 0, the alarm function is
disabled.
When PowerLogic P7 is used with an existing breaker, the cumulative broken
current can be set to the current value.

Mechanical wear
CB open counter
The CB open counter is to record the number of CB close to open.
Protection trip counter
The protection trip counter is to record CB open times issued from global trip.
Open time and close time
•

Open time is the time interval between general trip signal is valid or the
control opening is successful and CB status is open.

•

Close time is the time interval between close signal is valid and CB status is
close.

Days without operation
The days without operation is calculated as the total days the CB remains closed
from its last operation or since PowerLogic P7 power up.
For CB open counter, protection trip counter and open time and close time,
two thresholds can be set on each of them. One threshold is to produce a warning
for maintenance purpose and the other is to produce an alarm for lock out
purpose.
For days without operation, the threshold for alarm can be set.
NOTE: By default these warnings and alarms are linked to the alarm function.
The alarm function activates the Alarm LED and Alarm list on the HMI.

Spring monitoring
The spring monitoring function measures the time taken to charge the CB spring.
After configuring CB not ready in the matrix, the function starts a timer and asserts
Block closing when CB not ready is true, then stops the timer and deasserts Block
closing when CB not ready changes to false.
The elapsed time is recorded as the Spring charging time shown on the HMI under
Control > Bay x > CB x > Supervision > Status. Only the last spring charging
time is available.

P7/EN M/02-004A

571

Protection and control device

Monitoring functions

If the Spring charging time alarm value is configured to a non-zero value, an alarm
is instantly raised when the charging time exceeds this value. This alarm resets
after 10 s.
Timer reset
The spring charge timer and the recorded Spring charging time will be instantly
reset by any of the following events:
•

The Reset spring charging time command is issued.

•

The Pos.quality.source attribute from CBXCBR/CBLXCBR is substituted. This
case indicates the CB truck has been withdrawn (assuming the CB truck
status is mapped to the Inhibit input of CBXCBR/CBLXCBR).

Block diagram
CBLSCBR variant removes current inputs (IA, IB, IC), abrasion estimation,
abrasion warnings/alarms in CBSCBR.

Trip circuit supervision
Description
Trip circuit supervision (TCS) monitors the trip circuit to detect open circuit trip
coils or broken wiring. The trip circuit is monitored by connecting one of the relay
opto inputs in series with the trip coil. Various schemes are available to allow TCS
to work in open or closed position as well as with latched trip contacts.

TCS schemes
Two schemes are available:

576

P7/EN M/02-004A

Monitoring functions

Protection and control device

•

One-input schemes are straightforward and look for an energized input and
sometimes are blocked by the trip signal or breaker position.

•

Two-input schemes can simply provide the same logic as a one-input scheme
with the two optos ORed avoiding the need to block the element for beaker
position or tripping.

Position is provided from the XCBR. The inclusion of breaker position is beneficial
if TCS is arranged as follows.
Figure 281 - Basic TCS scheme

Circuit Breaker
TRIP
52a

TRIP
COIL

Opto

52b

P711E7B

TRIP

Circuit Breaker

TRIP
Blocking
Diode

52a

Opto

52b

TRIP
COIL

P711EKA

In this use case, the TCS will start whenever the trip contact is operated, or the
breaker is open. The TCS mode can be set to Block by state which will block the
TCS if the breaker is open or being opened/tripped. With this setup, the trip circuit
is only monitored when the breaker is closed.
Figure 282 - TCS scheme with single opto and open/closed monitoring

Circuit Breaker
TRIP
52a

TRIP
COIL

Opto

R2

52b

P711E8B

During tripping, this scheme will start but the TCS timer is set to avoid operation.
For latched trips, the TCS mode can be set to Block by trip to avoid operation
when the trip contact is closed.
A second opto will avoid operation during trips. The available TCS schemes are
as follows:

P7/EN M/02-004A

577

Protection and control device

Monitoring functions

•

When the opto is in parallel with the trip contact opto.
Figure 283 - TCS scheme with two optos
Circuit Breaker
TRIP
52a

TRIP
COIL

Opto

Opto

52b

P711E9C

•

When the opto is in series with the trip contact opto.
Figure 284 - TCS scheme with series optos

Circuit Breaker
TRIP
52a

TRIP
COIL

Opto

Opto

52b

P711EAB

The TCS scheme with series optos has the advantage of supervising more of the
circuit in the open state. With this connection both optos may not always pick up
and the de-energized opto will have a voltage drop of up to 5 V. This does not
affect operation but for 24 V schemes the opto pickup ratio should be reduced to
allow for the lower voltage that will be seen by the opto that is energized.
Both of these schemes are not affected by the trip contact operation and will not
be blocked for CB status or tripping, and the TCS mode can be set to On. All of the
above schemes use a single Trip healthy input which can be linked to multiple
opto inputs in the matrix.
Close circuit supervision (CCS) has the same operation as TCS except that CCS
Modes are the inverse of TCS. Block by state will block CCS when closed/closing
and Block by close will block whenever the close command is present. The TCS
can be linked to the CB proxy CB warn input to block closing. TCS and CCS
alarms are added as additional inputs to the CB alarm.

TCS wiring
PowerLogic P7 includes a TCS opto input directly across the first 3 output relays
on the power supply auxiliary module (TIO) in slot Y. These contacts can be used
for tripping (or closing) which has the advantage of supervising the full trip circuit.
It is not mandatory to use these inputs for the TCS function. The output contact
and TCS binary input are independently linked to functions in the matrix. If only the
TCS binary input is required, then TIO.BO1 should not be assigned in the matrix.
•

For breakers with electronic trip circuits.
These inputs are very low burden and normal optos will often not see enough
current for pickup. All binary inputs have a TCS setting to allow operation with
these units. It has a constant current of approximately 1 mA and a nominal
voltage of 19 V.
The settings are susceptible to interference in substation environments. This
generally has little impact on TCS where a delay can be added if required,
however the TCS setting should not be used for any input linked to a
protection function.

578

P7/EN M/02-004A

Monitoring functions

Protection and control device

Resistor values for TCS schemes
Many legacy TCS schemes have a series resistor R1 to limit current to less than
60 mA to avoid tripping if the coil/opto input is shorted. This is extremely unlikely
and is usually only applied for consistency with legacy schemes. In these
schemes the series resistor needs to be bypassed during tripping, so the
dedicated TCS outputs cannot be used. Typical resistor values are shown below.
Figure 285 - TCS scheme with a series resistor R1

Circuit Breaker

voltage requirements.

The standard opto inputs draw a 30 mA pulse and is not suited to series resistors.
A blocking diode can be applied in preference to R2 to allow a single opto to be
used for both open and closed monitoring. As this diode is normally placed
between the opto and the trip contact, the TCS outputs cannot be used for both
tripping and TCS input.

Switch supervision (SWSSWI)
Description
Periodic maintenance of the switch is required to ensure correct operation. Switch
supervision is included with each switch package and includes the following
information related to the switch operation:
•

Number of the switch close to open.

•

Switch open time and close time.

Mechanical wear
Switch open counter
The switch open counter is to record the operate number of the switch close to
open.
NOTE: The supervision value of the switch open counter can be set to a
custom value when PowerLogic P7 is used to replace a legacy IED that
monitors a switch in service.
Switch open time and close time
•

Switch open time is the time interval between the open signal is valid and the
switch status is open.

•

Switch close time is the time interval between the close signal is valid and the
switch status is close.
NOTE: Both of the switch open time and the switch close time can be reset.

All statistics are not incremented when PowerLogic P7 is in test mode. The SW
supervision data are managed as follows:
•

SW open counter: not incremented

•

Operating time (open and close time): recorded

On each of the switch supervision data, two thresholds can be set . One is to
produce a warning for maintenance purpose, the other is to produce an alarm for
lock out purpose. Each of these warnings and alarms will raise a switch
maintenance status information.

582

P7/EN M/02-004A

Monitoring functions

Battery monitoring (ZBAT)
Description
The Battery monitoring function is used to monitor the DC voltage of
PowerLogic P7 and helps to detect issues such as battery charger failures.
The Battery monitoring function provides four alarm signals when the auxiliary DC
voltage input exceeds or falls below the corresponding set thresholds.

Bay status (PDGAPC)
Description
The bay status function gives an indication of whether all phases of the line are
dead. A bay-dead condition is determined by monitoring the status of the
CBclosed input and by evaluating measured phase currents and phase-to-phase
voltages.
If any phase current quality is invalid, BayDead is forced to false and its quality is
set to invalid.
Otherwise, BayDead is asserted when either of the following conditions is true; if
neither condition is true, BayDead remains false.
•

All current qualities are valid, CBclosed is inactive, and none of the phase
currents exceeds the current threshold.

•

All current and voltage qualities are valid, none of the three phase currents
exceeds the current threshold, none of the three phase-to-phase voltages
exceeds the voltage threshold, and this condition persists for more than
20 ms.
NOTE:
•

It is recommended to configure the CBclosed input and connect it via
matrix to all CB position signals in the bay. When all connected CB
positions are open, CBclosed is set to inactive.

•

If CBclosed is not configured via matrix, CBclosed defaults to inactive.

Bay dead matrix
With PowerLogic Engineering Suite, locate to the Bay 1 configuration > Bay
dead to set up the bay dead matrix.
Configure the CB closed input to all CB positions in the bay through the bay dead
matrix. When there is no CB in the bay, an opto or other signal representing the
bay alive information can be used as the CB closed input in the matrix. If no CB
closed signal is available then the bay dead indication is based on the bay current
and voltage measurements.

586

Temperature (RTDTTMP)
Description
This function gives the temperature value measured by Resistance Temperature
Detectors (RTDs):
•

Platinum Pt100 (100 Ω at 0 °C or 32 °F) or nickel (Ni100 or Ni120) type RTDs
, in accordance with the IEC 60751 and DIN 43760 standards.

PowerLogic P7 supports two MET148-2 module with up to 16 RTD channels. This
module communicates with PowerLogic P7 through the Controller Area Network
(CAN) link. Ambient is fixed to RTD number 8 on the first MET module.
The function also indicates RTD faults:
•

RTD disconnected (t > 201 °C or t > 393.8 °F)

•

RTD shorted (t < -31 °C or t < -23.8 °F).

Each output signal of the temperature measurement for RTD fault indicates one of
four meanings:
1. RTD measurement is in normal range.
2. Short Circuit (t < -31 °C or t < -23.8 °F).
3. Open Circuit (t > 201 °C or t > 393.8 °F).
4. RTD board is disconnected.
In the event of a fault, the display of the value is set to -32768 for open circuit and
32767 for short circuit with invalid quality. The associated monitoring function
generates a failure status.
The measurements and RTD health can be accessed via:
•

the HMI, under General > Device config > Slot A > RTDs.
NOTE: When the STMP function is enabled, the measurements are
shown on the HMI under Measurements > Bay x > Temperature. The
RTD name and function can be set with STMP.

•

the communication link.

Output signals
Table 516 - Output signals of the RTDTTMP

588

Current loop (mA) input (CLTGSN)
Description
The current loop (mA) input function is used together with the Current Loop Input/
Output (CLIO) module to accommodate a variety of transducer types and
measurement ranges.
Four current loop inputs are provided. These inputs interface with different types
of transducers that monitor and control systems. Various transducers include rotor
earth resistance measurement units, tap position indicators, vibration monitors,
tachometers and pressure transducers.
The supported ranges on the device are:

P7/EN M/02-004A

•

0-1 mA

•

0-10 mA

•

0-20 mA

•

4-20 mA

•

4-22 mA

589

Protection and control device

Monitoring functions

Function overview
•

Linear current conversion: The CLTGSN performs a linear conversion on the
small current signals received from the CLIO module, presenting the channel
input values along with the converted channel values. The converted values
can be used in programmable stage.

•

Connection and communication status: The function verifies the connection
between the device interface and the CLIO module, reporting the channel
communication health.

•

Input range specification: Provides the specific range selection for CLIO
module inputs as well as the corresponding range of linear converted values.
The health of mA inputs are evaluated based on the specified input range.

•

Control of CLIO module input channels: Provides the management of four
input channels available on a CLIO module, each channel is associated with
a dedicated CLTGSN.

•

Definition of value units and descriptions: A label can be provided for the
values obtained after the linear conversion process. The label should include
any units and/or multipliers. For example, Resistance (kohm) or Pressure
(hPa).

On the HMI, the status and settings of each current loop input can be viewed at:
General > Device config > Slot A > mA inputs

The linear relationship between Measured value (InVal) and Calculated value
(OutVal) is shown as follows:

NOTE: If the Max set is less than the Min, the slope will be negative. This is
because the mathematical relationship remains the same irrespective of how
they are set. For example, for 4-20 mA range, Max corresponds to 20 mA and
Min corresponds to 4 mA.
The range of Min and Max values is constrained between -9999 and +9999 in
0.001 steps to allow the converted value to be displayed on the mimic.
NOTE: To display larger or smaller values, scale the input and display the
factor in the label. For example, an hours run indication can be scaled in days,
weeks, months, years or ×100 hours.

Channel input for different ranges
The quality of the input signal will depend on the input range.
•

Within 20% of the expected range, the input and the converted output will be
marked as questionable.

•

Above 20% range, it will be marked as invalid.
NOTE: Where the range starts at zero, the measurement is questionable
above -0.1 mA and invalid below this value.

•

Between Min and Max values, the input and output values have good quality.
NOTE: If communications to the CLIO module is lost, the measurements will
report zero with invalid quality.

Channel supervision
In addition to changing the measurement quality, two health signals are also
available.

P7/EN M/02-004A

•

CLIOFail indicates whether communication is available to the module
providing the input channel measurement. If the communication is healthy,
CLIOFail is OK, otherwise it is Alarm.

•

EEHealth indicates the health of mA signal being measured. It follows the
measurement quality indicating a warning if the measurement is questionable
and an alarm if the measurement is invalid.

Current loop (mA) output (CLGGIO)
Description
The current loop (mA) output function works with the CLIO module to output
analog measurements from PowerLogic P7. This reduces the need for separate
transducers and enables long distance transmission less susceptible to electrical
noise.
Each output is a current signal within a defined range (typically 4...20 mA) that can
be fed into standard moving coil ammeters or sent to a SCADA via an analog RTU
(Remote Terminal Unit).
The device provides four current loop (mA) output channels. Each channel can be
individually enabled or disabled, and can be configured with output ranges of 0-1
mA, 0-10 mA, 0-20 mA or 4-20 mA.
With PowerLogic Engineering Suite, an analog measurement in the device can be
mapped to Input MV of a channel. Input minimum, Input maximum and Label
settings define the range and unit of the analog measurement input. The mapped
analog measurement is then converted to Calculated mA ouput.
On the HMI, the channel input, output, output health, and CLIO health can be
viewed under General > Device config > Slot A > mA ouputs > Status.

P7/EN M/02-004A

593

Protection and control device

Monitoring functions

Output value calculation
If the channel is set to On and Input MV is mapped to a valid analog
measurement, the function calculates the Calculated mA ouput with the following
formula:

OutVal = ( AnIn - Min) ´

Rtg max - Rtg min
+ Rtg min
Max - Min
E71207A

Where,
•

OutVal is the Calculated mA ouput. If the input quality is invalid, OutVal is
zero.

•

AnIn is the analog measurement mapped to Input MV.
NOTE: Power systems measurements (e.g. A, V, W, VAr etc.) determined
by the device are provided as per unit values. For other measurements
(e.g. Hz, °C etc.) the actual value is used.

•

Min is the Input minimum setting. This needs to consider whether the input is
a per unit or actual value.

•

Max is the Input maximum setting. If the range of an actual measurement
exceeds the maximum setting, a programmable stage can be used to bring
the input within the required range.

•

Rtgmin is the minimum value of the output’s Range, for example 4 mA for the
4-20 mA range

•

Rtgmax is the maximum value of the output’s Range, for example 20 mA for
the 4-20 mA range
NOTE: If Min and Max are set to the same value, their difference is treated as
0.001 in the formula above.

Output health
The mA output health indicates the health of the mA signal being output. It has
three states: Ok, Warning, and Alarm, which correspond to Good, Questionable,
and Invalid of the Calculated mA output quality.

Programmable stage (PSGAPC)
Description
The programmable stage function is used to map any measured input values from
measurements to dedicated protection or monitoring stages.
The function allows the stage to perform the defined mathematical calculation on
the measured input value from Input 1 (InVal1) and a reference value. The
reference value can be a fixed value from the setting Fixed value (FixVal) or
another measured input Input 2 (InVal2). The calculated value can then be used
as a measurement with range outputs or a threshold can be added to operate at a
certain value.
To select the status signal to be mapped to Input 1 or Input 2, go to Bay 1
protection > Application > Programmable stage in PowerLogic Engineering
Suite.
Thresholds are enabled by selecting the Start mode as over or under. The stage
then compares the Calculated value against the Start value. The Start signal is
issued when the Calculated value is over or under the Start value. The Operate
signal is issued when the Start signal is active and the Operation delay has
elapsed. A settable hysteresis is available which holds the Start signal until the
calculated value has returned to an operational level.
The mathematical calculation is defined by the setting Operation mode (OpMod).
The mathematical calculations available for the measured input value are as
follows:

596

•

Input 1

•

Input 1 + Reference

P7/EN M/02-004A

Monitoring functions

Protection and control device

•

Input 1 - Reference

•

Reference - Input 1

•

|Input 1 - Reference|

•

Input 1 × Reference

•

Input 1 / Reference

•

Reference / Input 1
NOTE: Power systems measurements (e.g. A, V, W, VAr) determined by the
device are provided as per unit values. For other measurements (e.g. Hz, °C),
the actual value is used.

P7/EN M/02-004A

Maintenance

Protection and control device

Maintenance
PowerLogic P7 devices together with communication accessories and cabling,
require maintenance in work according to their specification. Keep a record of the
maintenance actions. The maintenance can include, but is not limited to, the
following actions.

Safety instructions
This page contains important safety instructions that must be followed precisely
before attempting to install, repair, service or maintain electrical equipment.
Carefully read and follow the safety instructions described below.

DANGER
HAZARD OF ELECTRIC SHOCK, EXPLOSION OR ARC FLASH
•

NEVER work alone.

•

Apply appropriate personal protective equipment (PPE) and follow safe
electrical work practices.

•

Turn off all power supplying this equipment before working on or inside it.

•

Verify that all power is off using a properly rated voltage sensing device.

•

Protect the ring-type terminal blocks with the appropriate IP20 cover after
cabling.

Failure to follow these instructions will result in death or serious injury.
NOTE: Consider all sources of power when turning off the power supply,
including the possibility of backfeeding.

DANGER
FIRE HAZARD
•

Apply proper tightening torque to all wire connections.

•

Disconnect the power supply before removing or replacing a module.

•

Never touch electronics parts.

•

In case of module replacement, perform the commissioning operations
before using the protection and control device.

Failure to follow these instructions will result in death or serious injury.

WARNING
UNINTENDED DEVICE OPERATION
•

Verify that the terminal blocks are restored into the correct location after
removing them for wiring or testing.

•

Verify that the modules are secured with retaining screws.

Failure to follow these instructions can result in death, serious injury, or
equipment damage.

P7/EN M/02-004A

599

Protection and control device

Maintenance

CAUTION
RISK OF SETTING CHANGES ERASED
Never plug in or draw out the communication modules while PowerLogic P7 is
in service.
Failure to follow these instructions can result in injury or equipment
damage.

Self-monitoring
Watchdog relay
PowerLogic P7 is equipped with a watchdog relay (in Slot Z). This is a changeover
relay which is kept permanently in the on-position by the device. In the event of
device failure, or when the auxiliary power supply fails, the watchdog relay reverts
to the off position.
The watchdog relay will be triggered (de-energized) by a detected internal
hardware/firmware issue or by a configured matrix input (Device configuration >
Slot Y+Z > Binary outputs in PowerLogic Engineering Suite). The watchdog
relay will only be reset when the hardware/firmware issue is removed and the
configured matrix input is FALSE. When the watchdog is triggered (de-energized),
the maintenance LED
on the front panel will be illuminated (LD0/LPHD1.
PhyHealth = Alarm) and the watchdog trigger information will be indicated on the
HMI.

Maintenance LED
The maintenance LED
(see Physical LEDs on the front panel) is on when any
of the following cases occurs:
•

The embedded self-tests detected a hardware or firmware issue. In this case
the device is no longer fully operational.

•

The device is in Test or Test-blocked mode. In this case the LED will be
flashing.

•

The device is in Simulation mode. In this case the LED will be flashing.

•

The watchdog relay is triggered.

•

The device is in Safe mode. In this case the device is no longer operational.

•

The device is being energized during start-up. This case is normal and does
not indicate any internal failure. When the start-up phase of the device is
completed, the LED is off if no internal failure is detected.

When the maintenance LED
is on, the
icon will appear on the top right
corner of the HMI (when fitted). Tap this icon to view the current device
maintenance information.
NOTE: The maintenance status is also indicated on the rear LEDs. See
Physical LEDs on the CPU board, page 152.

Self-tests
PowerLogic P7 runs a series of self-diagnostic tests for hardware and software in
boot sequence and also performs runtime check. These self-tests detect any
failure and can avoid random PowerLogic P7 behavior. The main purpose is to
avoid an unwanted tripping or failure to trip in the event that it occurs on the power
system or on the equipment to protect.

600

P7/EN M/02-004A

Maintenance

Protection and control device

PowerLogic P7 can detect two types of internal failure:
•

A major failure when the protection functions cannot be processed properly or
can initiate an unwanted trip. In this case, PowerLogic P7 will go into the
fallback position:
◦

The output relays are in the off position.

◦

Watchdog relay (on slot Z) goes into the off position.

◦

! LED,

LED, and the virtual configurable LEDs are off.

◦

LED of the front panel is on.

◦

LED of the front panel is on.
NOTE: Tap
on HMI to go to the maintenance panel which shows
the HW/FW healthy status.

•

◦

The communication is inoperative.

◦

The communication with PowerLogic Engineering Suite is inactive
depending on the type of internal failure.

A minor defect is a malfunction of components (hardware or firmware) that
are not directly used by protection functions (i.e. memory, used for logging
emergency events, communication channels, etc.). It shall not result in loss of
PowerLogic P7 operability.
◦

The output relays stay in their current status.

◦

Watchdog relay (on slot Z) stays energized.

◦

! LED,

LED, and the virtual configurable LEDs stay in their current

position.
◦

LED of the front panel is on.

◦

LED of the front panel is off.

◦

An alarm message is displayed on the front panel to highlight the origin of
the issue.
NOTE: Press the
button to remove the message. The alarm
message is logged in the Alarm message list.

◦

The communication is still operational depending on the type of the
internal failure.

◦

The communication with PowerLogic Engineering Suite, on the front panel
is active depending on the type of internal failure.

List of self-tests
The self-tests are described in the table below.

P7/EN M/02-004A

601

Protection and control device

Maintenance

IED mode
PowerLogic P7 has following main modes:
•

Normal mode

•

Simulation mode

•

Safe mode

•

Default mode

Normal mode
In Normal mode, the device has several sub-modes:
•

On: The device normally works in this mode.

•

Test: When LD0/LLN0.Mod is set to Test, the device works in this mode and
the binary output relays can still operate.

•

Test-blocked: When LD0/LLN0.Mod is set to Test-blocked, the device
works in this mode and the binary output relays will not operate.
NOTE: These modes affect all the functions of the device, not only all the
functions under the LD0, but also the functions under the BayLD.

The device can be placed in On/Test/Test-blocked control mode by selecting the
mode from the HMI under > Control > Device mode.
The device can also be placed in On/Test/Test-blocked control mode by a binary
input signal via matrix. Go to General > System info in PowerLogic Engineering
Suite.
Table 531 - Related input signals under the LD0LLN0
Name

Short label

Infotip

ModTestIn

Test mode

Sets device in test mode, if
Block mode is also active

Device mode

Simulation mode
The Simulation mode allows the device to respond to GOOSE or SV messages
marked as simulation. In this mode, the device continues to process normal
GOOSE or SV messages until a simulated GOOSE or SV message is received for
a subscription.
The device can be placed in Simulation mode by setting Simulation accepted to
On (LD0/LPHD1.Sim = True) from the HMI under > Control > Device mode.
The device can also be placed in Simulation mode by a binary input signal via
matrix. Go to General > System info in PowerLogic Engineering Suite.

602

P7/EN M/02-004A

Maintenance

Table 534 - Related output signals under the LPHD

Simulation accepted

If simulated GOOSE or SV are
received they will be accepted
and process messages
ignored

Safe mode
The device enters Safe mode when supervision initialization fails or fatal
protection error occurs. In Safe mode, the device is no longer operational and all
the output relays are forced into OFF position, thus the power system is no longer
protected. If the auxiliary power supply is lost, the output relays are also in OFF
position.
When the device is in Safe mode, the HMI still allows maintenance work such as
viewing the build version, checking error causes, and performing a factory reset
from the maintenance panel (see Performing factory reset, page 604). Web server
remains available for updating configuration files and switching back to Normal
mode using PowerLogic Engineering Suite.

Default mode
After a factory reset the relay will restart in Default mode. In this mode, the HMI
home screen will indicate that default configuration is currently active.
When the device is in Normal mode, it can be put in Default mode by performing
factory reset operation from the HMI after a reboot (see Performing factory reset,
page 604). Once any setting is changed or the configuration file is downloaded,
the device will return to Normal mode.
The device mode information including watchdog, protection, binary outputs, front
panel and communication status are shown as follows:

Performing factory reset
The factory reset function is used to restore the default configuration, settings, and
access control. After a factory reset, the IED enters Default mode. For details
about IED mode, see IED mode, page 602. For authorization information, see
User accounts and rights, page 136.
Three methods are available to perform the factory reset operation:
•

from the login page

•

from the maintenance panel

•

from the menu
NOTE: By default, only the INSTALLER role can perform a factory reset. If a
Role error appears, log in with INSTALLER or a role granted the factory reset
right.

Performing factory reset from the login page
To perform factory reset from the login page:
1. From Login page, tap Forgot your password?.
2. From the pop-up window, tap Confirm.
Forgot your password?
Running a factory reset will allow you to log
in with the default password.

reboot the device or tap Cancel to cancel the factory reset operation.

NOTE: A 5-minute timer is started to permit to cancel the factory reset
function. If timed out, the function is cancelled automatically and the
device remains the original mode; otherwise, the device can be manually
rebooted and will start in Default mode.

Protection and control device

Maintenance

Preventive maintenance
DANGER
HAZARD OF ELECTRIC SHOCK, EXPLOSION, OR ARC FLASH
•

Apply appropriate personal protective equipment (PPE) and follow safe
electrical work practices.

•

Do not use the arc fault system as a substitute for proper PPE when working
on or near equipment being monitored by the system.

•

Only qualified personnel can install and service this equipment. Read this
entire set of instructions and check the technical characteristics of the device
before performing such work.

•

Perform wiring according to national standards (NEC) and any requirements
specified by the customer.

•

Observe any separately marked notes and warnings.

•

NEVER work alone.

•

Before performing visual inspections, tests, or maintenance on this
equipment, disconnect all sources of electric power.

•

Assume all circuits are live until they are completely de-energized, tested,
and tagged.

•

Pay particular attention to the design of the power system.

•

Always use a properly rated voltage sensing relay to ensure that all power is
off.

•

Properly ground the equipment.

•

Connect the device's protective ground to functional earth according to the
connection diagrams presented in this document.

•

Do not physically open the device.

•

Install all components before powering this device.

Failure to follow these instructions will result in death or serious injury.
NOTE:
•

See NFPA 70E, NOM-029-STPS-2011, or CSAZ462 for more information
about safe electrical work practices.

•

Consider all sources of power when turning off power supply, including
the possibility of backfeeding.

WARNING
UNINTENDED EQUIPMENT OPERATION
•

Periodically perform system testing according to the testing recommendation
in this manual.

•

Perform system testing after changing the protection system scheme.

Failure to follow these instructions can result in death, serious injury, or
equipment damage.
To obtain the maximum availability of the installation, it is essential to ensure that
PowerLogic P7 is operational at any time.
PowerLogic P7's internal self-tests, the watchdog relay, and the
LED alert the
user in case of internal device failure. However, elements outside PowerLogic P7
are not subject to the self-tests. Therefore, it is necessary to perform regular
preventive maintenance.
Visually check PowerLogic P7 and pay attention to dirty components, loose wire
connections, damaged wiring, user interface screen and LEDs, and other
mechanical connections.

606

P7/EN M/02-004A

Maintenance

Protection and control device

Then, perform all the recommended commissioning tests. First test all the binary
inputs and outputs involved in tripping the circuit breaker. A test of the complete
chain including the circuit breaker is also recommended.

Recommended intervals
Test PowerLogic P7 periodically according to the end user's safety instructions
and national safety instructions or law.
The necessary time between visual inspections and functional checks depends on
the installation operating conditions. Generally, we recommend to carry out
periodic checks or tests every six (6) years. In corrosive or harsh offshore
environments, functional testing should be carried out more often; we recommend
every three (3) years.
For the testing procedures, see Commissioning, page 106.
The table below summarizes the recommended intervals.
Test

Recommended intervals

User interface check

1 year

Relay health check

6 years

Date and time synchronization check

6 years

Wiring and connections check

6 years

Analog inputs operation check

6 years

Binary inputs wiring and operation check

6 years

Binary outputs wiring and operation check

6 years

Preventive maintenance tasks
Checking device health

CAUTION
UNINTENDED EQUIPMENT OPERATION
Periodically check the health condition of the device according to the site
maintenance plan.
Failure to follow these instructions can result in injury or equipment
damage.
•

Check that the different currents and voltages measured by PowerLogic P7
are appropriate for the load being powered.

•

Check that PowerLogic P7
LED is off and no maintenance message
displayed on the front panel.

•

Check the health of boards and modules. See Slot health, page 111 for more
information.

Checking date and time synchronization
Check the status of main synchronization source and backup synchronization
source from the HMI.

P7/EN M/02-004A

607

Protection and control device

Maintenance

Checking the wiring and connection

DANGER
HAZARD OF ELECTRIC SHOCK, EXPLOSION OR ARC FLASH
Turn off all power supply to the equipment before working.
Failure to follow these instructions will result in death or serious injury.
•

Check that the connections, including the ground terminal and the CT
connections, are tight and free from corrosion.
If the CT connections are not tightened properly, this generates excessive
heat rise which can lead to the destruction of CT/VT analog input module
connector and the CTs.

•

Check the wiring connections in the rear panel: for the type of screws,
screwdriver to be used, and torque (see Installation, page 45).
In addition to the periodic wiring and connections check, a thermal
measurement with thermal camera is recommended to make visible any
warm points of the installation so that loose connections could be detected
and corrected before a real issue occurs.

•

Check the ground connection.

Checking analog input operation
Check current transformers (CTs), voltage transformers (VTs), and, if used,
CBCTs connection. Check that measured analog values and angles are correct
on the front panel.

Checking binary input wiring and operation
•

Check that physical inputs states are correct, according to normal input
configuration and wirings.

•

Check binary inputs status from the HMI. See Slot health, page 111Binary
inputs, page 110 for more information.

Checking binary output wiring and operation
•

If accessible, check physical binary outputs position (continuity tester).

•

Check binary outputs status from the HMI. See Binary outputs , page 111 for
more information.

Checking the trip chain
Check regularly that the complete trip chain, from the CTs to PowerLogic P7 and
through to the trip coil, is always operational.
The complete protection chain is validated during the simulation of a fault that
causes tripping of the switchgear by PowerLogic P7. Simply testing one function
can demonstrate that the whole system is working correctly, provided it has been
installed correctly.
To validate the complete protection chain, proceed as follows:
1. Select one of the protection functions that trips the switchgear.
2. Depending on the function(s) selected, inject a current or a voltage
corresponding to a fault and note whether the switchgear trips.

Firmware update
WARNING
UNINTENDED DEVICE OPERATION
Take the device out of service before upgrading or downgrading the firmware.
Failure to follow these instructions can result in death, serious injury, or
equipment damage.

NOTICE
LOSS OF DATA
Save configuration files, disturbance records and events before upgrading or
downgrading the firmware.
Failure to follow these instructions can result in permanent loss of data.

P7/EN M/02-004A

611

Protection and control device

Maintenance

NOTICE
EQUIPMENT DAMAGE
Ensure that the power supply is not interrupted during the firmware upgrade or
downgrade process.
Failure to follow these instructions can result in non-functional device.

NOTICE
UNINTENDED EQUIPMENT OPERATION AND NUISANCE TRIPPING
After writing new firmware, configurations or settings to a protection and control
device, perform a test to verify that the protection and control device operates
correctly with the updated parameters.
Failure to follow these instructions can result in unwanted shutdown of
the electrical installation.
PowerLogic P7 allows upgrading or downgrading the firmware of the device with
PowerLogic Engineering Suite.
Reasons for upgrading the firmware:
•

New functionality

•

Security patches

•

Correction of product behavior

Reasons for downgrading the firmware:
•

Device homologation

•

Device configuration compatibility

Updating the firmware
Prerequisites:
•

The device is not in service status.

•

The PC is connected to your device. See Connection modes, page 158.

•

Device credentials. See Default password setting, page 146.

•

Firmware package (.zip).
NOTE: Visit the Schneider Electric website (www.se.com) to get the
firmware package.

To install the firmware package with PowerLogic Engineering Suite:
1. From the Project page, select

> Update device firmware.

2. Select the firmware package (.zip) from your PC.
3. Configure the connection settings from the prompt, and select Connect.
4. If prompted, enter device credentials, and select Authenticate.
5. Confirm the prompted Information comparison .
– Select OK to proceed. Go to Step 6.
– Select Disconnect to exit.
NOTE: Connection is not allowed for unmatched device model
(incompatible order code/data model).

612

P7/EN M/02-004A

Maintenance

Protection and control device

6. After a successful connection, a prompt will remind you to save the
configuration.
– Select Ignore and continue to start sending the firmware package to the
device. Go to Step 7.
– Select Save to exit and switch to synchronization process. See
Synchronizing parameters, page 162.
7. When the prompt indicates Firmware files sent, select Next to proceed.
Firmware update starts automatically on the device.
NOTE: The device will reboot during this process. Do not interrupt the
power supply of the device during this period.
8. When the prompt indicates device availability, confirm the current version, and
select Close to complete the process.
After a successful firmware update, the HMI of the rebooted device will indicate
Default mode, which means the device is written with the new firmware with
default settings.
Post-update steps:
1. Verify the firmware version from the HMI (

> General > System info).

2. Restore the settings from the saved configuration files or by configuration
conversion.
For more details, see Converting device configuration, page 613.
3. Perform functional tests after firmware update.

Converting device configuration
PowerLogic P7 allows converting the existing data model configuration from
current firmware version to a later firmware version with PowerLogic Engineering
Suite.
Prerequisites:
•

PowerLogic Engineering Suite is logged in.

•

A data model based on the target firmware version is available on
PowerLogic Engineering Suite.

To convert the data model configuration to a different firmware version:
1. From project page, select

> Convert device configuration.

2. Select the target firmware version from drop down, and select Convert.
If the desired firmware version is not in the drop down list, upload the data
model from > Modules & Licenses > Data Models.
3. When the prompt indicates Operation completed, select Close to complete
the process.
The data model configuration is successfully converted to the target firmware
version.
If the prompt indicates Operation failed instead, the configuration conversion is
failed. Select Show logs for details.

License update
The main application and application level of the device can be changed by
updating the license file with PowerLogic Engineering Suite. Depending on the
ordered device option, the license update can be performed via the Mini-USB/
DHMI port or Port 1/2/3.

P7/EN M/02-004A

613

Protection and control device

Maintenance

NOTE:
•

Higher level devices cannot be downgraded to a lower level (e.g. L1 to
L0).

•

Contact your local Schneider Electric sales representative to get the
license file or firmware package.

Updating device license to a higher level
This procedure applies when the device license is updated to a higher level, but
the main application is unchanged (e.g. L0 Motor to L1 Motor).
NOTE: The device configuration is retained in this procedure.
Prerequisites:
•

The device is not in service.

•

A new license file. The serial number of this license file must match the
device.

•

A PowerLogic Engineering Suite account with the Installer or Application
administrator role.

•

A device account with the ENGINEER role.

•

If Port 1/2/3 are used, the web server logical port needs to be enabled.

To update the device license:
1. Connect the device to PowerLogic Engineering Suite with accounts listed in
prerequisites.
2. In device view, click > Update device license.
3. In the pop-up window, import the new license file.
4. After the prompt Check Completed appears, click Next.
5. Confirm the update summary, and click Send.
The new license file will be written into the device. When this process finishes,
the device will reboot .
NOTE: When updating the license for devices with V02.002.002 or earlier
firmware, PowerLogic Engineering Suite will show License Update Failed
in Step 5, and the device will not automatically reboot.
Manually reboot the device to finish the license update.

Updating device license to another application

NOTICE
LOSS OF DATA
Save configuration files, disturbance records and events before updating the
license.
Failure to follow these instructions can result in permanent loss of data.
This procedure applies when the device license is updated to any of the following:
•

Another application (e.g. L0 Motor to L0 Generator)

•

Another application and a higher level (e.g. L0 Motor to L1 Generator)
NOTE: The device will load the default configuration of the target application
in this procedure.

Prerequisites:
•

614

The device is not in service.

P7/EN M/02-004A

Maintenance

Protection and control device

•

A new license file. The serial number of this license file must match the
device.

•

A firmware package that is the same version as currently installed on the
device.

•

A PowerLogic Engineering Suite account with the Installer or Application
administrator role.

•

A device account with the ENGINEER role.

•

If Port 1/2/3 are used, the web server logical port needs to be enabled.

To update the device license:
1. Connect the device to PowerLogic Engineering Suite with accounts listed in
prerequisites.
2. In device view, click > Update device license.
3. In the pop-up window, import the new license file.
4. After the prompt Check Completed appears, click Next.
5. Import the firmware package.
6. After the prompt Check Completed appears, click Next.
7. Confirm the update summary, and click Send.
The new license file and default configuration will be written into the device.
When this process finishes, the device will reboot.
NOTE: When updating the license for devices with V02.002.002 or earlier
firmware, PowerLogic Engineering Suite will show License Update Failed
in Step 7, and the device will not automatically reboot.
Manually reboot the device to finish the license update.
After the update, the device will have the new application with default settings
and will be disconnected from the current PowerLogic Engineering Suite
device, which is no longer compatible with the new application.
A compatible configuration should then be loaded to the device.

Replacing the modules
Identification of the modules
PowerLogic P7 is a modular protection and control device. The modules fitted on
all slots can be replaced individually.
To access the identification of the modules:
•

From the HMI of PowerLogic P7, expand > General > Device config to
locate to the desired slot, navigate to Version info or Slot info.

•

From PowerLogic Engineering Suite, expand Device configuration to locate
to the desired slot, navigate to Basic info.
NOTE: Slot A represents the CPU module.

1. Power off PowerLogic P7.
2. Check if there is enough space (> 35 cm or 13.78 in) to extract the module. If
there is not enough space, remove the device from its mounting.
3. Check if the external signals are disconnected from the device.
4. Remove the wires, external modules, SFP modules and serial port terminal
block connected to CPU module (see Changing a SFP module, page 621 for
removing SFP modules).

Replacing other modules

DANGER
HAZARD OF ELECTRIC SHOCK, EXPLOSION OR ARC FLASH
•

NEVER work alone.

•

Apply appropriate personal protective equipment (PPE) and follow safe
electrical work practices.

•

Turn off all power supplying this equipment before working on or inside it.

•

Verify that all power is off using a properly rated voltage sensing device.

•

Protect the ring-type terminal blocks with the appropriate IP20 cover after
cabling.

Failure to follow these instructions will result in death or serious injury.
NOTE: Consider all sources of power when turning off the power supply,
including the possibility of backfeeding.

DANGER
FIRE HAZARD
•

Apply proper tightening torque to all wire connections.

•

Disconnect the power supply before removing or replacing a module.

•

Never touch electronics parts.

•

In case of module replacement, perform the commissioning operations
before using the protection and control device.

Failure to follow these instructions will result in death or serious injury.

removing them for wiring or testing.

•

Verify that the modules are secured with retaining screws.

equipment damage.

•

CUTS AND PHYSICAL IMPACT
Wear gloves when handling modules during replacement.
Failure to follow these instructions can result in injury or equipment
damage.

NOTICE
EQUIPMENT DAMAGE DUE TO ELECTROSTATIC DISCHARGE (ESD)
Wear an ESD wrist strap to keep grounded before replacing the board.
Failure to follow these instructions can result in equipment damage.

NOTICE
EQUIPMENT DAMAGE
During the first power-on after the replacement, ensure that the power supply of
the device is uninterrupted for at least 5 minutes to allow system update and
reboot.
Failure to follow these instructions can result in a non-functional device.

Returning for expert assessment
To return PowerLogic P7 for expert assessment, use the original packaging if
possible.
PowerLogic P7 must be returned accompanied by its settings sheet and the
following information:
•

Name and address of the initiator

•

PowerLogic P7 type and serial number

•

Date of the incident

•

Detailed description of the incident

•

LED status and message displayed at the time of the incident

•

List of stored events

•

Repair and Modification Authorization (RMA) form which is from the country
organization

End of life
At end of life of PowerLogic P7, Schneider Electric's Environmental Data Program
helps decrease the amount of waste and allow recovery of product components
and materials. Refer to the Product Environmental Profile and the End of Life
instructions available on the Schneider Electric website: www.se.com. The
instructions can also be accessed from the PowerLogic P7 landing page by
scanning the product QR code, see Equipment identification, page 49.
Dispose the device according to the legislation of the country when
decommissioning the device. Proper disposal helps prevent potential disclosure of
data contained in the device that was not removed.

NOTICE
UNAUTHORIZED OR UNINTENDED ACCESS TO CONFIDENTIAL DATA
•

Verify that all data from the device was saved and erased.

•

Perform a factory reset to recover the device's default configuration.

Failure to follow these instructions can result in permanent loss of data.

Safety information

Protection and control device

Safety information
Important information
Read these instructions carefully and look at the equipment to become
familiar with the device before trying to install, operate, service or maintain
it. The following special messages may appear throughout this manual or
on the equipment to warn of potential hazards or to call attention to
information that clarifies or simplifies a procedure.
The addition of either symbol to a “Danger” or “Warning” safety label indicates that an
electrical hazard exists which will result in personal injury if the instructions are not
followed.
This is the safety alert symbol. It is used to alert you to potential personal injury
hazards. Obey all safety messages that follow this symbol to avoid possible injury or
death.

DANGER
DANGER indicates a hazardous situation which, if not avoided, will result in
death or serious injury.
Failure to follow these instructions will result in death or serious injury.

WARNING
WARNING indicates a hazardous situation which, if not avoided, can result in
death or serious injury.

CAUTION
CAUTION indicates a hazardous situation which, if not avoided, can result in
minor or moderate injury.

NOTICE
NOTICE is used to address practices not related to physical injury.

User qualification
Electrical equipment should be installed, operated, serviced, and maintained only
by qualified personnel. No responsibility is assumed by Schneider Electric for any
consequences arising out of the use of this material.
A qualified person is one who has skills and knowledge related to the construction,
installation, and operation of electrical equipment and has received safety training
to recognize and avoid the hazards involved.
Use the password protection feature to help prevent untrained and non-authorized
personnel interacting with PowerLogic P7.

Protection and control device

About the Document

About the Document
Document Scope
PowerLogic™ P7 protection and control device (PowerLogic P7) user manual is for
people who are experts on electrical power engineering, panel builders,
commissioning engineers, and experienced users, communication specialists or
general users.
This document describes the functionality of PowerLogic P7 and provides the
logic diagrams, input and output signals, setting parameters, and technical
characteristics of the protection, control, monitoring, measurement, logging and
recording functions. The document also provides the installation, commissioning,
operation, and configuration instructions.

Validity Note
This document applies to the PowerLogic P7 range devices.
The characteristics of the products described in this document are intended to
match the characteristics that are available on www.se.com. As part of our
corporate strategy for constant improvement, we may revise the content over time
to enhance clarity and accuracy. If you see a difference between the
characteristics in this document and the characteristics on www.se.com, consider
www.se.com to contain the latest information.

Product Related Information
Refer to Safety information, page 19.

General Cybersecurity Information
In recent years, the growing number of networked machines and production plants
has seen a corresponding increase in the potential for cyber threats, such as
unauthorized access, data breaches, and operational disruptions. You must,
therefore, consider all possible cybersecurity measures to help protect assets and
systems against such threats.
To help keep your Schneider Electric products secure and protected, it is in your
best interest to implement the cybersecurity best practices as described in the
Cybersecurity Best Practices document.

P7/EN M/02-004A
