In the application case without test block, it is mandatory to isolate the CT and VT
supplies. The CT must be shorted before isolating normally with test links. The VT
fuses are removed or the MCB opened. The relay side connection should be
tested to ensure it is dead before proceeding. The trip circuit and intertrips (e.g.
CBF) are isolated to avoid an unintentional circuit breaker trip during the
secondary injection test. The secondary injection test can be performed by
injecting the current and voltage into the related analog connection terminals.

Applying application-specific settings
There are different methods of applying the settings:
•

PowerLogic Engineering Suite
Use PowerLogic Engineering Suite when setting the relay for the first time. It
is preferred to use the Engineering Suite for configuring the settings,
especially when specific logic (not the default logic) or many specific settings
are to be applied, as it is much faster and allows less margin for error.

•

Front panel
The front panel of PowerLogic P7 can also be used to configure the settings,
when limited specific settings are to be applied.
NOTE: If the application-specific settings are not available, the secondary
injection test can be performed based on the default settings.

During testing it may be necessary to disable some protection functions. After the
completion of the injection tests, all the protection functions applied must be
enabled during the final check stage.

Current inputs
This test verifies that the accuracy of current measurement is within acceptable
tolerances.

P7/EN M/02-004A

113

Protection and control device

Commissioning

PowerLogic P7 has three phase current inputs and single phase current inputs.
Multiple instances of each type can be added to bays. The source for each input
and type can be configured within the CT setup.
•

Double check the connection and then start the injection test.

•

Apply current equal to the related CT secondary rated current.

•

Check its magnitude using a multimeter or reading from the test equipment.

•

Check the current magnitude displayed on the front panel of PowerLogic P7.

•

Calculate the current measurement accuracy, it shall be within ±1% at rated
current.

Voltage inputs
This test verifies that the accuracy of voltage measurement is within the
acceptable tolerances.
PowerLogic P7 has three-phase voltage inputs and single phase voltage inputs.
Multiple instances of each type can be added to bays. The source for each input
and type can be configured within the VT setup.
The following tests will be realized with the VT Connecting Mode set to 3ph which
is the most used configuration. Refer to Connection type, page 449 for more
details.
•

Double check the connection and then start the injection test.

•

Apply voltage equal to the related VT secondary rated voltage.

•

Check its magnitude using a multimeter or reading from the test equipment.

•

Check the voltage magnitude on the front panel of PowerLogic P7.

•

Calculate the voltage measurement accuracy, it is within ±1% at rated
voltage.
NOTE: Test equipment may generate electrical noise when its outputs are Off.
The electrical noise is displayed as a voltage signal if its voltage exceeds the
cutoff threshold.

Binary input and binary output wiring and operation check
For binary input wiring and check, see Checking binary input wiring and operation,
page 608.
For binary output wiring and check, see Checking binary output wiring and
operation, page 608.

Checking the protection functions
The tests described in Device check, page 107, Current inputs, page 113, and
Voltage inputs, page 114 have already demonstrated that the device inputs and
outputs work correctly and the analog inputs are within calibration, thus the
purpose of the tests for protection functions is as follows:
•

To determine that each active protection function of the device can trip
according to the correct application settings.

•

To verify correct assignment of the trip and alarm contacts by monitoring the
response to the related fault injection.

The following sections only present the test procedures for current protection and
voltage protection. The basic test procedures are similar to the other protection
functions. For the list of settings which are used for various current and voltage
protection including the default values, see Protection functions, page 191.

114

P7/EN M/02-004A

Commissioning

Protection and control device

Current protection
This test, performed on stage 1 of the overcurrent protection function in setting
group 1, is to check that the protection and control device is operating correctly at
the application-specific settings.
1. Determine which output relay has been selected to operate when an
overcurrent protection trip occurs.
2. Connect the output relay so that its operation will trip the test set and stop the
timer of relay test set.
3. Connect the current outputs of the test set to the protection and control device
current input terminals.
4. Apply a current of 120% of the current setting to the protection and control
device and it will trip according to the operation time setting.
5. Check the tripping time from the test set and compare with the operation time
setting.
6. Check the related fault recorder, events, and the related LEDs for trip
indications.
A similar test procedure can be applied for the secondary injection test to check
the other protection functions with the application-specific settings.

Voltage protection
This test, performed on stage 1 of the under-voltage protection function in setting
group 1, is to check that the protection and control device is operating correctly at
the application-specific settings.
1. Determine which output relay has been selected to operate when an undervoltage protection trip occurs.
2. Connect the output relay so that its operation will trip the test set and stop the
timer.
3. Connect the voltage outputs of the test set to the protection and control
device voltage input terminals.
4. Apply nominal voltage first and then a voltage of 80% of the voltage setting to
the protection and control device. It will trip according to the operation time
setting.
5. Check the tripping time from the test set and compare with the operation time
setting.
6. Check the related fault recorder, events, and the related LEDs for trip
indications.
A similar test procedure can be applied for the secondary injection test to check
the other protection functions with the application-specific settings.

Bay dead check
This test is to check if the bay dead function is operating correctly with the
configured matrix. The bay dead function is applied to give an indication if all
phases of the line are dead. Its condition is determined by monitoring the status of
the BayLive input and by measuring the phase currents and voltages.
More information about bay dead, refer to Description, page 586 and Block
diagram, page 587 section of bay dead function.
1. In PowerLogic Engineering Suite, check the CB status is mapped to the
BayLive input via the Matrix.
2. Force the CB position to open status.
3. With zero current and nominal voltage applied, check the bay dead is true.
4. Force the CB closed status and check bay dead is false.
5. Force the CB open status and check the bay dead is true.

P7/EN M/02-004A

115

Protection and control device

Commissioning

6. Inject nominal voltage and current and check the BayDead is false.

Primary injection test (optional)
DANGER
HAZARD OF ELECTRIC SHOCK, EXPLOSION OR ARC FLASH
•

Only qualified electrical engineers can perform the primary injection test.

•

Strictly follow the related primary injection testing instructions from the utility.

Failure to follow these instructions will result in death or serious injury.
Primary injection testing is recommended to:
•

confirm the external wiring to the current and voltage inputs is correct,

•

check the polarity of the current transformers at each end is consistent,

•

check the directionality of the directional elements.

For the directional overcurrent or ground fault protection, distant protection or
current differential protection applications, it is mandatory to check the correct
polarity of the current transformers. If the current or voltage positive or negative
sequence components are applied in the protection functions, it is mandatory to
ensure the correct phase sequence of current or voltage inputs.

DANGER
HAZARD OF ELECTRIC SHOCK, EXPLOSION OR ARC FLASH
Verify that all connections are restored according to the external connection or
scheme diagram after disconnecting any of the external wiring from the
protection device for any tests.
Failure to follow these instructions will result in death or serious injury.
For primary injection testing on conventional CT’s, it is necessary to calculate the
current and voltage magnitudes to be injected in the primary side according to the
actual application scenario, check that the current magnitude is more than 20 mA
and the voltage magnitude is more than 100 mV on the secondary side. Normally
the mains power supply in the substation or power plant can be applied for primary
injection tests.

Voltage connections

NOTICE
CIRCUIT OVERLOAD
Measure the voltage transformer secondary voltages using a multimeter to
ensure that they are compliant with PowerLogic P7’s input ratings.
Failure to follow these instructions can result in equipment damage.
Primary voltage injection:

116

•

Check ratio is correctly configured.

•

Check the polarity is correctly connected.

•

Check that the system phase rotation is correct using a phase rotation meter.

•

Inject the voltage into the voltage transformer primary connection terminals.

P7/EN M/02-004A

Commissioning

Protection and control device

•

Check that the voltage magnitudes and angles are displayed on the HMI of
the protection and control device in primary values.

•

The voltage magnitudes should be equal to the applied voltage.

•

The voltage angles should be correct according to the phase sequence.

Thus, the VT ratio, polarity, phase sequence and the external wiring from the
primary system to the protection and control device can be verified by the primary
voltage injection.

Current connections

NOTICE
CIRCUIT OVERLOAD
Measure the current transformer secondary values for each input using a
multimeter connected in series with corresponding protection device current
input.
Failure to follow these instructions can result in equipment damage.
Primary current injection:
•

Check ratio is correctly configured.

•

Check the polarity is correctly connected.

•

Inject current into the primary system through the phase under test.

•

Check that the current magnitudes and angles are displayed on the HMI of
the protection and control device in primary values.

•

The current magnitudes should be equal to the applied current.

•

The current angles should be correct according to the phase sequence.

•

Check that the current transformer polarities are correct against a phase
meter already installed on site and known to be correct by measuring the
phase angle between the current and voltage, or by contacting the system
control center for the direction of power flow.

When using a residual current measurement with a CBCT or a sensitive current
transformer, inject a single phase to validate the functionality.
Therefore, the CT ratio, polarity, phase sequence and the external wiring from the
primary system to the protection and control device can be verified by primary
current injection.

Demonstrating circuit breaker operation
The correct operation of the circuit breaker shall be verified sufficiently during the
commissioning test. CB operation can be controlled by the local or remote-control
commands.
CB operation test:

P7/EN M/02-004A

•

Check the actual CB position, read the current value and the CB position
status from the HMI of the device if the related CB position (52a, 52b) has
been connected to the protection and control device opto-isolated inputs.
When CB position is open (52b), there will be no current. When CB position is
closed (52a), there will be current.

•

Perform a local CB control command to trip and close the CB, the CB shall
operate correctly per the control command. See Controlling objects, page 173
for how to control CB.

•

Read the CB position status from the HMI of the protection and control device
after one control command, the CB position information shall be the same as
the actual CB status.

117

Protection and control device

Commissioning

Final check
CAUTION
UNINTENDED EQUIPMENT OPERATION
At the end of the commissioning, verify that no device remains in test mode.
Failure to follow these instructions can result in injury or equipment
damage.

NOTICE
CIRCUIT OVERLOAD
•

Remove all test or temporary shorting leads.

•

Verify that all connections are restored according to the relevant external
connection or scheme diagram after disconnecting any of the external wiring
from the protection device for any wiring verification tests.

Failure to follow these instructions can result in equipment damage.
The commissioning is now complete, but before putting PowerLogic P7 into
normal operation, check the following items:
•

Ensure that PowerLogic P7 is restored to service.

•

CB maintenance and current counters should be set as required. These
counters can be reset.

•

Double check the application-specific settings, to ensure that all the desired
protection and control functions are enabled with the correct settings. Extract
the final setting file from PowerLogic P7.

•

Check the Date and Time of PowerLogic P7, to ensure the date and time are
synchronized or set manually when no time synchronization is used.

•

Clear all event records, fault records, and disturbance records. Make sure
that alarms and LEDs have been reset.

The device is now ready for operation.

118

P7/EN M/02-004A

Cybersecurity

Protection and control device

Cybersecurity
Cybersecurity overview
This chapter contains up-to-date information about your product’s cybersecurity.
Network administrators, system integrators and personnel that commission,
maintain or dispose of a device should:
•

Apply and maintain the device’s security capabilities. See Device security
capabilities, page 121 for details.

•

Review assumptions about protected environments. See Protected
environment assumptions, page 123 for details.

•

Address potential risks and mitigation strategies. See Potential risks and
compensating controls, page 124 for details.

•

Follow recommendations to optimize cybersecurity.

WARNING
POTENTIAL COMPROMISE OF SYSTEM AVAILABILITY, INTEGRITY, AND
CONFIDENTIALITY
•

Change default passwords to help prevent unauthorized access to device
settings and information.

•

Disable unused ports/services and default accounts, where possible, to
minimize pathways for malicious attacks.

•

Place networked devices behind multiple layers of cyber defenses (such as
firewalls, network segmentation, and network intrusion detection and
protection).

•

Use cybersecurity best practices (for example: least rights, separation of
duties) to help prevent unauthorized exposure, loss, modification of data and
logs, interruption of services, or unintended operation.

Failure to follow these instructions can result in death, serious injury, or
equipment damage.

Security policy
NOTICE
LOSS OF ACCESS
•

Setup a security policy and procedure to back up the security administrator
user account.

•

Do not share a single user account with multiple users. Set one user account
associated to roles and rights per user.

•

Do not excessively decrease values of user parameters.

Failure to follow these instructions can result in loss of access to the
protection device.
Cybersecurity helps provide:

P7/EN M/02-004A

•

Confidentiality (to help prevent unauthorized access).

•

Integrity (to help prevent unauthorized modification).

•

Availability/authentication (preventing the denial of service and assuring
authorized access).

•

Non-repudiation (preventing the denial of an action that took place).

•

Traceability/detection (logging and monitoring).

119

Protection and control device

Cybersecurity

For an efficient security, the instructions and procedures should structure the roles
and responsibilities in terms of security within the organization; in other words,
who is authorized to perform what and when. These should be known by the
users.
The anti-intrusion and anti-physical access to any sensitive installation should be
set up.
All the security rules implemented in PowerLogic P7 are in complement of the
points above.
PowerLogic P7 is delivered with auto-login via the local control panel with the
default ENGINEER role. The auto-login function can be configured with the
parameter Local Default Access using CAE.
In PowerLogic P7, the control of accessibility to the settings, parameters,
configuration and logs is done with a user authentication after Log in, with a name
and password.
PowerLogic P7 controls the access:
•

through the front panel

•

through PowerLogic Engineering Suite (front and rear connection)

The Ethernet communication with PowerLogic Engineering Suite is encrypted.
The access through the communication protocols is not controlled by
PowerLogic P7 but by the SCADA system. The protocols does not include any
specific secured commands.

NOTICE
POTENTIAL COMPROMISE TO CYBERSECURITY
•

For transmitting data over an internal network, physically or logically
segment the network. Restrict the access to the internal network by using
standard controls, such as firewalls, and other relevant features supported
by your device, such as IP Table allowlist.

•

For transmitting data over an external network, encrypt protocol
transmissions over all external connections using an encrypted tunnel, TLS
wrapper or a similar solution.

Failure to follow these instructions can increase the risk of unauthorized
access.
The access through the binary inputs is not controlled.
Any SCADA system, and any computer using PowerLogic Engineering Suite, CAE
software and central server as well, should have an updated anti-virus, antimalware, anti-ransomware application activated during the use. On customer
side, firewalls and proxies are set up to authorize communication.
If a central server is used, a backup is recommended.
A password policy should be implemented with:
•

Change of the default passwords before PowerLogic P7 is put into operation
(see Password complexity, page 146).

•

Periodic change of the passwords.

•

Revocation of the passwords of users who leave or do not need to use the
device any more.

Product defense-in-depth
Use a layered network approach with multiple security and defense controls in
your IT and control system to minimize data protection gaps, reduce single-pointsof-failure and create a strong cybersecurity posture. The more layers of security in
your network, the harder it is to breach defenses, take digital assets or cause

Each layer provides additional defense mechanisms.

•

Secure design principles are applied to each layer as each layer may be
compromised.

•

The objective is to reduce the attack surface of the subsequent layers.

Device security capabilities
This section describes the security capabilities available with your device.

Information confidentiality and integrity
Security capabilities of the device help protect the confidentiality of information
through secure protocols that employ cryptographic algorithms, key sizes, and
mechanisms used to help prevent unauthorized users from reading information in
transit. For example, SSH, SFTP and HTTPS.
PowerLogic P7 protects the integrity of the public key used for firmware
authenticity check with One time Programmable (OTP) so that it cannot be
tampered with.
PowerLogic P7 protects the confidentiality of user password and symmetric keys
at rest by saving only the HASH value instead of the clear text. For the user
password in transit, PowerLogic P7 protects the confidentiality by transmitting
them over HTTPs and Lightweight Directory Access Protocol (LDAP)s.
PowerLogic P7 generates the private keys uniquely for each device to limit the
scope of exploitation to that very device once compromised.
PowerLogic P7 follows the internationally recognized recommendation for
implementing cryptographic security mechanisms. In the meantime, the design
teams constantly review and test the product to identify and fix any vulnerable
ones.

P7/EN M/02-004A

121

Protection and control device

Cybersecurity

Physical security
In order to help prevent unauthorized access, lock the USB cover on the front
panel of PowerLogic P7 with a wired lead seal. See USB ports, page 151 for
details.
After flush mounting the device, the screws on the device can only be reached
from the inside of the panel. See Flush mounting, page 52 for details.
The Joint Test Action Group (JTAG) debugging interfaces of CPU and Field
Programmable Gate Array (FPGA) components are not soldered on manufactured
devices.

Cybersecurity configuration
These security capabilities support the analysis of security events, help protect the
device from unauthorized alteration and records configuration changes and user
account events:
•

Internal time synchronization.

•

Time source integrity protection and PowerLogic P7 configuration event
logging.

•

Timestamps, including date and time, match PowerLogic P7 clock.

•

Device files such as disturbance records, configuration files, log and trace
files are stored in Flash Memory of PowerLogic P7 and can be extracted over
HTTPS, SFTP and SNMP.

•

Embed user information with changes.

•

Offload information to syslog or a protected storage or retention location.

User accounts and rights
These security capabilities help enforce authorizations assigned to users,
segregation of duties and least rights:
•

User authentication is used to identify and authenticate software processes
and devices managing accounts.

•

User account lockouts configurable with number of unsuccessful login
attempts.

•

Password strength feedback using CAE.

Port hardening
The communication ports of PowerLogic P7 can be disabled from PowerLogic
Engineering Suite. Each logical port can be independently disabled. For more
information, refer to Port hardening, page 147.

Security banner
Security banner is supported on PowerLogic P7. For how to configure the security
banner, refer to the Security Banners section of the CAE user manual.
NOTE: Among the three configurable security banners on CAE, only Security
banner large is supported on PowerLogic P7.
When successfully configured, the security banner appears on the user login page
of HMI like below. If left unconfigured, no security banner is displayed.

P71119B

Security firmware upgrades
This security capability helps protect the authenticity of the firmware running on
PowerLogic P7 and facilitates protected file transfer: digitally signed firmware is
used to help protect the authenticity of the firmware running on the device and
only allows firmware generated and signed by Schneider Electric.

Device backup creation and restore
This security capability helps PowerLogic P7 participate in system level backup
operations by creating backups of the device state. In case of need, device can
recover with help of the backups to a known good operational state. The
protection, control and communication settings can be saved with PowerLogic
Engineering Suite; the security configuration can be saved with CAE.
For how to create such backups and to use them to restore device, refer to CAE
User Manual and PowerLogic Engineering Suite User Manual.
NOTE: The created backup files must not be modified in any way. Otherwise
they can’t pass validity check by PowerLogic Engineering Suite and the
restoration fails.

Security event logging
These security capabilities help provide a method to generate security-related
reports and manage event log storage:
•

Machine and human-readable reporting options for current device security
settings.

•

Audit event logs to identify:

•

◦

PowerLogic P7 configuration changes.

◦

Energy management system events.

Audit storage capacity of 3 event files with maximum 256 KB of each file and
alternate methods for log management.

Protected environment assumptions

P7/EN M/02-004A

•

Cybersecurity governance – available and up-to-date guidance on governing
the use of information and technology assets in your company.

•

Perimeter security – installed devices, and devices that are not in service, are
in an access-controlled or monitored location.

123

Protection and control device

Cybersecurity

•

Emergency power – the control system provides the capability to switch to
and from an emergency power supply without affecting the existing security
state or a documented degraded mode.

•

Firmware upgrades – PowerLogic P7 upgrades are implemented consistently
to the current version of firmware.

•

Controls against malware – detection, prevention and recovery controls to
help protect against malware are implemented and combined with
appropriate user awareness.

•

Physical network segmentation – the control system provides the capability
to:
◦

Physically segment control system networks from non-control system
networks.

◦

Physically segment critical control system networks from non-critical
control system networks.

•

Logical isolation of critical networks – the control system provides the
capability to logically and physically isolate critical control system networks
from non-critical control system networks. For example, using VLANs.

•

Independence from non-control system networks – the control system
provides network services to control system networks, critical or non-critical,
without a connection to non-control system networks.

•

Encrypt protocol transmissions over all external connections using an
encrypted tunnel, TLS wrapper or a similar solution.

•

Zone boundary protection – the control system provides the capability to:
◦

Manage connections through managed interfaces consisting of
appropriate boundary protection devices, such as: proxies, gateways,
routers, firewalls and encrypted tunnels.

◦

Use an effective architecture, for example, firewalls protecting application
gateways residing in a Demilitarized Zone.

◦

Control system boundary protection at any designated alternate
processing sites should provide the same levels of protection as that of
the primary site, for example, data centers.

•

No public internet connectivity – access from the control system to the
internet is not recommended. If a remote site connection is needed, for
example, encrypt protocol transmissions.

•

Resource availability and redundancy – ability to break the connections
between different network segments or use duplicate devices in response to
an incident.

•

Manage communication loads – the control system provides the capability to
manage communication loads to mitigate the effects of information flooding
types of DoS (Denial of Service) events.

•

Control system backup – available and up-to-date backups for recovery from
a control system failure.

Cybersecurity configuration
All PowerLogic P7 security rules are managed with EcoStruxure Cybersecurity
Admin Expert (CAE) software.
The CAE offers an independent user manual which describes functions in a more
detailed level, it can be downloaded from website of Schneider Electric.
The purpose of describing the CAE software in this manual is to help users start
with the security configuration specific to PowerLogic P7. For a thorough
understanding of the CAE functions, see the CAE user manual.

Introduction of CAE software
CAE allows users to centrally update usernames and passwords for all
PowerLogic P7 devices in the same local area network without having to do it bayby-bay. It can customize the rights of user roles and user accounts. More
advanced features of CAE include configuring the Syslog server, extracting locally
saved security logs from devices, and centralized authentication over RADIUS/
LDAP protocol with a Microsoft Active Directory or RADIUS server as the back
end.

Changing initial password
When launching CAE for the first time it will prompt for username and password.
The default username is SecurityAdmin and the default password is AAAAAAAA.
Both are case sensitive.

P7/EN M/02-004A

125

Protection and control device

Cybersecurity

After correctly entering the default username and password, CAE will ask for the
default password of the SecurityAdmin account to be changed.
Account will be locked if password is entered incorrectly up to 5 times. If this is the
only SECADM account of a project, waiting for a few minutes is obliged before any
further attempts with the same account. Alternatively, the blocked account can be
unblocked with another SECADM account immediately by using it to open the
project. Once the project is opened, all blocks of SECADM accounts will be
cancelled immediately.
After correctly enter the default username and the initial password, CAE will force
to change the initial password of the SecurityAdmin account

There is no password complexity requirement for a new project. When entering
password input, click and hold the icon of eye to verify input.
NOTE: It is strongly recommended to add a backup SecurityAdmin account so
that it can be used to unlock the CAE projects and reset the device passwords
when the main SecurityAdmin account password is lost.

CAE user interface overview
EcoStruxure Cybersecurity Admin Expert (CAE) user interface window is
composed of three main work areas:
1. Project Manager pane
2. Selection pane
3. Settings pane

•

Menu bar: Provide functions to manage user accounts, manage roles and
permissions, manage policies and settings, manage known devices, discover
devices and update device security. The organization of functions follows a
typical configuration workflow and user can jump from one to another.

•

Preferences: Provide the function to edit application preferences such as
settings for general information, security and dialog box.

•

Username: Provide the function to display the logged in username.

•

Log out: Provide log out function from the application.

•

?: Provide help function to show the application logs and application
information.

•

Create/Export/Import project: Provide the function to create, export or
import CAE security configuration project database.

Most CAE functions can be used without connection to PowerLogic P7 devices.
Connection with devices in the same local area network (LAN) is only required
when using function of MANAGEMENT OF SYSTEM for pushing new settings to
devices.
The CAE functions on menu bar are listed as follows:

P7/EN M/02-004A

•

USER ACCOUNTS: Provide the functions to create, rename, remove an
account, change the password of an account and assign accounts to roles.

•

ROLES: Provide the functions to create, remove roles and modify the
privilege.

•

SECURITY SETTINGS: Provide the functions to set security-related features,
such as session time out duration, password complexity requirement, Syslog
server and account locking.

•

SYSTEM EDITOR: Provide the functions to import, export, add devices in the
network.

•

MANAGEMENT OF SYSTEM: Provide the functions to discover devices in
the network and push the security settings to devices.

127

Protection and control device

Cybersecurity

CAE recommended workflow
The security settings of devices are managed with the CAE by projects. Each
project contains a set of roles and user accounts, with various security-related
settings. The project can work offline. There is no need of connection to
PowerLogic P7 devices when changing the settings. When offline work is done,
the CAE must connect with devices and send the settings to them.
NOTE: Changing device security settings with the CAE is a one-direction
process. The new settings can be pushed from the CAE project to
PowerLogic P7. The existing security settings cannot be extracted from
PowerLogic P7.
1. To manage the project, from the Project manager pane, open an existing
CAE project or create a new project.
Refer to Projects, page 129.
2. To manage the roles, from the ROLES menu, check whether the default rights
of roles are in line with your organization cybersecurity policy.
– Privileges can be changed for existing roles.
– New roles can be created.
– Existing roles can be removed.
Refer to Roles, page 129.
3. To manage the user accounts, from the USER ACCOUNTS menu, create,
remove or modify user accounts, assign roles and set password for accounts.
Refer to User accounts, page 130.
4. To manage other security settings, from the SECURITY SETTINGS menu, set
options including timeout, user account locking, password complexity, Syslog
server, Security banners and centralized authentication methods.
Refer to Security settings, page 132.
5. Optionally, to declare the devices, from the SYSTEM EDITOR menu, provide
IP addresses of devices to the CAE for connection. The CAE can discover
PowerLogic P7 devices automatically via Device Profile for Web Services
(DPWS) and User Datagram Protocol (UDP).
Refer to System editor, page 134.
6. To prepare the connections between computers with the CAE and
PowerLogic P7, connect them via Ethernet and the front USB port. Use only
one type of connection at a time for a same device.
– To connect via Ethernet connection, confirm the CAE computer is in the
same local area network (LAN) with devices to be managed.
– To connect with front USB port, confirm only one device is to be
connected.
7. To push new security settings to PowerLogic P7, from MANAGEMENT OF
SYSTEM menu, enable discover function to find all devices, and push new
security settings to them.
Refer to Management of system, page 135.

1. Click Create a new project to create a new project.

2. Select an option in Architecture field to set the Security Setting menu.

template can also be created from one of the existing projects.
4. Click Save to save the project.
5. Double click the new project in the Project manager pane to switch to the
project.
Authentication request is pop-up.
6. Type the account SecurityAdmin and default password AAAAAAAA to login.
No password complexity is required for a new project. CAE projects can only
be opened by SECADM (Security Administrator) role.
Force changing initial password window is displayed.
7. Type the customized password for the account SecurityAdmin.
The project will be loaded with CAE default template settings if Factory is
selected to create the project from.

Protection and control device

Cybersecurity

Logs
•

Logging parameters: All supported security standards are enabled in the
factory default configuration of PowerLogic P7. When creating a new CAE
project, the CAE enables the BDEW standard by default. For the types of logs
and which will be generated for which standard, see List of security event
logs, page 148.

•

Syslog parameters: Input remote Syslog server address and port in this
section. The device will send all the internally generated security logs to
server for backup after address and port defined.

•

SNMP parameters: For details, see PowerLogic P7 Communication User
Manual.

Certificate allowlist
PowerLogic P7 connects with the CAE via HTTPs. The device works as a server
with device self-signed HTTPs certificate to help ensure the credentials and
contents will not be transmitted in clear text.

At network discovery, the CAE searches for the certificates of the devices
connected to the system network, then the Security Administrator can decide
whether to trust or not the detected certificates. When the Security Administrator
trusts a device certificate, this certificate is stored by the CAE, so that at next
discovery, the CAE will be able to identify the certificate.

System editor
The System editor menu is optional for PowerLogic P7, because the CAE can
discover PowerLogic P7 devices in the same local area network automatically with
DPWS and UDP.
NOTE: In rare occasions the CAE may not discover all PowerLogic P7
devices in the same network. In this case, declaring them will help the CAE
find them.

134

P7/EN M/02-004A

Cybersecurity

Protection and control device

Management of system
When all security settings are done, they can be pushed to PowerLogic P7
devices.

Connecting to PowerLogic P7 with the CAE
The CAE can connect with PowerLogic P7 via Ethernet or front USB port.
•

To use the Ethernet connection, please ensure that the CAE computer is in
the same local area network with PowerLogic P7 devices to be managed.

•

To use the front USB port, please ensure the good connection by USB cable
between the CAE computer and PowerLogic P7. Make sure that the device
USB cable driver is installed. The device IP address for the front USB port is
always 90.0.0.1.

It is recommended to use PING command to verify the connection between
PowerLogic P7 and the CAE computer beforehand.

Push the new security settings to PowerLogic P7
To find all PowerLogic P7 devices automatically in the same LAN,
1. Click Discover.

If the devices cannot be found, re-try after disabling the firewall in the CAE
computer.
For new devices which have been found the first time, it is necessary to Trust
them first by adding their HTTPs certificate into the certificate allowlist of the
CAE. The new PowerLogic P7 devices will ask for Security Admin password,
which is AAAA.
2. Click Send security configuration to push new security settings to the
devices. The Security Admin password will also be updated as they are set in
the CAE project.

P7/EN M/02-004A

135

Protection and control device

Cybersecurity

Retrieving PowerLogic P7 internal security logs
•

Right click on PowerLogic P7 in the list of discovered devices to retrieve
security logs from the device. This operation can take a few seconds.

PowerLogic P7 keeps security logs with a cyclic non-volatile memory, upon
reaching the limit of memory file size the earliest entries will be replaced by new
ones.

Adding a device certificate issued by CA
Additional certificates issued by Certificate Authority (CA) entities can be added to
the device for some connections to be securely established (with Syslog server,
for example). To do this, follow the steps below:
1. Import the root certificate of the CA to CAE.
a. Open CAE on a computer connected to the device, either with the front
USB port or an Ethernet LAN.
b. Under SECURITY SETTINGS > Certificate store, click Add Certificate.
c.

In the pop-up window, import the root certificate from a local directory.

d. Click Add.
2. Add the root certificate to the device’s trusted chain.
a. Click MANAGEMENT OF SYSTEM.
b. Run a synchronization.
c.

Right click the discovered device.
NOTE: If the device has not been trusted by CAE before, trust the device
in the context menu and click Send Signing Certificate at the bottom
right of the MANAGEMENT OF SYSTEM tab.

d. Under Certificate Management > Trusted chain, click Add Certificate
(s).
e. Select the imported root certificate and click Update.
f.

Click Yes.

g. Click OK.
3. Add the CA-issued device certificate to the device.
a. Under Certificate Management > Internal, click Add PFX.
b. From a local directory, import the PFX file that contains the certificate.
c.

Input the password to the PFX file.

d. Click Import.
Note: If Step 3 isn’t applicable, the device can be enrolled to the CA with
the existing self-signed certificate. To do this, use the Enroll PFX option
on the same page and the automatic enrollment feature in CAE. See the
Certificate Management chapter of CAE manual for details.

User accounts and rights
Recommendations to optimize cybersecurity in a protected environment:

136

•

Assign users only the essential rights needed for their role.

•

Revoke user rights when no longer needed due to role change, transfer or
termination. User credentials do not expire.

•

Follow your organization’s user account management policy or contact your
network administrator.

P7/EN M/02-004A

Cybersecurity

Protection and control device

List of roles
PowerLogic P7 menus and screens are accessible according to the RBAC to help
prevent unauthorized operation. RBAC is essentially a collection of permissions.
Users are assigned particular roles, and through those role assignments acquire
the permissions to perform particular functions. Since users are not assigned
permissions directly, but only acquire them through their role (or roles),
management of individual user rights becomes a matter of simply assigning
appropriate roles to the user's account; this simplifies common operations, such
as adding a user, or changing user's account.
The following table lists the predefined roles. For detailed mappings, see Role-toright mapping, page 137.
Table 22 - List of roles for cybersecurity
Role

Default user account

Description

VIEWER

ViewerLevel

A Viewer can read all device data except security related logs. A Viewer cannot make any
changes.

OPERATOR

OperatorLevel

An Operator can perform control operations, change settings, make configuration changes.
An Operator can also retrieve process data and records via SFTP.

ENGINEER

EngineerLevel

An Engineer can perform the same actions as an Operator. An Engineer can also change
protection setting groups, upload configuration files, and update firmware.

INSTALLER

DefaultInstaller

An Installer can upload configuration files, update firmware, run system diagnostics via
SSH, and perform a factory reset. An installer cannot change settings or perform control
operations.

SECAUD

DefaultSecAud

A Security Auditor can read security related logs including user authentication logs.

SECADM

SecurityAdmin

A Security Administrator can read security related logs and manage the security policy of
PowerLogic P7, including certificates, users, roles, and rights.

RBAC for PowerLogic P7 complies with IEC 62351-8 and can be extended further
with CAE, depending on the organization.

List of rights
PowerLogic P7 allows and controls the following usages:
•

Read information provided by PowerLogic P7, including measurements,
status of the controlled and monitored switchgear, settings, alarms, and
tripping context.

•

Open and close switchgear.

•

Upload the log files and disturbance record files.

•

Configure PowerLogic P7 for integration into the cubicle, protection, and
control.

•

P7/EN M/02-004A

Cybersecurity

Protection and control device

HMI Auto-login
To facilitate the commissioning work by panel builder and system integrator,
PowerLogic P7 provides an Auto-login feature when manufactured. This feature
grants privilege of the role of ENGINEER for HMI access, without request of
password. All roles and rights are described in List of rights, page 137 and List of
roles, page 137.

Login
If the front panel auto-login has been changed from ENGINEER, it is necessary to
login in to perform control actions or setting changes. If this is required, touch the
icon to locate to the User Login screen, enter the name or role and the
password. Refer to Login, page 154 for more information.
For login by PowerLogic Engineering Suite, refer to Connecting to the device
using the Mini-USB or DHMI port, page 159 or Connecting to the device via
Ethernet, page 160 for more information.
Only a Security Admin role (SECADM) can login to a CAE project by CAE tool.

Access lockout
The first invalid password entry sets the counter of failed authorization and
initiates an attempt timer.
If the user reaches the maximum number of retries, a message is displayed and
the user is blocked. In this case, only the VIEWER access rights are granted and
any other access is blocked.
The maximum number of failed authentications is set by CAE. The default is
defined at 5 and can be set up to 99.
When the maximum number of failed authentications is reached, a lockout period
is activated. This lockout period is by default 3 minutes with a maximum of 30
minutes. It can be set by CAE.
If the attempt timer expires, or the correct password is entered before the attempts
counter reaches the maximum number, then the attempts counter is reset to zero.
If a user is locked out, the block is applied to that user for all PowerLogic P7
interfaces. The blocking of one user does not result in blocks to the others.
If the user entry is blocked, recovering the RBAC or pushing a new RBAC will not
reset the blocked user entry, but a PowerLogic P7 reboot will reset the blocking
time and attempts counter, so the user entry will be unblocked.
NOTE: Set sufficient Maximum login attempts and Password attempts
timer to allow user connection after errors.

Logout
If the user logins manually via front panel and there is no operation within the
Minimum inactivity period(20), the user will be logged out. After logout, if Active
‘Local Default Access’ field is selected in Yes(20), the default ENGINEER role will
automatically login via front panel, otherwise, the front panel will remain logout.
A user icon in the top right corner of the title bar indicates the login status of
PowerLogic P7. The user icon changes from grey to green color when any user
logs in.

(20) The parameter is configured in CAE.

P7/EN M/02-004A

139

Protection and control device

Cybersecurity

NOTE: It is recommended to set Minimum inactivity period to 15 minutes in
order to download or upload files during installation, operation and
maintenance.

Number of accounts
The maximum number of user accounts which can be memorized in
PowerLogic P7 is 255.

Session management
The maximum connection per role is one when login as Operator or Engineer,
except the auto-login connection from the local control panel.

Centralized authentication
Centralized authentication enables the user account and privilege management of
PowerLogic P7 to be merged into an existing authentication infrastructure in the
customer network. PowerLogic P7 supports centralized authentication via
RADIUS protocol or LDAP protocol, which are used to communicate with a
RADIUS server or a Microsoft Active Directory server (AD server), respectively.
With centralized authentication configured, users can keep using their existing
username for other devices such as other protection relays, network switches,
workstations on PowerLogic P7. The existing RADIUS server or AD server in the
customer network can also be used to manage the user account and privilege for
PowerLogic P7. Once the account settings are changed at the RADIUS or AD
server, PowerLogic P7 will take account of those changes for user authentication
and authorization.

Solution overview
Depending on the existing authentication server used in the customer network,
different solutions are available:
•

If the existing authentication server is an AD server, PowerLogic P7 can be
directly connected to the AD server or connected to the AD server with the
Cybersecurity Application Platform (CAP) as a proxy. To simplify the firewall
setup, connecting to the AD server with the CAP is recommended.

•

If the existing authentication server is a RADIUS server, PowerLogic P7 can
be directly connected to the server.

AD Server as the existing authentication server
When the existing authentication server is an AD server, connecting
PowerLogic P7 devices to the AD server with the CAP as a proxy is recommended
because this method simplifies the firewall setup.

P71117A

In Connecting PowerLogic P7 devices to the AD server with the CAP as a proxy,
page 141, the CAP server is placed in Secure Zone 1, where PowerLogic P7
devices and other devices such as RTUs, switches are also located. The firewall
only needs to allow the connection between the CAP server and the AD server,
which makes the setup easier and more scalable.
NOTE: Secure Zone 1 is considered to be the most isolated and secure
segment, because it has OT devices that control the critical infrastructure
devices such as circuit breakers and switches. Secure Zone 2 is the network
segment used for information management services such as e-mail, file
servers and the Internet. To ensure the security of Secure Zone 1, firewalls
and/or network diodes are used to isolate Security Zone 1 from Security Zone
2.
However, if using CAP is not ideal or possible, PowerLogic P7 devices can be
directly connected to the AD server over the LDAP protocol.
Figure 59 - Directly Connecting PowerLogic P7 devices to the AD server

a firewall.

RADIUS server as the existing authentication server
When the existing authentication server is a RADIUS server, PowerLogic P7
devices can be directly connected with the authentication server.

Depending on the existing authentication server used in the customer network,
different configuration procedure is needed.

Configuring centralized authentication between the device and the AD
server
The following items are required to carry out the configuration:
•

An AD server.

•

A laptop with Active Directory Users and Computers (ADUC) installed. This is
Laptop 1.
NOTE: Laptop 1 must be able to join the domain controlled by the AD
server above. You need the domain administrator privilege on the AD
server to achieve this.

•

A laptop with CAE installed and the Security Admin privileges to both CAE
and PowerLogic P7. This is Laptop 2.
NOTE: You can also install CAE on Laptop 1 and only use one laptop as
a result. The same Security Admin privileges are still required in such a
case.

•

142

A configured CAP server if you choose to connect via the CAP proxy.

P71115A

PowerLogic P7 can connect with the AD server either directly or connect with the
CAP as a proxy as shown in Connecting PowerLogic P7 devices to the AD server
with the CAP as a proxy, page 143. In both cases, PowerLogic P7 and the AD
server must be configured accordingly to ensure successful connection. The
following guide will walk you through the full process.

Configuring the AD server for centralized authentication
In this procedure, a new user and a new group are created in the AD server. Then
the new user is assigned to the new group. The VALIDATION Organizational Unit
(OU) is used as an example of designated OU during the user creation steps.
1. On Laptop 1, open AUDC.
2. Expand the val.ctc.se.com domain.
3. Right click OU VALIDATION.
4. Click New > User on the pop-up menu.
5. Create the new user.
As an example, below is the necessary information when creating a new user
named Alice Wang:
•

First name: Alice

•

Last name: Wang

•

Full name: AWang

•

User logon name: Alice Wang

•

User logon name (pre-Windows 2000): Alice Wang
NOTE: The full name AWang is the username used for authentication at
PowerLogic P7 device.

6. Back in AUDC of Laptop 1, expand the val.ctc.se.com domain.
7. Right click Common Name (CN) Users.
8. Click New > Group on the pop-up menu.
9. Create the new group.
As an example, below is the necessary information when creating a new
group named Relay Engineer:
•

Group name: Relay Engineer

•

Group name (pre-Windows 2000): Relay Engineer

•

Group scope: Global

•

Group type: Security

10. Click OK.
11. Add the created user AWang to the created group Relay Engineer with the
following information:
•

Select this object type: Groups or Build-in security principals

•

From this location: val.ctc.se.com

•

Enter the object names to select (examples): Relay Engineer

12. Click OK.

P7/EN M/02-004A

Protection and control device

Cybersecurity

Configuring the device for centralized authentication
1. On Laptop 2, open CAE.
2. Click SECURITY SETTINGS.
3. Click Authentication(21).
4. Fill in the text boxes under LDAP Protocol Details.
As an example, below is the necessary information when filling in the text
boxes for the VALIDATION OU and the val.ctc.se.com domain:
•

Base DN: ou=VALIDATION, dc=val,dc=ctc,dc=se,dc=com

•

Server IP Address: The IP address of the CAP or the IP address of the
AD server, depending on the connection method you are using.

•

Server Port: The corresponding port number at the AD server.

5. Click Add a new group, a text box appears.
6. Fill in the text box with Relay Engineer.
7. In the Roles list, check ENGINEER as shown in Map one role to one group,
page 144.
Figure 64 - Map one role to one group

8. Click Add a new group again.
9. If you need to assign multiple roles to a group, fill in the text box with Relay
Power User.
10. In the Roles list, check the roles you want to assign as shown in the example
of Map multiple roles to one group, page 144.
Figure 65 - Map multiple roles to one group

11. Click Add a new group again.

•

A RADIUS server.

(21) When the Centralized authentication default role is set to None, users are granted VIEWER permissions.

144

P7/EN M/02-004A

Cybersecurity

Protection and control device

Configuring the RADIUS server for centralized authentication
This procedure uses TekRADIUS as an example.
1. On the RADIUS server, open TekRADIUS Manager.
2. Click the USERS tab.
Two panels are under the USERS tab. In the left Browse Users panel, you
can find all created usernames and the groups they belong to. When you click
a specific username, its attributes appear in the right panel, as shown in Map
one role to one user, page 145.
Figure 66 - Map one role to one user

3. In the Browse Users panel, click a username.
4. In the right panel, check the Type field of the second attribute is SuccessReply or not. If not, change it to Success-Reply.
To the left of the Type field is the Attribute field for which you can choose any
value available. The chosen value will be reused when configuring the device
later.
5. Change the Value field of the second attribute to a specific role, such as
INSTALLER.
If multiple roles need to be assigned to one user, a semicolon is required to
split the different roles. To assign the installer and engineer roles to a user, for
example, you can change the Value of the second attribute to INSTALLER;
ENGINEER as shown in Map multiple roles to one user, page 145.
Figure 67 - Map multiple roles to one user

Configuring the device for centralized authentication
1. On the laptop, open the CAE software.
2. Click SECURITY SETTINGS.
3. Click Authentication(22).
4. Under the drop-down menu of Centralized authentication protocol, choose
Radius.
5. Under the drop-down menu of Mode, choose RADIUS_CLEAN or EAP_
TTLS.
6. Fill in the IP address, Port and Shared secret of the RADIUS server, and the
role Attribute that you have chosen when configuring the RADIUS server.

(22) When the Centralized authentication default role is set to None, users are granted VIEWER permissions.

P7/EN M/02-004A

145

Protection and control device

Cybersecurity

Passwords
Changing password
The password change is done exclusively through the CAE tool by a SECADM
user.

Password complexity
PowerLogic P7 accepts the default username EngineerLevel and the default
password AAAA.
PowerLogic P7 password complexity can be configured by CAE and there is no
restrictions by default in the device.
With CAE it is possible to configure the password according to NERC and
IEEE 1686 standard recommendations.
Standard

Configuration

NERC

8 characters minimum with ASCII [33 to 122] characters.
The lesser of three or more different types of characters (e.g. uppercase
alphabetic, lowercase alphabetic, numeric from 0 to 9, non-alphabetic
characters)

IEEE
std1686

8 characters minimum with ASCII [33 to 122] characters
1 lowercase letter, 1 uppercase letter, 1 digit from 0 to 9 and 1 non-alphabetic
character

Password reset
When a password is lost, following options are available to reset the password to
default:
•

by the security administrator
The security administrator can reset the passwords for the other users with
the CAE tool.

•

through device factory reset
The passwords can be reset after the factory reset of the device. Please refer
to Performing factory reset, page 604.
NOTE: A factory reset will result in loss of all device configuration and stored
data, the device will reboot and operate the watchdog contact. Reserve the
security log is optional during the factory reset operation.

Default password setting

NOTICE
UNAUTHORIZED EQUIPMENT TAMPERING
At the first use of PowerLogic P7, change the default passwords.
Failure to follow these instructions can result in unauthorized product use
and setting changes, and compromised security of the information
contained in the product.
When delivered from factory, the RBAC configuration of PowerLogic P7 is as
follows:

(23)

PowerLogic P7 does not have any backdoors such as super password as
authentication-bypass mechanism.

Port hardening
It is possible to disable communication ports from the Device configuration >
Slot A tab of PowerLogic Engineering Suite.
The rear Ethernet ports and the logical ports on each rear Ethernet port can be
independently disabled.
These logical ports are listed below with Ethernet port number in brackets:
•

PowerLogic Engineering Suite Web server (port 8080/8443/9867)

•

IEC 61850 (port 102)

•

SFTP (port 2222)

•

Web HMI (port 9443)

•

DNP3 over Ethernet (default on port 20000, configurable)

•

Modbus TCP/IP (default on port 502, configurable)
NOTE: By default, all logical ports listed above are disabled except the Web
server port on P7 PORT1. It is strongly recommended to keep unused ports
disabled to reduce the risk of attack to PowerLogic P7.

Security event logging
The following types of time-stamped events are logged in a dedicated security log
file:
NOTE: For a detailed list of all the events logged, see List of security event
logs, page 148
•

Security parameter changes (such as user creation, password change)

•

User authentications, attempt to authentication and logout

•

Certificate updates

•

Access to the security events

•

Device reboots

•

Device software updates

•

Protection parameter changes
Only the occurrence of the first change in each session is logged.

•

Setting-group change, with the mention of the active setting group

(23) The previous passwords cannot be reused.

P7/EN M/02-004A

147

Protection and control device

Cybersecurity

•

Control, configuration uploading, firmware uploading

•

File downloading

PowerLogic P7 can store up to 3 event files with maximum 256 KB of each file.
When the maximum capacity is reached, the oldest events are erased.
NOTE: The existing security event log files are backed up to the csbak
directory during firmware update.
The audit event logs are read-only and cannot be erased by any users. During the
factory reset, however, if authorized by a user with firmware upload privilege
(ENGINEER or INSTALLER role by default), they can be erased.
The security events can be read from PowerLogic P7:
•

By central server tool or any security server automatically

•

By CAE with the security administrator access rights

Security logs depend on the security standards that are configurable by the CAE.
The security logs can be pushed to a Syslog server over TLS-encrypted
connection if the Syslog server IP address and IP port are configured and
connected. To establish the encrypted connection, the device must trust the
Syslog server by:
•

•

adding one of the following certificates to the device’s Trusted Chain:
◦

The Syslog server certificate

◦

The root certificate of the CA that issued the Syslog certificate

adding the Syslog server certificate to the device’s Internal certificates
NOTE: To help avoid unexpected device behavior, delete the self-signed
Syslog server certificate from internal certificates before adding a new
one.

For details, see Adding a device certificate issued by CA, page 136.
For mutual TLS, the Syslog server needs configuration to trust the device as well.
Configuration for the Syslog server is done outside CAE.
The generalized security logs include the information according to events
definition in IEEE 1686 and IEC 62351 Part 7.

WARNING

Upgrades management
When PowerLogic P7 firmware is changed, the security configuration will be
replaced by the default configuration. It is recommended that the security
configuration is reviewed after an upgrade to analyze rights for new or changed
device features and revoke or apply them according to your company’s policies
and standards. Users can manually update the security configuration by using
CAE.
PowerLogic P7 firmware is signed by Schneider Electric global signing center.
During the upgrade procedure the firmware will be checked for authenticity by
decrypting the signature with the public key kept with the product read-only
memory.

Schneider Electric cybersecurity support
The Schneider Electric website provides the list of known vulnerabilities and
allows cybersecurity administrators to report an incident or vulnerability.
Visit Schneider Electric cybersecurity homepage at Report a cybersecurity
incident | Schneider Electric Global.

149

Protection and control device

Operation

Operation
The front panel, if fitted, can be used for both entering all the data required for
operation of PowerLogic P7 and accessing the data for device management.
The following tasks are available from the front panel:
•

Control switchgear units
◦

View device status on an animated mimic diagram

◦

Local opening and closing devices controlled by PowerLogic P7

•

Readout and modification of settings

•

Readout of live operating data

•

Readout of operating data logs

•

Readout of event logs after overload situations, ground faults, short circuits
and so on in the power system

•

Readout of hardware and firmware options from related version

•

Device resetting and triggering additional control functions used in testing and
commissioning

•

Change device mode as required for testing and operation

•

Enter a password to login according to different access rights for settings and
operations
NOTE: If set up, the web HMI provides very similar functionality as the front
panel. See Using the web HMI, page 156.

Control is also possible through the PC interface. This requires a suitable PC
installed with the engineering tool PowerLogic Engineering Suite. See Using
PowerLogic Engineering Suite, page 157.

Using the HMI
NOTICE
EQUIPMENT DAMAGE
Do not press buttons using sharp objects.
Failure to follow these instructions can result in equipment damage.
PowerLogic P7 can be equipped with a user-friendly front panel.

Press the Home button to cancel an operation or return to the home page.

Physical LEDs on the front panel
PowerLogic P7 has four LEDs on the front panel representing the status of
PowerLogic P7 regarding power, alarm, trip, and operation mode (see IED mode,
page 602).

LED test mode
A long press of the Reset button on the front panel for at least 3 seconds will enter
the LED test mode if the screen is active. All physical LEDs on the front panel will
light up one by one, and then turn off one by one. After that the active LEDs will
return to previous states.

Physical LEDs on the CPU board
PowerLogic P7 has four physical LEDs on the CPU board to indicate its current
working states.
(24) Start-up is a transient state between PowerOn and Normal/Safe/Factory reset mode. The device can also enter Factory reset mode after
start-up.
(25) Degraded operation mode is a transient state. When some nonfatal errors occur, the IED continues running as long as the protection
domain works normally. The fault situation can be observed by HMI maintenance LED, SOE, system event log or LPHD1/PhyHealth in
database.

Configurable virtual LEDs
The device provides 24 user configurable virtual LEDs.
To view the virtual LEDs from the HMI:
•

Tap the virtual LED icon on the top right corner of the touchscreen display,
a list of virtual LEDs configured for the device will be displayed.

When any virtual LED status is changed, the LED side bar comes down from the
top right side of the touchscreen display for 2 seconds. Tapping the side bar will
open the LED list page.
When the trip LED

is ON, the LED list page will be displayed.

•

If the display is off when the trip LED turns on, the display will turn on and
show the LED list page.

•

If the display is active when the trip LED turns on, the display will switch to the
LED list page after the defined screen timeout or a maximum of 15 minutes
has elapsed.

•

When the trip LED is reset, the display will time out if not being used.

To configure the virtual LEDs, see Configuring virtual LEDs (LEDIHMI), page 165.

Login and logout
For more details about the password management and the user level, see

Login
Login is required before changing settings or accessing the data protected by the
Role Based Access Control (RBAC) mechanism.
NOTE: In the default RBAC, the HMI is set to auto-login. This can be
deactivated by CAE if required.
The login screen can be accessed by tapping the
home page. The login procedure is as follows:
1. Tap the

icon on the top bar of the

icon to enter the User Login page.

2. Enter the username and password by the physical keyboard or the pop-up
soft keypad.
3. Tap the Login.
NOTE: After a correct password is entered, the icon turns to green. The
view and edit capability of settings depends on the access rights of the
selected user and the role managed by the RBAC.

Logout
It is possible to log out manually by pressing the green
Log out option from the drop-down menu.

icon and then selecting

To set the device language:
•

Setting session language
Within the device login page, the user can change the HMI language that only
applies to the current session. Once the current session expires after inactivity or
the user manually logs out, the HMI resets back to the configured language.
NOTE: The session lifetime can be changed with the Minimum inactivity
period (min) setting in the CAE.
The session language can be changed regardless of user login status. To change
the session language, tap on the HMI home page. From the language dropdown, select the required session language and tap Confirm.

Changing settings
To change the data of settings with the front panel:
1. Navigate to the desired setting page from the front panel.
2. Select the parameter field you want to change.
3. Enter the value with the pop-up keypad or select the value from the dropdown menu.
4. Confirm the change operation.
NOTE: On the right side of the pop-up keypad, the maximum, the minimum
and the step values of this parameter are listed. If the entered value is beyond
the range, an information box will pop up.

155

Protection and control device

Operation

Adjusting screen settings
To adjust the LCD settings of PowerLogic P7:
•

From the HMI, expand > General > Device config > HMI > Setting and
set the screen backlight or screen timeout.

•

From PowerLogic Engineering Suite, expand Device configuration > HMI >
Basic info and set the screen backlight or screen timeout.

Managing alarms
Checking and resetting the alarm message
Check the alarm LED ! status on the front panel or the alarm icon
on the top
bar of the device HMI to see if any alarm is triggered. Once an alarm occurs, the
alarm LED lights up and at the same time, the alarm icon status changes. The
number next to the alarm icon represents the number of alarms.
To check the alarm messages:
•

Tap
to access the Alarm List page to view the alarm name, occurrence
time and detailed information.

To reset all alarms:
•

Tap Reset all from the Alarm List page.

To reset single alarm:
•

Tap R on the single alarm row from the Alarm List page.

After performing reset operation, the alarm icon will disappear and alarm LED will
turn off.

Using the web HMI
PowerLogic P7 provides a web HMI function that can be used with a standard web
browser. The web HMI has almost the same look and functionality as the physical
HMI. If the web HMI and the physical HMI are both available for a device, they can
be used at the same time.
Depending on the ordered device option, the web HMI can be set up through the
Mini-USB/DHMI port or Port 1/2/3. For the web browser, Schneider Electric
recommends Edge, Chrome, Safari or Firefox.
NOTE: The web HMI does not have the auto-login feature, so a valid
username and password are necessary to use the web HMI.
Prerequisites:
•

A valid username and password.

•

Web service is enabled on Port 1/2/3 if these ports are used.
1. In PowerLogic Engineering Suite, navigate to Device configuration >
Slot A > Port 1 Ethernet or Port 2/3 Ethernet.
2. Set Webserver status to On.

To set up the web HMI:
1. Connect the device with a computer through the Mini-USB/DHMI port or Port
1/2/3.

156

P7/EN M/02-004A

Operation

Protection and control device

2. Verify that the web HMI service port of 9443 is reachable on the device.
NOTE: The IP address for Mini-USB/DHMI port is fixed to 90.0.0.1; the IP
address for Port 1/2/3 is configured with PowerLogic Engineering Suite.
Scenario

Action

Port 9443
reachable

Go to Step 3.

Port 9443
unreachable

•

Ensure software (e.g. port-blocking applications) and system settings (e.
g. firewalls) are properly configured to allow a stable connection between
the computer and the device.

•

Ensure the network interface used on the computer and the connected
device port are in the same IP range.
On a Windows computer, network interfaces can be viewed and modified
under Control Panel > Network and Internet > Network and Sharing
Center > Change adapter settings.

3. On the computer, use a browser to visit one of the following addresses:
•

https://90.0.0.1:9443/PowerLogicP7.html (for Mini-USB/DHMI port)

•

https://PortIPAddress:9443/PowerLogicP7.html (for Port 1/2/3,
PortIPAddress must be replaced by the actual IP address of Port 1/2/3)

4. When an error message indicating non-private connection appears, click
Advanced > Continue to x.x.x.x (unsafe) (may vary slightly depending on
the browser used).
The web HMI will start loading.
5. When the loading finishes, type in the username and password and click
Login.

Using PowerLogic Engineering Suite
Overview

WARNING
UNINTENDED EQUIPMENT OPERATION
Make sure that the modification of setting parameters with reboot of
PowerLogic P7 has no impact on people and equipment.
Failure to follow these instructions can result in death, serious injury, or
equipment damage.
PowerLogic Engineering Suite is an engineering tool for configuring
PowerLogic P7 devices, local operation and customized functions. The
Engineering Suite is supplied directly through the Schneider Electric website www.
se.com.
The Engineering Suite has a graphical interface where PowerLogic P7 settings
and parameters are grouped under different menu tabs. The contents of the menu
tabs depend on the device type and the selected application mode. Refer to the
user manual of PowerLogic Engineering Suite for detailed information on the
setting views of each menu tab.
The Engineering Suite stores the device configuration in a setting file.
When starting to work with the Engineering Suite, there are three options:

P7/EN M/02-004A

•

Create a new setting file without connecting to PowerLogic P7.

•

Open an existing (previously saved) setting file without connecting to
PowerLogic P7.

•

Connect to PowerLogic P7 and read the settings from the device.

157

Protection and control device

Operation

Depending on the ordered device option and actual requirements, the Engineering
Suite can be connected to a single device via the Mini-USB or DHMI port on the
device or to a group of devices via remote access over Ethernet connection.

Connection modes
PowerLogic Engineering Suite software can be used in three modes:
•

Offline

•

Connection using the Mini-USB or DHMI port

•

Network connection

Using offline PowerLogic Engineering Suite
This allows users to prepare configuration and setting parameter files for
PowerLogic P7 devices prior to commissioning.
When PowerLogic Engineering Suite is offline, you can create a configuration and
setting file from scratch or open a previously saved configuration or setting file as
a basis for further modification for a PowerLogic P7 of the same type. Refer to
PowerLogic Engineering Suite User manual for more information.

Using PowerLogic Engineering Suite with the Mini-USB or DHMI port
Depending on the ordered device option, the Mini-USB or DHMI port can be used
during commissioning of a PowerLogic P7:
•

To upload (write), download (read), and modify device configuration and
setting parameter files.

Refer to the User Manual of PowerLogic Engineering Suite for more information
on uploading (writing) / downloading (reading) configuration and setting parameter
files to/from the connected devices.

NOTICE
UNINTENDED EQUIPMENT OPERATION AND NUISANCE TRIPPING
After writing new firmware, configurations or settings to a protection and control
device, perform a test to verify that the protection and control device operates
correctly with the updated parameters.
Failure to follow these instructions can result in unwanted shutdown of
the electrical installation.
The PC fitted with PowerLogic Engineering Suite is connected to PowerLogic P7
using the Mini-USB port or DHMI port.

NOTICE
UNINTENDED EQUIPMENT OPERATION
Use the mini-USB port for connecting with a PC only. Do not use the mini-USB
port for other purpose.
Failure to follow these instructions can result in equipment damage.

Using PowerLogic Engineering Suite with a network connection
The network connection can be used during operation of a PowerLogic P7:
•

To manage the protection system.

•

To check the status of the electrical network.

•

To do a network fault analysis after a protection event.

The connection window allows configuration of the PowerLogic P7 network and
provides access to the configuration and setting parameters of the protection and
control application of the PowerLogic P7 units on the network.
The PC fitted with PowerLogic Engineering Suite can be connected to a group of
PowerLogic P7 units via Ethernet communication network. The Ethernet port used
on the PowerLogic P7 must allow access to web services. Any new configuration
sent to this port must also allow web services to ensure Engineering Suite can still
access the device.

Setting up the connection
Connecting to the device for the first time
Connecting to the device using the Mini-USB or DHMI port
1. Plug a corresponding cable into the Mini-USB or DHMI port.
2. Choose one of the following actions depending on where you are in
PowerLogic Engineering Suite:

P7/EN M/02-004A

Location

Action

Home page

Click Peer to peer connection to a device, select your device type,
and click Connect.

Project page

Click Add devices > Add connected devices. Then, in the pop-up
window, select your device type and choose USB Cable as the
connection method. Then click Connect.

159

Protection and control device

Operation

3. Enter a valid username and password.
NOTE: For the list of default usernames and passwords, see Default
password setting, page 146.
4. Click Authenticate.
5. Follow on-screen instructions to finish creating the working copy of the device
in PowerLogic Engineering Suite.
NOTE: The IP address of the Mini-USB or DHMI port is fixed to 90.0.0.1.

Connecting to the device via Ethernet
To connect to a device or multiple devices via Ethernet:
1. Log in PowerLogic Engineering Suite software.
2. On the home page, click Connect to devices.
3. From the pop-up Connect to devices window, select one of the following
connect types from the drop-down list.
•

Ethernet Network

•

Serial Connection

•

Modem Connection

4. Configure other options if required.
5. Select Multiple devices.
6. Click Next >.
After several minutes, Connection successful is displayed for each device
in the Progress column from Connect to multiple device window.
7. Click Configure to start configuring connected devices.
After several minutes, a site view window is displayed to show multiple
devices information.

Displaying live data from the device
To display device live data on PowerLogic Engineering Suite:
1. Click《 at the top right corner.
2. If the device is connected, go to Step 3.
If the device is not connected, click Connect to device and finish the device
connection process.
3. Click the play button.

Device information
PowerLogic Engineering Suite will provide the device information for the
connected device.
NOTE: You can use this function to verify that the correct device is connected.
•

Device nameplate
The device information, such as device name, product code, serial number,
can be viewed under General > System info.

•

HMI information
The HMI application version can be viewed under Device configuration >
HMI > Basic info.

•

Board information
The board information, such as board type, hardware version, FPGA version,
can be viewed under Device configuration > Slot # > Basic info.
